I am on the button (Small Blind) in a heads-up situation against Minni. K4o is a strong enough hand to open for a raise in a blind-vs-blind battle. Raising to 300 is a standard size that allows me to take control of the pot early and potentially win the blinds or see a flop with the initiative and position.

I’m in the big blind with Q♦T♠ (QhTs) facing a button open to 300. In heads-up this is a strong, versatile hand that fares well against a wide opening range. Calling 200 to see a flop leaves Flash with position and the initiative; I prefer to take the initiative and get value from worse hands while folding out some of his air. A small min‑3bet to 500 is allowed, but I want meaningful fold equity and to build a pot with a hand that plays well multi‑street. Current pot is 400; raising to 1000 means I’ll add 900 (opponent must call 700), creating ~2000 if called — a size that isolates and gives me control. Plan: if he folds I pick up the pot; if he calls I’ll play the flop aggressively but prudently OOP; if he 4‑bets large I can fold and preserve a deep stack. Raise to apply pressure and maximize expected value vs a wide button range.

I opened with a standard raise from the button (SB) with K4o, which is a reasonable steal attempt. However, Minni has responded with a 3-bet to 1000. K4o is a weak hand that does not play well against a 3-betting range, even with the advantage of being in position post-flop. Calling 700 more would put me in a difficult spot on most flops, and 4-betting as a bluff with such weak cards is unnecessarily risky given the current stack sizes and hand stage. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button I should open a wide range and use positional advantage. 5♠3♦ is marginal but perfectly acceptable to raise in heads-up to take the initiative and apply fold equity. I’ll make a small standard open — min-raise to 2x BB (total 200) — which keeps the pot manageable with a weak hand. Plan: fold to a sizable 3-bet, but if called use position to c-bet or probe based on the flop texture.

I am in the Big Blind with Kh 8d. Minni, who is currently the chip leader with a significant stack, has raised from the Small Blind to 200 (a min-raise). I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. Kh 8d is a strong enough hand to defend my blind against a wide button/small blind opening range. While I could consider a 3-bet, calling allows me to see a flop cheaply with a hand that has reasonable high-card value and play post-flop.

I am first to act on the flop after defending my Big Blind. The board is 9h-Tc-5c, which is quite coordinated and likely connects with many hands in the Small Blind's opening range. I currently have King-high with no direct draw or significant backdoor potential. Betting here into the pre-flop aggressor (Minni) would be a low-percentage play. Checking is the standard and most prudent action, allowing me to see how Minni reacts to the board texture before deciding whether to continue.

Heads-up I opened a very wide range preflop so flop c-bets should be frequent. I flop a pair of fives (5♠ vs board 9♥ T♣ 5♣) — a medium-strength hand with decent showdown value but vulnerable to overcards and tens/nines. Flash checked, so a half-pot continuation bet will accomplish three things: (1) get value from worse hands (backdoor pairs, 3x, small pocket pairs, and some draws), (2) fold out many unpaired overcards and weak floats, and (3) keep the pot manageable against stronger hands. I’ll bet 200 (≈½ pot). Plan: call small raises but release to a large raise or shove since my kicker is weak and board can improve opponents’ ranges on turn/river; if called I’ll re-evaluate on turn and proceed cautiously to control the pot.

I have King-high with no draws on a coordinated board (9h-Tc-5c). Minni's continuation bet is half the pot, and I am out of position with very little equity or potential to improve. While the price to call is relatively low, continuing here without a piece of the board or a strong draw is unlikely to be profitable over the long term, especially against an aggressive opponent. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am in the Small Blind with J8o. This is a standard hand to play from this position against a single opponent. Since it is just me and Minni (the Big Blind), I will raise to 300 to exert pressure and take control of the pot early. Minni has a large stack and has been active, but J8o is well within a standard button/SB opening range.

Facing a raise to 300 from the SB, I’m in the BB with J♥7♠. To call I need to put in 200 more (300 total) into a 400 pot, so my break-even equity is ~33%. Heads-up the opener’s range is wide, and J7o has reasonable showdown/play equity against a wide opening range and can hit top pair. A 3-bet to 500 as a bluff is risky out of position with a marginal hand and unnecessarily bloats the pot deep-stacked. I’ll call 300 total to see a flop, keep the pot manageable, and fold to strong aggression on dry boards or proceed cautiously on flops where I pick up equity or a pair.

I have J♥7♠ on T♠ Q♠ Q♣ — I completely missed. This is a paired, two-spade board and I have only a remote runner-runner spade plan and very little showdown/value equity. Flash was the preflop raiser (wide/aggressive) and checking here likely means either weakness or pot control; bluffing now has poor fold equity and risks getting raised off a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, fold to significant flop aggression, but consider calling a small probe bet for pot odds and re-evaluate on the turn (only turn J or favorable runouts change my plan).

On this flop of Ts-Qs-Qc, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a Nine) and some overcard potential with the Jack. The board is paired, which reduces the likelihood that Minni has a strong hand unless she has a Queen or a Ten. Since I was the pre-flop aggressor and Minni checked to me, a continuation bet is appropriate to take down the pot immediately or to build equity. A bet of 300 (half pot) is a standard sizing to apply pressure on her non-pairing hands like low cards or small pocket pairs.

Board T♠ Q♠ Q♣, I have J♥7♠. I checked to control the pot OOP and Flash (preflop aggressor, plays wide) c-bet a relatively small 300 into the 900 pot — I must call 300. Calling gives me ~25% break-even equity (300 to win ~1,200). My hand has very little raw equity (no pair, roughly 6 immediate outs — 3 jacks, 3 sevens — and only a remote runner‑runner spade plan), so I’m usually behind Qx/Tx/value hands. However Flash’s range is wide and the bet size is small, so folding immediately would be overly tight; raising OOP as a bluff with J7 is poor — no real fold equity and it bloats a pot I don’t want to play big out of position. The best line is to call 300, float the turn and re-evaluate: fold to substantial turn aggression unless I improve (pair a J or get a very favorable runout) or he shows clear weakness (checks) and I can take the pot later. If I pair the J I’ll start extracting value; otherwise keep pot control and fold to big pressure.

The turn (T♥) pairs the tens and gives a double‑paired board (Q Q T T). I have J♥7♠ — no pair and essentially zero backdoor equity now (the spade runner‑runner is dead). My only realistic outs are jacks or sevens, but even if I hit a jack/7 the river two‑pair kicker situation is marginal and many hands (any Q or T) already beat me with full houses. Flash is the preflop aggressor and bet the flop — bluffing here has poor fold equity and risks a check‑raise from a full house. With the pot already sizable (1,200) and deep stacks behind, I want to keep the pot under control and avoid getting committed with a bluff that has little chance. I’ll check and plan to fold to meaningful river aggression unless I actually pair or see a very favorable runout and a passive line from him.

The board has double-paired with Queens and Tens (Ts-Qs-Qc-Th). My hand, J8o, is currently just Jack-high, which loses to almost any pair or even Ace-high (A-Q-Q-T-T). While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 9), a straight would still be vulnerable to any player holding a Queen or a Ten for a full house. Minni called my flop bet, which suggests she has some connection to the board or a draw. By checking the turn, I control the size of the pot and take a free card to see if I can improve or if the river provides a better opportunity to bluff or get to a cheap showdown.

Board is T♠ Q♠ Q♣ T♥ 9♥ and I hold J♥7♠ — I have no pair and essentially zero showdown equity vs any Qx or Tx (the board is already Q Q T T so many hands are full houses). Both of us checked the turn and the river 9♥ didn’t change my hand. Betting as a bluff here has very thin fold equity against a wide but calling SB opener (they will call small bluffs with Tx/Qx and worse) and invites a raise I should fold to, needlessly risking chips with no chance to improve. Pot control and stack preservation are optimal: check and take showdown; fold to any river aggression. Action: check.

On the river, the board is Ts-Qs-Qc-Th-9h. My hand (Js-8d) has completed a straight (8-9-10-J-Q). However, the board is double-paired with Queens and Tens, meaning any opponent holding a Queen or a Ten has a full house. Furthermore, a player with King-Jack would hold a higher straight (9-10-J-Q-K). Minni has checked both the turn and the river. While this could indicate weakness, betting for value here is thin because most hands that call me will likely be full houses or higher straights, whereas hands I beat (like Ace-high or small pocket pairs) will likely fold. Checking back is the safest way to realize my showdown value and win the pot without risking a check-raise.

Heads‑up on the button (I’m SB). J♥3♠ is marginal, but in a 2‑player game the button should be opening very wide to seize initiative and use post‑flop position. A standard 3x BB open (to 300) matches table tendencies and gives good fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. Plan: if Flash folds I scoop the blinds; if he calls I’ll play postflop aggressively with position (c‑bet on favorable boards, evaluate ranges on resistance); if he 3‑bets large I’ll fold this weak holding.

I am in the Big Blind with Ten-Eight of spades, a strong suited hand with good playability. Minni opened to 300 from the Small Blind/Button. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 into a 400 pot), and T8s is well within the defending range against a standard button open. I will call to see the flop.

On a flop of Ad-5h-5d, I have Ten-high with no significant draws. This board heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range, which includes many Aces. I will check to see how Minni reacts, with the intention of folding to most bets given the lack of equity in my hand.

I was the preflop opener and have position; opponent checked the A♦5♥5♦ flop. My J♥3♠ is essentially air, but as the aggressor in heads‑up play I should c‑bet to take advantage of fold equity and deny free cards. Flash’s calling range from the BB includes lots of Ax and pocket pairs that beat me, but also many missed broadways and weak hands that can fold. I’ll make a half‑pot continuation bet (300): big enough to credibly represent an Ace or a 5 and fold out a chunk of villain’s weaker holdings, but small enough not to overcommit if called. Plan after this bet: fold to a substantial check‑raise (I have very little showdown equity), and if called I’ll re‑evaluate the turn — play cautiously on A/5/very coordinating cards and consider barreling blanks if villain shows weakness.

The flop is Ad-5h-5d. I am holding Ten-high (Ts 8s) with no real equity or draws on this board. The board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor (Minni), as their range contains many more Aces than mine. Minni has continued for 300 into a 600 pot. Continuing here would be a mistake with nothing but Ten-high; even if I were to represent a 5, it is a low-frequency play that is unnecessary in this spot. I will fold.

I am on the button with Q8o, which is a strong enough hand to open in a heads-up or short-handed situation. Minni has been playing aggressively, so I want to take the initiative and see how they respond pre-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is a solid opening move.

Call to 300. T9♣ is a very playable, suited connector heads-up — great equity and implied odds vs a BTN open. The pot is 400 and I only have to add 200 (≈33% pot odds), which is cheap for a hand that can make straights, flushes and top pairs. I'm out of position, so I don't want to overcommit with a marginal 3‑bet; calling keeps my range wide and lets me realize equity on favorable flops. Plan: on connected/club/paired flops I will continue (lead or raise as appropriate); if I completely miss and face a large c‑bet I'll fold. If Flash 4‑bets large pre, I will fold this speculative holding.

Flop is 7c 4c Jd and I hold Tc9c: a very strong semi-bluff. I have a four-card club flush draw (9 club outs) plus a gutshot to an 8 (4 eights, one overlaps), about 12 unique outs (~45% to improve to the river). I’m out of position, so I want to seize the initiative, deny free cards, and get fold equity against Flash’s wide BTN/SB raising range. A sizing of ~2/3 pot (400 into the 600 pot) balances fold equity (break-even fold freq ≈40%) with value when called — it folds out some overcards and weak holdings while getting called by worse pairs and draws that I can outdraw. If he folds I scoop the pot; if he calls I still have ~45% to hit and can proceed based on the turn (continue when I pick up equity or proceed cautiously to a big raise). If he raises big I will re-evaluate (likely fold to very large 3-bets OOP). Therefore I will lead here with a pot-pressure semi-bluff of 400.

On the flop of 7c 4c Jd, Minni has led out with a bet of 400 into a 600 pot. My hand, Q8o (with the 8c), is currently just Queen-high. While I have a backdoor flush draw and two overcards to the middle cards, Minni's lead often indicates a pair of Jacks, a middle pair, or a strong draw. Given that I have no real equity and calling to float would be expensive against an aggressive opponent, the most disciplined play is to fold and wait for a better spot.

I’m on the button (small blind) with J♣3♠ — not a premium holding, but in heads‑up play the button should open a wide range. Raising takes the initiative, preserves fold equity, and gives me position postflop if called. I’ll make a small, standard heads‑up open to 200 total (I’ve already posted 50, so I’m adding 150) to keep the pot controllable while forcing Flash to make a decision. If he 3‑bets big I can release this marginal hand; if he calls I can use position to navigate the flop and realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind holding Q7o. Minni, on the Button/Small Blind, has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more to see the flop in a pot that will be 400. This gives me 3:1 pot odds, requiring about 25% equity to make a profitable call. Given the wide range Minni likely has from the small blind and the favorable odds, Q7o is strong enough to defend. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

I am in the Big Blind and the board has come Ace-high (Ad 5c 2d). I have Queen-high (Qc 7h) with no real draw or pair. On an Ace-high board, the pre-flop raiser (Minni) has a significant range advantage. I will check to the aggressor and most likely fold if she bets, as I have very little equity to continue.

Heads‑up on the button with Jc3s. I opened preflop and Flash (BB) just checked the A♣?No — board is 5♣ 2♦ A♦; I completely missed (no pair, no diamond). In heads‑up play the preflop raiser should c‑bet frequently to take advantage of the wide checking range from the BB. A small continuation bet preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Pot is 400, so a ~1/3–40% pot stab (150) is the right size: opponent only needs to fold ≈27% for this bluff to be +EV. If he folds I win the pot outright; if called I can proceed cautiously — check turn with no improvement and give up to big aggression, but continue/barrel if I pick up a 3 or (especially) a 4 that completes the wheel. If he check‑raises the flop I’ll let this marginal hand go. Betting 150 maximizes immediate fold equity and keeps options open with position.

On this Ace-high board (Ad 5c 2d), my Queen-high has no significant showdown value and very little equity to improve. Minni, as the pre-flop raiser, has a range advantage on this board texture. Although the bet size of 150 into 400 offers decent pot odds, continuing without a pair or a draw is unprofitable. I will fold.

I am holding Ten-Five offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation against Minni, who currently holds a significant chip lead. T5o is a very weak starting hand, even in heads-up play, and does not justify a raise or a call against an aggressive opponent like Minni. Folding is the most disciplined play to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with Td Js — a strong, playable hand in position. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to call, but I want to take initiative and deny the BB good odds. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB): this build pot, creates fold equity (Flash must call 200 to chase ~400), and gives me position to c‑bet/realize equity on most flops. If Flash 3‑bets large I’ll re-evaluate (often folding or flatting depending on sizing); if he calls I plan to use position to apply pressure or extract value on favorable textures.

I have been dealt pocket Tens (Tc, Ts) in the Big Blind, which is a premium starting hand. Minni raised to 300 from the Button. To maximize value and protect my hand, I will put in a 3-bet. A standard re-raise size here is around 3x to 4x the original raise. I will raise to 1000 total.

Heads‑up on the button with Td Js (off‑suit). Flash 3‑bet to 1,000 after my raise to 300 — a sizable but not huge 3‑bet heads‑up. I’ll flat-call. TJs plays very well in position against a wideish 3‑bet range: it has acceptable equity and lots of post‑flop playability, and I can use position to realize equity or apply pressure. Calling keeps the pot manageable and avoids bloating the pot with a marginal hand by 4‑betting; folding surrenders too many profitable spots. I need 700 more (1000 total) to call; plan is to evaluate the flop and use positional advantage to c‑bet/float or fold to heavy action if the texture and betting indicate I’m behind.

On a dry, low board of 3h-6c-4d, my pocket Tens are currently an overpair. Having been the pre-flop 3-better, I have a range advantage. I will place a continuation bet of approximately 40% of the pot to extract value from smaller pairs (like 77, 88, 99) or overcards (like AK, AQ) and to protect my hand against potential straight draws or overcards appearing on the turn.

Flop is very dry (3h 6c 4d) and I have two overcards (Td Js) with no real draws. Flash is a 3‑bettor preflop, but his flop sizing (800 into this pot) is small — this looks like a standard c‑bet to buy the pot cheaply rather than an all‑in value line. Calling is the best option: it costs 800 to win ~3600 (≈22% break‑even equity) and my hand has about ~24%+ chance to pair by river plus additional showdown equity vs some of his bluffs/A‑high hands. Raising to 1600 would be a thin semi‑bluff with limited fold equity against a 3‑bettor and can commit me to a pot I’m behind in; folding surrenders too much given the cheap price and position. Plan: call, use position to evaluate the turn — barreling or check‑raising only if I improve or get a very favorable action/texture; fold to heavy turn aggression if I miss and he shows strength.

The 7s on the turn is a coordinated card that completes a straight for any hand containing a five (like 54s, 65s, or A5s), which are all within Minni's calling range. While my pocket Tens are still an overpair, the board texture has become significantly more dangerous. I will check this turn to control the pot size and see how Minni responds. This allows me to see a river more safely or potentially catch a bluff if she interprets my check as weakness, while also not bloating the pot against a potential straight or set.

Turn summary: I’m heads‑up on the button with Td Js on 3h‑6c‑4d‑7s. Flash was the preflop 3‑better, c‑bet 800 on the flop (I called) and now checked the turn. The board is very dry and I have two overcards with only one card to come (≈6 outs to pair → ~13% to hit the river).

Plan and reasoning: checking is safe but gives up an opportunity to take the pot now — Flash’s line (small flop c‑bet then check turn) looks weak and his preflop 3‑bet range is wide enough that a modest turn stab should get folds often. I want to apply pressure while keeping the sizing manageable in case I get called. Betting ~1/3 pot is the best balance: pot = 3600, bet 1200. If he folds I scoop 3600; the bluff break‑even fold frequency if I had no showdown equity would be 1200/(3600+1200)=25%, which is very achievable here. I do have ~13% to pair the river as backup. If he calls I can still realize my equity and re‑evaluate the river; if he check‑raises large I will strongly favor folding (too little equity to continue vs big aggression).

Action: bet 1200 to take the initiative, leverage fold equity, and keep the pot controlled if called.

Minni has bet 1200 into a pot of 3600 (1/3 pot). This small sizing could represent a variety of hands, from thin value to a bluff or a draw. While the 7s is a scare card that completes straights for any 5 in her range (like 56s or A5s), my pocket Tens remain an overpair. Given the pot odds (calling 1200 to win 4800), folding is not an option. Raising would likely only isolate me against better hands. I will call to keep her bluffs in and see how the river develops.

The board has become extremely coordinated (3-4-6-7-8), and my pocket Tens, while previously an overpair, have been significantly devalued. Any 5 in Minni's range now makes a straight (3-4-5-6-7 or 4-5-6-7-8). Additionally, hands like 9-10 (which I partially block) or 9-5 would also make a straight. Many of Minni's calling hands on the flop and turn, such as 67s, 78s, 55, or A5s, have now overtaken me. Since I am out of position and my hand is now essentially a bluff-catcher or marginal showdown hand, I will check to see how Minni acts. I am unlikely to get value from worse hands by betting, and checking allows me to control the pot or potentially fold to a large, polarising bet.

I have T♦ J♠ on 3h‑6c‑4d‑7s‑8d — I missed completely (no pair, no straight/flush). Pot is 6000 and Flash (preflop 3‑better) checked the river after calling my turn stab. His line (3‑bet preflop, small flop c‑bet, check turn, call turn bet) looks like medium‑strength made hands or cautious A/K‑type holdings that have enough showdown value to call a turn bet. To make a river bluff I need a fold frequency of bet/(pot+bet) (e.g. a 2000 bet needs ~25% folds). Given he called the turn, I don’t expect him to fold that often — he’ll call light with any pair or A‑high. Betting here is likely negative EV; checking back controls the pot, preserves my stack, and avoids getting called by better. I’ll check and take the showdown rather than firing a thin bluff.

I am on the button with 9-5 suited. In a heads-up scenario, this is a reasonable hand to open for a raise to maintain aggression and leverage my positional advantage. I will raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind and take control of the pot early.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a button open to 300. 7♠4♠ is a marginal hand but suited and has reasonable equity versus a wide opening range — plus deep stacks give good implied odds. Calling 200 more (300 total committed preflop) keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize equity and see a flop with backdoor flush/straight potential. A minimum 3-bet here would be a thin bluff and unnecessary given opponent’s frequency of opens; I’ll call and evaluate on the flop, folding to heavy aggression if I miss and have no redraws, or applying pressure when I pick up a draw or pair.

Flop: T♠ T♥ 6♦. I hold 7♠4♠ — no pair and only a backdoor running spade possibility (need both turn and river spades; ~4% to complete). Opponent opened preflop so their range contains many tens, overpairs and broadways that interact well with this board; I have virtually no showdown equity and no clean semi-bluff draw. With deep stacks and a dry, paired board, I should avoid building the pot out of position with air. I’ll check to control the pot size, see my opponent’s action, and only continue to very small bets (or if I improve on the turn); fold to a meaningful c-bet.

On a paired board like Ts-Th-6d, the pre-flop aggressor typically has a range advantage. While my 9d-5d hasn't connected, this is a prime board for a continuation bet to fold out overcards and weaker hands in the big blind's range that missed. I will place a small bet of 200 (1/3 pot) to maintain pressure and try to take the pot down immediately.

Heads-up on the big blind with 7♠4♠ on T♠ T♥ 6♦: I have no pair and only a runner‑runner spade draw (~4% to complete), so raw showdown equity is low. Flash opened wide from the button and made a very small c‑bet (200 into a 800 pot) — that gives me ~20% pot odds to call. Raising would be a thin, high-variance bluff with little equity; folding to such a tiny bet surrenders too much value against a wide betting frequency. With deep stacks and a cheap price to see the turn I’ll call the 200 to realize my backdoor equity and keep options open. If the turn misses (no meaningful improvement) and Flash shows pressure with a sizable bet, I’ll fold; if I pick up a spade or pair (or get a free card on a harmless turn) I can continue or apply pressure.

On the turn I have 7♠4♠ on T♠ T♥ 6♦ 9♥ — no pair and no live draws. The only potential backdoor flush required both turn and river spades, which is now impossible, so my showdown equity is essentially zero versus a button opener’s range (tens, overpairs, broadways). Opponent has shown a wide opening range and a small flop stab, but with deep stacks there’s no reason to build the pot or bluff-raise aggressively with pure air. I’ll check to see the river for a free card and avoid committing chips; plan is to fold to any meaningful bet on this street and only consider calling a very tiny probe on the river if pot odds and player read justify it.

The turn 9h has improved my hand to two pair (Tens and Nines). While this is a significant improvement, the board is still dangerous with various straight draws (QJ, 87) and the possibility that Minni is slow-playing a Ten or holding a Nine with a better kicker. By checking back here, I am exercising pot control with a medium-strength hand that has showdown value. This also allows me to evaluate the river and potentially catch bluffs from missed draws if Minni decides to lead out on the final street.

Board: T♠ T♥ 6♦ 9♥ J♦. I hold 7♠4♠ — no pair and my only backdoor spade draw was killed on the turn, so I have essentially zero showdown equity versus a button opener’s range (tens, jacks, pairs, broadways). Flash’s line (wide preflop open, tiny 200 flop stab, check on the turn) shows some weakness but also contains many hands that will call a river bluff. Betting here would be a thin, high-variance bluff with low fold equity; the +EV line is to control the pot and avoid throwing good chips away with pure air. I will check and fold to any meaningful river bet (only consider calling a very small probe if the size and read justify it).

I have Two Pair (Tens and Nines) on a board that is quite connected (Ts-Th-6d-9h-Jd). While my hand has decent showdown value, the river Jack completes several possible straight draws (like 8-7 or Q-8) and any Jack in Minni's range now beats my pair of Nines. Minni has checked to me twice after calling the flop. By checking back, I ensure I get to see the showdown without risking a check-raise or being called only by hands that have me beaten. My hand is a classic medium-strength hand that is best played for pot control here.

Heads-up on the button with TdKh — a strong, playable hand in position. I can just complete to 100, but I want initiative and fold equity against Flash (who shows preflop aggression at times). With deep stacks I prefer to raise and play postflop in position. I’ll make a standard heads-up raise to 200 (total) to pressure the big blind, deny cheap flops, and keep a wide, aggressive opening range. If he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate (likely fold to a polarizing shove/large 3-bet, call or 4-bet depending on sizing and reads); if he calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and extract value.

I am in the Big Blind with 9s 4c. Minni has raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, 94o is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly post-flop, especially out of position against an active opponent like Minni. Given that we are still in the early-middle stages of the tournament (Hand 54/150) and I have a healthy stack, there is no need to defend with such a marginal hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation with Jack-Trey suited. This is a standard hand to open in a wide-range button strategy. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and leverage my position for post-flop play.

Facing a button open to 300 from the SB with 8♣4♠ in the big blind: this is a very weak, unsuited, disconnected hand and I’m out of position postflop. To call 200 into a 400 pot I need >33% equity; vs a reasonable heads-up opening range 8-4o is usually well below that and is frequently dominated. A min 3‑bet to 500 would be a thin bluff (poor blockers) and could lead to awkward multi-street spots with deep stacks and positional disadvantage. I’ll fold and wait for a better spot to pick up chips.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with As2o — this is a strong, playable hand in position. I’ve already posted 50; to call would be passive and miss the initiative. I want fold equity and to seize position, so I’ll open-raise. A standard 2.5–3x BB sizing is appropriate with deep stacks to pressure the BB while keeping postflop play manageable. I’ll raise to 300 total (3 BB). That commits +250 more chips (total committed = 300), gives me a good range advantage and blockers to opposing Aces, and lets me lead the hand or fold/defend comfortably if Flash shows aggression.

I am in the Big Blind with 9-4 offsuit, which is a very weak starting hand. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the button. While the pot odds are 3:1 to call, defending such a weak hand out of position against an aggressive stack leader like Minni is generally unprofitable. I'll fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I have King-Four suited on the button. In a heads-up situation, this is a strong starting hand that warrants a raise to take control of the pot and apply pressure to the Big Blind. I will stick with the standard opening raise size used throughout this match.

Facing a standard SB open to 300 heads-up, 9♦7♣ is marginal but playable — especially given the cheap price to see a flop. I only need to add 200 (total commit 300), which gives good pot odds vs a wide opening range from the button. I’m out of position postflop, so I don’t want to 3‑bet light and bloat the pot with a speculative offsuit hand; calling preserves stack depth and lets me see a flop and evaluate further. I will call 300 (total).

Board is Kc Kh Ks — trips on board. I have 9♦7♣ with no pair and no backdoor draws; I’m behind any pocket pair (which becomes a full house) or any king (quads). Flash opened preflop so his range is weighted to hands that now beat me. Betting out-of-position as a bluff is thin and likely to get called by better hands; pot control is optimal. I will check and concede to any sizable bet, only considering a call to a very small probe bet for price.

I have the absolute nuts with Four of a Kind (Kings). It is impossible for my opponent to have a better hand. On this board (Kc Kh Ks), it is very difficult for Minni to have any piece of it. If I bet now, I likely force a fold from almost her entire range. By checking back, I allow her the opportunity to either bluff on the turn or catch a card that gives her a marginal hand she might be willing to pay off with. Slow-playing is the optimal way to extract value here.

Board: Kc Kh Ks 6s. I hold 9♦7♣ — essentially air with no pair and no meaningful draw. The preflop raiser’s range contains many hands that now beat me (any K = quads, any pocket pair = full house), and although both checked the flop that could be slowplay or simple passivity. There’s no equity to continue and no reason to inflate the pot out of position. I’ll check to control the pot and preserve my stack, and plan to fold to any turn bet (only consider a river bluff if Villain gives a clear second-street weakness).

I have Quad Kings, the absolute nuts. The board is incredibly dry (Kc Kh Ks 6s). Minni has checked twice. If I bet now, I am extremely likely to force a fold because it is so difficult for her to have connected with this board in any way. By checking back the turn as well, I maintain a very weak appearance, potentially inducing a bluff from her on the river or allowing her to catch a marginal hand (like an Ace for a full house) that might pay off a value bet.

River: board Kc Kh Ks 6s Qs, my hand 9♦7♣ — no pair, no draw. I controlled the pot by checking flop and turn; Flash (preflop opener) checked both streets which could be weakness, but this board heavily favors any pocket pair (full house) or the remaining King (quads). The combined chance Villain has a full house/quads is meaningful and I have essentially zero showdown value. Betting as a bluff would need a very high fold frequency to be +EV and Flash’s wide opening/flatting tendencies make him liable to call down light. With a 600-chip pot and deep stacks, I’m not risking chips on a thin bluff. I’ll check to go to showdown and, if Flash now bets, fold — no reason to bloat the pot with air.

I have the absolute nuts with quad Kings. The board is incredibly dry, and Minni has checked all three streets, suggesting she likely has very little. However, with the Queen on the river, she might have improved to a full house or might be willing to call a value bet with an Ace-high or a pocket pair, thinking I'm attempting to steal the pot on a board where the board itself is often the best hand. I will place a 2/3 pot-sized bet to extract some value.

Board: Kc Kh Ks 6s Qs. I hold 9♦7♣ — no pair, no backdoor draw, essentially zero showdown value versus reasonable lines. Pot was 1000; Flash bets 400. Calling 400 to win 1400 requires ~28.6% equity. Flash was the preflop raiser and checked flop and turn (possible slowplay) and his opening range contains many hands that now beat me outright (any pocket pair → full house, any K → quads). A small river bet could be a blocking or thin-value bet, but I don’t put him on bluffs often enough to justify the call, and raising as a bluff here is unnecessary and spewy with deep stacks. I controlled the pot earlier and should not bloat it with air — fold and preserve my stack.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with Q♥8♥ — a suited, playable hand with good postflop potential and lots of fold equity. Versus one opponent and with deep stacks, the standard play is to open-raise to seize initiative, build the pot, and pressure the BB. Min raise to 200 (2x BB) is small relative to our stacks and keeps many hands in his calling range while giving us positional advantage postflop. If he calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and control pot size on wet boards; if he 3-bets large I’ll re-evaluate and likely fold. Raise to 200 maximizes expected value here.

In heads-up play, defending the big blind against a min-raise is standard when receiving 3:1 pot odds, even with a marginal hand like 9-6 offsuit. I will call to see the flop and re-evaluate based on the board texture.

The flop is Ace-high and I have missed it completely with 9-high. Standard procedure here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see their reaction. I have very little equity on this board and no reason to lead out into a range that contains many Aces.

Heads-up as the preflop aggressor with Q♥8♥ on A♠3♠5♥. The flop is Ace-high which hits a lot of the BB’s calling range, but Flash checked — giving us a chance to take the pot down. I have no pair but two hearts (a backdoor flush) and decent blockers to some A-x/A-Q combos. Standard heads-up strategy is to continuation-bet a wide range here. I want fold equity while keeping the pot manageable, so I’ll make a half-pot c-bet (~50%). If he folds we pick up the pot; if he calls I can continue with equity/position on the turn or check behind to control size; if he raises large I’ll respect it and fold. Bet 200 keeps pressure without overcommitting with a marginal hand.

I have 9-high on an Ace-high board. I missed the flop entirely. Minni, who raised pre-flop, has followed up with a continuation bet. I have no pair, no draw, and virtually zero equity against their range, which frequently includes an Ace or better. Continuing would be a significant mistake.

In the Small Blind (SB) with T8o, I have a hand that is slightly above the bottom of my opening range in heads-up play. Against Minni, who has been quite active, I want to maintain aggression or see a flop cheaply. Calling the additional 50 chips is very attractive given the pot odds, but raising to 300 has been my standard opening size and allows me to take control of the hand pre-flop. However, given the current stack sizes and Minni's tendency to defend, I will simply raise to 300 to put pressure on the big blind.

Heads-up against a button open to 300. Pot is 400 and I need to add 200 (total 300) to call — ~3:1 pot odds. Qc3d is a marginal hand but heads-up the opener’s range is wide, and calling to see a flop is the correct, lower-variance play. I’m out of position so I don’t want to 3-bet light with Q3o; folding would be too nitty given the price. I’ll call and evaluate the flop rather than risking more chips preflop.

Heads-up OOP with Qc3d on Ah 2s Td. I have no pair and essentially no meaningful single-card draw (only remote runner-runner outs), so betting OOP is leaking value and bloating the pot with a hand that has poor equity. The pot is 600 and Flash opened wide preflop, so he will c-bet often — I should check and force him to prove strength. Plan: check to control the pot; if he bets a small amount I can call to see a cheap turn, but I will fold to a sizable continuation bet since I have very little showdown value. Preserving my 23k stack for better spots is the priority.

I have flopped middle pair (Tens) on an Ace-high dry board. While betting for value/protection is an option, middle pair is a classic hand to check back for pot control. Checking allows me to see how Minni acts on the turn and keeps the pot size manageable, as I am likely either way ahead of her air or way behind an Ace. It also helps protect my checking range.

Out of position with Qc3d on Ah‑2s‑Td‑Kc. I checked the flop as planned and Flash checked back turn — I have no pair and essentially no realizable draw, so betting the turn would bloat the pot and have poor fold equity against a preflop raiser’s range (Ax, Kx, Tx, pairs). With deep stacks and a wide but often calling range, preserving chips is correct. I’ll check through to the river and re-evaluate; I’ll only bluff the river if it’s a clean, late card and villain shows weakness, otherwise fold to meaningful river pressure.

The King of Clubs on the turn is an overcard to my middle pair of Tens. Since Minni checked for the second time, it's unlikely she has a strong Ace or a King, but she could be slow-playing or simply have a hand with some showdown value like a weak Ten or a Deuce. Betting doesn't accomplish much here; better hands like an Ace or King aren't folding, and weaker hands might not call. I will check back to control the size of the pot and try to reach showdown with my pair of Tens.

Both players checked flop and turn — Flash showed weakness and the river 4♦ is a harmless, “clean” card. My Q♣3♦ has essentially no showdown value (no pair, no real draw), so checking to showdown is unlikely to earn chips. This is a good spot to bluff: a half‑pot-sized bet represents a made hand (Kx/Ax or a turned two‑pair) and pressures marginal holdings. Pot is 600; a 300 bet gives Flash ~3:1 odds to call, so I only need him to fold ~33%+ of the time for this to be +EV. I’ll bet 300 to try to take it down; if he raises I’ll fold to avoid bloating the pot with air.

On a board of A-2-T-K-4, I hold a pair of Tens. I checked the flop and turn for pot control and to reach showdown cheaply. Minni's lead for 300 on the river after checking twice feels like a 'thin' value bet or a bluff designed to take down a pot that I haven't shown interest in. Given that I need to be right only 25% of the time (300 to win 900), and my pair of Tens is a strong bluff-catcher in this specific line, I will call to see her hand.

Heads-up on the button with Kh Jc — a strong, positionally advantaged hand. The pot is only 150 and calling 50 is passive; I want initiative and to narrow Flash’s defending range. With deep stacks (~230bb) I can play multi-street poker in position, so I’ll raise to build the pot and gain fold equity. I choose 300 total (≈3x BB) — bigger than the minimum to discourage some light defenses but small enough to keep postflop maneuverability. Plan: C-bet favorable flops, proceed cautiously vs heavy 3-bets (fold to large 3-bets, call small ones and play postflop in position).

I am in the Big Blind with Jack-Trey offsuit. Minni has raised to 300 from the Small Blind. Jack-Trey offsuit is a very weak hand to defend against a 3x open, especially out of position. Given the stack depths and the need to preserve my stack for better spots, I will fold this hand.

I have been dealt a pocket pair of fours (4s 4h) on the button in a heads-up situation. Pocket pairs are quite strong in this format, and I definitely want to play this hand. A standard raise is appropriate here to build the pot and take control of the hand. I will raise to 300 total chips.

Flash opened to 300 from the button. I’m in the BB with 6s9c — a marginal but playable heads‑up hand. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total committed 300), which gives ~3:1 immediate pot odds. The raise size is small relative to stacks and the button’s opening range is likely wide, so 6s9c has reasonable equity and flop-playability. I don’t want to 3-bet with this hand (poor blockers, out of position postflop) and folding would be too nitty vs a frequent button opener. Plan: call to see a flop, realize equity and proceed cautiously out of position — continue with pairs/draws, fold to large aggression on dry misses.

I hold 9♣6♠ on 8♥7♥5♠ — that’s a made 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9). Heads‑up the opener’s range is wide, so most of his single pairs, two‑pair draws and heart draws are behind me or will pay to see another card. I’m out of position so I want to extract value and charge draws while keeping worse hands and floats in. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep enough to get value, so I’ll bet ~1/2 pot to maximize calls from pairs and draws without bloating the pot unnecessarily. If he raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (I’m very often ahead, so I’ll usually call reasonable raises and proceed to river), but for now bet for value.

On a highly coordinated flop of 8h-7h-5s, my pocket fours represent a small pair and an inside straight draw (any 6 gives me an 8-high straight). Minni has led into me with a 'donk bet' of 300. This board texture heavily favors the big blind's defending range, which contains many connectors and suited hands. However, I am getting 3-to-1 pot odds to call, and I have position for the remainder of the hand. While my hand is vulnerable, it has enough equity and potential to justify seeing a turn card before deciding whether to commit further.

I have a 9-high straight (5-6-7-8-9) with 6♠9♣. The turn Q♥ completes a three-heart board, which means any opponent who started with two hearts now has a flush and beats me — but those combos are relatively rare. Flash called my half-pot flop bet, so his continuing range is wide and contains many one-pair hands and weaker holdings that will pay a value bet. I want to extract value while keeping the pot manageable in case he has a made flush and raises. Bet ~½ pot (600): it gets called by worse pairs and floats, charges single-card heart/backdoor hands, and lets me fold to a large raise that will usually represent a flush.

The turn card, the Queen of Hearts, is quite problematic for my hand. It completes a possible flush (as there are now three hearts on the board) and hits a range of hands that Minni could be 'donk-betting' with. My pair of fours is now effectively a bluff-catcher at best, and while I do have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 6), the 6 of hearts would be a dangerous card to hit as it would also complete the flush. Given Minni's continued aggression and the coordinated nature of the board, I do not have sufficient equity to justify a call. Folding is the safest and most mathematically sound decision here.

Heads-up on the button with pocket deuces and deep stacks — this is a hand I want to play for value and with some fold equity. Calling 50 is cheap for set-mining, but an open-raise gives initiative, can isolate the big blind, and often wins the blinds immediately. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) — big enough to apply pressure but small enough not to bloat the pot unnecessarily. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll reassess (often folding to very large 3-bets, calling reasonable 3-bets to set-mine); if called I’ll use positional advantage to play postflop.

In a heads-up scenario, JTo is a strong hand that is well ahead of a typical button opening range. Minni's raise to 300 is standard. While I could 3-bet for value or as a semi-bluff, calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has good playability and retains a lot of equity against her wide range without overinflating the pot out of position.

In this heads-up situation, I am out of position after calling a pre-flop raise. The flop of Kc-7d-3d is relatively dry, although it does offer a diamond draw. My hand, Jack-high (JTo), has completely missed this board and has very little equity. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how they react. Leading out (donk betting) without a piece of the board or a significant draw is generally unwise, so I will check and likely fold to a standard continuation bet unless the turn provides some improvement.

Flop Kc 7d 3d — I hold pocket deuces (2d2c). Heads-up my preflop raise gives me initiative and Flash’s flop check frequently represents a missed broadway/overcard or a weak pair/draw. This board is K‑high with two diamonds; my hand is currently an underpair but I have tiny backdoor diamond potential. Standard heads‑up play is to c‑bet for fold equity and to protect my pair — checking just gives free cards and lets villain realize equity. I’ll make a half‑pot c‑bet (300): large enough to fold lots of unpaired hands and deny free turns, but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (check back most blank turns, fold to heavy aggression unless I improve to a set or a very favorable runout). If he check‑raises big, I’ll fold most of the time.

On a board of Kc-7d-3d, my hand (Jack-high) has missed entirely. Minni's continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot is a standard move on this dry, King-high texture. Given that I have no draws, no pair, and am out of position, there is no reason to continue. Floating or raising here would be low-equity plays against a range that contains many Kings and pocket pairs. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 7c 3c. This is a speculative suited hand. In a heads-up situation, I'll typically play a wide range from the button, but 7-3 suited is near the bottom of the playable range. However, at 50/100 blinds, completing the small blind for an additional 50 chips is a low-cost way to see a flop. I will call to see if I can connect with the board or find a bluffing opportunity later.

Heads-up in the big blind with A♠9♠ — a strong, profitable holding against a small‑blind complete. Flash only called 50, so raising isolates and extracts value from worse Aces, Kx and broadway holdings while generating fold equity. I’ll make a standard, modest preflop raise to 300 total (consistent with table sizing) which builds the pot but leaves room to fold to a big 3‑bet or play postflop for value if called. If called I plan to c‑bet many flops and use my nut‑ace + spade backdoor equity to apply pressure.

I limped from the button with 7-3 suited, a speculative hand. Minni raised to 300 total. While I have position post-flop and the cost to call is only 200 more into a 400-chip pot (giving me 3:1 odds), 7-3 suited is simply too weak to profitably defend against a raise from a solid player like Minni. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to avoid unnecessary chip loss with a dominated hand.

3♣5♠ (3h5s) is a very weak, offsuit hand, but we're heads-up on the button (post-flop position) and it only costs 50 to see a flop into a 150 pot. That's excellent pot odds (~3:1, break-even ~25% equity). Raising with this rag hand is unnecessary and invites 3-bets or bigger pots out of position in future streets. Goal is to realize equity in position and keep the pot small — call the 50 to complete and evaluate the flop. If we miss, we'll use position to check/fold to heavy action; if we connect, we can apply pressure.

I am in the big blind with Td 5h. Minni has limped from the button. While T5o is a weak hand, I have the option to see a free flop. There is no reason to bloat the pot with a weak hand out of position against a stack that covers me. I will check and see the flop.

I have nothing on this flop (Ten-high, no draws). Since this was a limped pot, there's no reason to lead out with a bluff out of position. I will check and see if Minni wants to take a stab at it, likely folding to any significant bet.

We missed this flop (3h5s on 8♠2♥J♣) but we're heads-up on the button with position and the pot is small (200). This is a dry, non-coordinated board that favors a high c-bet frequency; betting allows us to take the pot down now and fold out many overcards / weak jacks. I'll make a half-pot bluff to apply pressure and keep the pot manageable if called. If Flash raises large I will relinquish the hand (we have very little showdown value); if he just calls I can continue selectively on favorable turns. Bet size = 100 (~1/2 pot).

I have Ten-high with no draws on a board of Jack-high. In a limped pot, Minni has led for a minimum bet. While the price to call is small, my hand has virtually no equity and I am out of position. I will fold and conserve my chips for a more favorable hand.

Holding 9c 3h in the Small Blind is a very weak starting position. While it's only 50 more to call, this hand plays poorly post-flop and Minni has been quite aggressive recently. I'll fold and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button with Ad Kc — a premium hand. I want to take the initiative, build the pot and get value while also having fold equity. Min-raise is 200, but a standard 3xBB sizing is cleaner here: raise to 300 total (that includes my posted SB). This sizing pressures the BB, balances value and fold equity given our deep stacks (24375 vs 15475), and leaves plenty of maneuvering room postflop. Plan: continuation bet most flops; if Flash 3-bets, re-evaluate based on sizing (call smaller 3-bets to play postflop; consider 4-bet/shove only vs very large or polar 3-bets).

In Hand #66, I am in the Big Blind with King-Five offsuit (Kh 5s). Minni, on the Button, has raised to 300. This is a standard button open in a heads-up situation. My hand is marginally strong enough to defend the big blind against a wide opening range from the button, especially since I only need to call 200 more into a pot of 400. While K5o is not a premium hand, folding here would be too tight given the current blinds and the frequency of button raises. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate my position then.

I have completely missed this Ace-high board with my King-high. On an Ace-high flop, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage. Since I am first to act, the standard play is to check. If Minni bets, I will likely fold as I have no real equity or draws to continue.

I have top pair top kicker (Ad Kc) on a fairly dry but two-spade flop (6d As 9s). Flash checked after calling my preflop 3x; this is an ideal spot to take the initiative and get value while charging draws. I’ll bet 300 (50% pot = 300 into 600) — big enough to extract from worse A-x, Kx and medium pairs and to charge spade/runner draws, but small enough to keep worse hands and bluffs calling. With deep stacks this sizing leaves good maneuvering room post‑turn. If called I’ll continue to value-bet on safe turns and be cautious on spade turns (check or size down); if Flash check-raises large I’ll assume a very strong hand (two pair/set or a polarized bluff) and re-evaluate (call smaller raises, fold to polar/shove-sized aggression).

On this Ace-high board, Minni's continuation bet is very likely to be successful as the board texture favors the pre-flop aggressor. Holding only King-high with no pair and no significant draw, I have very little equity to continue in this hand. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

I have Ace-Queen offsuit (AQo) on the button in a heads-up situation. This is a premium hand and should be played aggressively for value. I will open with a standard raise to 300 to build the pot and take the initiative.

Heads-up dynamics: the button (Flash) is opening very wide, so my calling range from the big blind can be quite wide. I hold Q7o — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. The pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (I’ve already posted the BB), so calling gives me correct pot odds (~33% required). I’m reasonably deep (24,625 vs Flash’s 14,975), so implied odds and postflop maneuverability favor defending. 3-betting to 500 with Q7o is too thin out of position and would commit unnecessary chips with a weak hand. I’ll call to see a flop, control the pot on dry boards, and proceed cautiously: float/bluff on favorable textures, value/c-bet when I hit top pair or good equity, and fold to heavy resistance when I miss and have little equity.

Heads-up, out of position with Q7o on a paired, dry flop (9♦ 2♠ 2♥). I have no pair and little equity; there’s no meaningful draw to pursue. Betting as a C‑bet can win the pot sometimes, but it also commits chips with a very thin hand and plays poorly if I get raised. I’m deeper than Flash, so I can apply pressure later when I actually improve or pick better spots — no reason to bloat the pot now. I’ll check to control the size of the pot, induce bluffs, and fold to strong aggression (or call small bets as a cheap bluff‑catcher).

I have Ace-Queen high on a dry, paired board of 9-2-2. As the pre-flop aggressor, I have a range advantage on this board. A continuation bet here serves to take down the pot immediately, get value from some weaker Ace-high hands, and prevent my opponent from realizing equity with low cards. I will bet 300, which is half the pot, to maintain initiative.

Flop: I'm OOP in the BB with Q♦7♥ on 9♦ 2♠ 2♥. I checked to control the pot and induce — Flash (button) c-bets a small 300 into 900, which is a standard thin continuation bet from a very wide opening range. Calling 300 to see the turn requires roughly ~20% equity (300 to win ~1500), and versus a wide c-bet range my queen-high has reasonable showdown value and will beat a lot of pure air/weak high-card hands. I don't want to 3‑bet bluff OOP with a marginal hand; keeping the pot small and realizing equity is the safer +EV line. Plan: call the 300, fold to significant raise, and re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair the Q or pick up meaningful equity I'll start extracting value, otherwise use position/stack advantage later to apply pressure.

I have Q♦7♥ on 9♦ 2♠ 2♥ 6♥. I checked the flop and called a thin c‑bet — I didn’t improve on the turn and there are no meaningful draws that complete (a heart on the river won’t give a flush). I’m out of position and have only queen‑high, so there’s no value to gain by barreling OOP and no reason to inflate the pot. Checking controls the pot, preserves fold equity on later streets, and lets me fold to significant turn aggression or call small bets as a cheap showdown when facing wide, thin ranges from the button. Plan: check; if Flash bets very small I can call as a bluff‑catcher, but I’ll fold to larger, committed bets.

On a paired board of 9-2-2-6, my Ace-Queen high still has significant showdown value in a heads-up pot. After my flop continuation bet was called, Minni's range consists of 9x, mid-pocket pairs, or other Ace-high hands. By checking back the turn, I control the pot size, realize my equity for free, and can potentially induce a bluff on the river or catch an Ace or Queen to move ahead of her 9x/pocket pairs. Betting again is unlikely to fold out a 9 and rarely gets called by worse hands.

I hold Q♦7♥ on 9♦ 2♠ 2♥ 6♥ K♥ — no pair, no real draw. Flash opened wide preflop and c-bet the flop, then checked the turn, but the river King significantly improves his preflop raising range (lots of Kx), so a river bluff OOP is thin. Betting to steal risks getting called by Kx/9x/2x and even some wide hands he’ll call with; with deep stacks the price of a failed bluff is high and EV negative. Pot is only 1,200, so the prudent play is to check and take showdown (or only call a very small, obvious bluff).

On the river, the board has paired deuces and now features a King overcard. I have Ace-Queen high. Looking back at the action, Minni called my flop continuation bet on a dry 9-2-2 board, which suggests she likely has a piece of the board, such as a 9, or a small pocket pair (33-88). By checking the turn, I opted for pot control and to realize my showdown value. Minni's check on the river further indicates she is likely in 'showdown mode' with a marginal hand. While the King is a good scare card for me to bluff, it's risky because her range is condensed toward hands that might not fold for a single bet (like a 9). Since I still have decent showdown value with Ace-high in a heads-up pot, and betting is unlikely to get called by worse hands or fold out many better ones, checking is the most prudent play to see the showdown.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) I should be opening very wide to use position and fold equity. 3♣9♥ offsuit is marginal but acceptable in this spot — raising gives initiative and often wins the pot preflop. I’ll make the minimum legal raise to 200 total: it preserves fold equity while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: fold to a large 3‑bet (this hand plays poorly multiway/against aggression); if called I’ll continuation-bet favorable flops and use position to control the pot or fold to strong resistance.

I am in the Big Blind with 6s 4s. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Small Blind/Button. I only need to call 100 more into a pot of 300, giving me 4:1 pot odds. Suited gappers like 64s play well enough post-flop in heads-up situations, especially given the excellent price. I will call to see a flop.

I am first to act on the flop of Qh Qd 7h. I have 6-high with no draws on a board that is very unlikely to have hit my range. Leading into the pre-flop aggressor on a paired board with zero equity is not a profitable strategy. I will check and most likely fold to any significant bet from Minni.

I opened very wide from the button with 3♥9♣, so my preflop range is strong enough to represent Qx or a set here. The flop Q♥ Q♦ 7♥ misses my specific hand (no pair, only a backdoor heart possibility) but Flash checked. In heads‑up play a high frequency continuation bet is profitable — many of the Big Blind’s calls won’t contain a Queen. I want to maximize fold equity while keeping the price small if I’m called, so I’ll make a half‑pot probe (200 into the 400 pot). If Flash folds I take the pot; if he calls I have very little showdown equity and will give up to significant turn aggression (or a check‑raise). I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and only continue with a clear improvement or a very favourable runout.

Minni has bet 200 into a pot of 400 on a board of Qh Qd 7h. I have 6-high with no pair and no real draw (only backdoors). While the price is 3:1 to call, my hand has virtually zero equity against Minni's range, which includes many Queens, pocket pairs, and better Ace/King highs. There is no reason to continue with such a weak holding on a board that favors the pre-flop aggressor.

I am on the button with 6-5 offsuit. This is a standard hand to play in a heads-up situation, but I should be careful as I have a smaller stack than Minni and she has been aggressive. I'll complete the small blind to see a flop for a cheap price.

Flash (button/SB) just limped to the big blind. Heads-up A♥7♦ is a solid raising hand — it dominates a lot of limp-calling junk and gives us initiative postflop. I want to isolate and deny good odds to see a cheap flop, so I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x the BB). This size is small relative to effective stacks, builds the pot when I’m likely ahead, and keeps postflop options open. If Flash 3-bets big I’ll fold or re-evaluate; if called I’ll c-bet favorable ace/low-textured boards and play straightforwardly on turns/rivers.

I am holding 6-5 offsuit on the button in a heads-up match. After my initial limp, Minni raised to 300. With stack sizes this deep (over 150 big blinds) and having positional advantage for the rest of the hand, calling 200 more to see a flop is a standard play. I am getting 2-to-1 pot odds and the opportunity to outplay my opponent post-flop.

I hold A♥7♦ — top pair with the best possible kicker on 7♠–3♦–6♣. Flash limp‑called preflop so his range is wide (small pairs, suited connectors, broadways, occasional traps). The flop is relatively dry: the main hands that beat me are unlikely (sets or the specific 4+5 combo that makes a straight). I want to get value from worse pairs (6x,3x), Ax hands, and charge any backdoor/runner draws while denying free cards. I'll c‑bet about half the pot to extract value and keep the pot manageable given deep stacks. If he raises I will re‑evaluate (call small raises, be cautious/fold to very large, polarized check‑raises).

I have middle pair (sixes) with a five kicker on a low, connected board (7-3-6). There is also a backdoor straight draw. Minni made a continuation bet of 300 into a 600 pot. This is a standard sizing and I cannot fold middle pair here, especially in a heads-up match where ranges are wide. Calling allows me to keep the pot manageable while seeing how the turn develops, maintaining position.

Turn: Ac pairs the board and gives me A♥7♦ two pair (Aces and Sevens). Flash limp-called preflop and called my flop c‑bet, so his range is wide (weaker one-pair hands, small pairs, some Ax, occasional connectors/draws). Very few hands legitimately beat me — mainly sets (33,66,77) or the rare 4‑5 straight. I’m ahead versus the bulk of his range and should extract value. With the pot at 1,200 a bet around 2/3 pot (~800) is large enough to charge worse pairs and Ax combos but small enough to keep calling ranges intact. If Flash raises big I’ll strongly weigh set/straight and re‑evaluate (call small/medium raises, be cautious/fold to a polarized shove depending on sizing). Betting 800 maximizes value while controlling the pot against the most likely hands.

The Ace on the turn is a significant scare card that connects well with Minni's pre-flop raising range and flop continuation betting range. My middle pair of sixes has very little potential for improvement, as I am likely drawing to only two outs if she has an Ace or a pair of sevens. Minni's bet of 800 into a 1200 pot (2/3 pot) represents considerable strength on this board. In a heads-up match, while it is important to defend, middle pair is a weak candidate for calling a second barrel on an overcard turn, especially when out of position (though here I am in position, the strength of the Ace makes it difficult to continue). Folding allows me to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up from the SB on the button Q8o is well within a standard opening range — limp/call is suboptimal. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50, but I want to seize initiative and apply pressure to Flash’s wide BB defending range. I’ll raise to 300 (≈3x BB): large enough to generate fold equity and isolate, but not so large that I commit too much with a mediocre hand. Postflop plan: c-bet on dry or Q-high flops, play cautiously and pot-control on ace/connected/flushy boards, and fold to large 3-bets or heavy aggression unless I improve.

I am in the Big Blind with J2s. Minni, on the button, has raised to 300. This is a standard open from a wide range in heads-up play. While J2s is not a premium hand, it has suitedness and some high-card value. The pot odds are 2:1 to call (200 to win a total pot of 400). In a heads-up situation, J2s is generally strong enough to defend the big blind against a standard raise, especially given the stack depths and the potential to realize equity on the flop. I will call to see a flop and evaluate from there.

On a flop of Tc-Ts-Jd, I have flopped top pair (Jacks) with a weak kicker (Deuce). The board is paired (Tens), which means anyone with a Ten has a very strong hand (trips). However, Minni's range as the pre-flop raiser is wide. Checking is the standard play here to see how she reacts. If she bets, I have a clear call given I have top pair, though I must be cautious of the Tens or a better Jack. I will check to her.

I opened from the SB with Q8o and wanted to apply pressure preflop, but this flop (Tc Ts Jd) is not one I planned to c-bet — it misses my hand and connects reasonably well with Flash’s calling range (T’s, J’s, pocket pairs, broadways). I have only queen-high with no meaningful draws, so betting is a thin bluff that will be called by many better hands and can get me raised off the pot. I’ll exercise pot control and use position to see a turn card. Plan: check and evaluate — call smallish continuation bets to realize equity (we have stack depth), fold to large, committed-sized bets or raises, and if we pick up a queen or safe turn I’ll start betting for value/put pressure.

I have improved to a full house (Tens full of Jacks) on this turn. Given the board contains three Tens, the only hands beating me are the case Ten (Quads) or Pocket Jacks (Higher Full House), both of which are mathematically unlikely. After the flop was checked around, I will check again on the turn to induce a bluff or a bet from weaker hands like Ace-high or small pocket pairs that Minni might be holding, as a bet now might fold out her entire range.

Check. The turn (Tc Ts Jd Th) has made trip tens and dramatically improved Flash’s calling range — any jack, any pocket pair, or any ten in his range now beats or ties me. I have Q8o with virtually no equity and betting here is a thin bluff with poor fold equity and a high risk of a check‑raise or overbet that would commit a large portion of my stack. I’ll pot‑control and use position to see the river, folding to heavy river aggression and only considering a very narrow bluff on a harmless river card.

On a board of Tc-Ts-Jd-Th-7s, I hold Js-2s, giving me Tens full of Jacks (T-T-T-J-J). This is an extremely strong hand. The only hands beating me are the case Ten (Td) for Quads or pocket Jacks for a higher full house. Given that Minni checked both the flop and the turn as the pre-flop raiser, it is highly unlikely she holds a Ten or pocket Jacks, as she would almost certainly bet those for value on such a board. My goal now is to extract value from weaker hands like Ace-high, or smaller pocket pairs (like 88 or 99) that might believe their hand is good or that I am bluffing after checking twice. A bet of 400 (two-thirds pot) is a standard value size that can get called by an Ace or a Jack (splitting the pot) while looking potentially like a missed draw or a thin bluff.

Fold. The river (Tc Ts Jd Th 7s) is extremely hostile to my Q8: the turn already put three tens on board, so Flash’s check-call line then leading small on the river is far more likely to be value (full houses/quads or jacks/other made hands) than a bluff. Calling 400 into a 1000 pot requires ~28.6% equity — my realistic equity vs his value-weighted range is essentially zero (only beats very rare pure bluffs). No reason to gamble a marginal portion of my stack against a range that very rarely bluffs here, so I fold and preserve my chips for better spots.
In a heads-up situation, A3o is a strong starting hand on the button. I will put in a standard raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially take down the blinds or see a flop with a lead.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB) heads-up. I’m in the big blind with T5o; pot is 400 and I need to call 200 (300 total committed including my posted BB). With deep stacks and the button’s likely wide opening range in heads-up, T5o has enough equity and the pot odds (~33%) justify defending. I don’t want to 3-bet this weak offsuit hand out of position because that bloats the pot and puts me in tough spots postflop. Plan: call to see a flop, play the board cautiously — value/raise with a real pair or strong draw, fold to heavy pressure if I miss.

Check. I hold T♠5♦ on 4♦4♥Q♥ — no pair, no heart, and only weak showdown equity. Out of position versus a wide heads-up opener, I want to control the pot and see how Flash reacts. Checking lets me realize equity and fold to significant pressure or call a small c-bet; leading here with a marginal hand would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots.

On a board of 4d-4h-Qh, I have Ace-high with a Trey kicker. This is a paired board, which often misses the caller's range. Since Minni checked to me, I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to represent a Queen or a pocket pair, and to capitalize on the folded equity against her likely missed range.

Flash (button) opened wide preflop and now makes a small flop bet (300 into a 900 pot) on 4♦4♥Q♥. I have T♠5♦ — no pair, no backdoor heart, only modest showdown equity. However the price is good: calling 300 into 900 requires only ~20% equity and the button’s c-bet range is fairly wide heads-up. Raising OOP with this weak hand would bloat the pot and put me in tough spots; folding would be too nitty given the pot odds. Plan: call the 300 to see the turn, realize equity, and fold to significant turn aggression unless I pair/improve or pick up a plausible draw. If I hit a T (or the turn bricks and villain barrels big) I’ll re-evaluate.

Board is 4♦-4♥-Q♥-K♦; I hold T♠-5♦. I checked/called the small flop c-bet to see a cheap turn — I still have no pair and no realistic draw after the K♦. Betting OOP here as a bluff is unattractive: my passive line (check-call) on the flop is consistent with a wide continuing range, so a turn bet unlikely folds out many of Flash’s likely hands (Qx, Kx, Ax, sets) and just bloats the pot when I have essentially zero equity. With deep stacks and a 1200 pot, pot control is the right play — check and keep the pot manageable, gather info from Flash’s turn action, and re-evaluate on the river (call very small bets as a bluff-catcher only or fold to larger aggression).

The turn King is a significant scare card. While it could have improved some of Minni's calling range from the flop, it also fits well into my pre-flop raising range. However, after her call on the flop, she likely has some piece of the board (a Queen or a draw). By checking back, I control the size of the pot and allow my Ace-high to potentially win at showdown or improve on the river, without risking a check-raise on a relatively coordinated board.

Board: 4♦-4♥-Q♥-K♦-7♠. I hold T♠5♦ — no pair, no flush/straight draws. Opponent opened wide preflop and made a small flop c-bet, then checked the turn, which reads as pot control with Qx/Kx or a missed overcard rather than a big value hand (they would have barrel wider on turn if they were trying to push with lots of equity). I have no value to extract and my line (check-call flop, check turn) gives me very little river bluff credibility/fold equity. A bluff here would be thin and will be called too often by Q/K/any decent showdown hand; a value bet is impossible. With a 1200 pot and deep stacks, preserving stack and minimizing variance is best. Check to see showdown; if Flash now fires a credible bet I’ll fold.

On this board of 4-4-Q-K-7, I have Ace-high. I made a continuation bet on the flop which Minni called, but we both checked the King on the turn. Minni has now checked the river as well. While I could attempt a bluff to push her off a small pocket pair, Ace-high has significant showdown value in heads-up play, especially against missed heart draws or lower Ace-high combinations. I will check behind to take the hand to showdown and avoid a potential check-raise.

Heads-up on the button with A♦2♣ is a clear open-raise spot. I want initiative and to take advantage of my positional advantage and the Ace blocker, so I’ll raise rather than limp. I’ll take the minimum open to 200 total (a ~2x BB open) to keep the pot controlled while applying pressure to the Big Blind and preserving stack depth for postflop play. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold A2o; if he calls I can c-bet many flops and use position to extract value or fold to heavy resistance.

I am in the Big Blind with King of Clubs and Four of Hearts. Minni, who is currently the chip leader, has raised to 200 from the Small Blind (Button). This is a standard 2x open. Given the pot odds (I need to call 100 to win a total pot of 300), I am getting 3:1 on a call. King-high hands have reasonable equity in a heads-up situation against a wide button-opening range. I will call to see a flop and re-evaluate, as folding such a hand to a minimum raise is too tight in this format.

I am out of position in a heads-up pot on a dry flop of Q-8-5. I have King-high with no direct draw. Standard play here is to check to the pre-flop aggressor and see how they respond. Leading out (donk betting) doesn't make much sense with this hand strength.

Heads-up on the button I opened preflop and Flash just called, so his range is wide (broadways, small-medium pairs, suited connectors and some Axs). Flop 8♣-5♥-Q♦ is pretty dry and misses a lot of that calling range. I hold A♥2♣ — Ace-high and an Ace-blocker to many Ax combos — so a c-bet here will fold out many unpaired broadways and pick up value from worse hands that call. I’ll lead for a medium c-bet to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable; if he 3-bets big I’ll give it up, if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

I have King-high on a Queen-high dry board. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of 250 into a 400 pot. While King-high has some showdown value in heads-up play, my specific hand (Kc 4h) has no real connectivity, no draws, and I am out of position against a chip leader who has been playing aggressively. Calling here would likely lead to me folding on the turn to a second barrel if I don't improve to a King, and even then, my kicker is very weak. I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am holding 72 suited on the button. While it is suited, 72 is statistically one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. In a heads-up scenario, the small blind typically plays a wide range of hands, but 72 suited remains at the bottom of that range. Given that the opponent, Minni, has shown a tendency to be aggressive post-flop and frequently defends their big blind or raises, I will choose to fold this hand. Completing the small blind for 50 more is an option, but the lack of connectivity and high-card strength makes it difficult to play profitably across most board textures.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with 6♠9♥ and deep stacks — opening ranges should be wide. 6-9 offsuit is marginal but perfectly playable in position. I want initiative and fold equity to avoid giving the BB a free look and to let me use position postflop, so I’ll open to the minimum raise (200 total, 2x BB). This keeps the pot controlled, applies pressure to Flash’s wide defending range, and preserves maneuverability postflop (I can c-bet favourable flops or check behind when needed). If I face a large 3-bet I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing; if called I’ll play postflop aggressively in position.

In this heads-up situation, I'm in the big blind with 5s 6c, which is a marginal but playable hand given the pot odds. Minni has raised to 200, making it 100 more for me to call. The pot is currently 300, giving me 3:1 on a call. Given my stack size and the stage of the tournament, I will call to see a flop, looking for straight draws or two-pair/trips potential while playing cautiously against an aggressive opponent.

I have 6-high on a very dry, paired board (Kc Ks 8c). Since Minni was the pre-flop aggressor and I have no pair or draw, the standard play is to check and evaluate her response. I do not have enough equity to lead out or call a significant bet.

Heads-up on the button I opened preflop to take the initiative. Flop Kc Ks 8c is fairly dry and paired — I have no pair and no real draw (no clubs, no single-card straight draw). Flash checked, which in heads-up often shows weakness or a hand he doesn’t want to lead with. I want to use my initiative and fold equity to steal the pot here, so I’ll make a standard half-pot continuation bet (200 into 400). This sizing pressures non-K hands (Ax without clubs, Qx, Jx, small pairs) while keeping the pot manageable if I get called. If Flash raises, I’ll fold most of my range since I have almost no showdown value; if he calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously — barrel only on safe cards or give up to strong action.

On a paired board of Kc-Ks-8c, my 6-high has almost zero equity. Minni, being the pre-flop aggressor, is likely continuation betting. Since I have no pair, no straight draw, and no flush draw, there is no reason to put more chips into the pot. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with 4c 2c. While these are suited connectors, they are at the bottom of the range. However, at 150 total chips in the pot and only 50 to call, the pot odds are 3:1. In a heads-up situation, calling with suited gappers is acceptable to see a flop, especially since I have a significant stack and can afford the small investment to potentially outplay the opponent or hit a big draw.

I'm in the big blind (24375) with Js4d. Flash (SB) merely completed to 100, so the pot is 200 and I can check or make a tiny raise to try to steal. Js4o is a marginal, unsuited hand and I'll be out of position postflop with deep effective stacks (~15425), so raising invites multi-street decisions I don't want to play with this holding. I prefer pot control and seeing a flop cheaply. Plan: check to see the flop; if I pair the J or pick up strong equity (good draws) I'll continue, otherwise keep the pot small and fold to heavy aggression on dry boards.

I have top pair (J♠) with a weak 4-kicker on a very dry flop (5♣ J♦ 3♠). Flash merely completed from the SB so his range is wide and contains many worse Jx, small pairs (5s/3s) and overcards that will call a c‑bet.
