This is a spot to extract value and take the initiative without bloating the pot with a marginal kicker. A half‑pot bet (100 into 200) will get calls from worse hands, deny free cards to any backdoor draws, and define his range. If he raises big I’ll fold or strongly soften my line; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the turn (proceed cautiously if an overcard comes).

On the flop of 5c-Jd-3s, my 4c-2c gives me an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or 6 completes the straight). The pot is currently 300 (200 from pre-flop plus Minni's 100 bet), and it costs me 100 to call, giving me pot odds of 3:1. With 8 outs to a straight, my equity to hit on the turn is roughly 17%. While I don't have direct pot odds to call, the implied odds are significant in heads-up play, as hitting a straight often results in a large payout. I also have a backdoor flush draw. Calling is a standard move here to see the turn card.

I have top pair (J♠) but a very weak 4-kicker on a fairly dry board (5♣ J♦ 3♠ → 9♣). Flash (SB) called my small half‑pot c-bet on the flop, so his calling range is wide: worse Jx, small pairs (5s/3s), occasional 9x, and some floating/overcards. On the turn I want to extract value from worse Jx and small pairs while keeping the pot controlled and charging any backdoor draws. A half‑pot turn bet (~200 into 400) accomplishes that — gets called by worse, folds pure air, and doesn’t overcommit me with a vulnerable kicker. If he raises big, I’ll give strong weight to folding; if he calls I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

On the turn, the 9 of clubs has significantly improved my hand's potential. I now have an open-ended straight draw (any Ace or 6) and a flush draw (any club). With 15 outs to a potentially winning hand (8 for the straight, 9 for the flush, minus 2 overlapping outs), I have approximately 32.6% equity to hit on the river. The pot is currently 600 and it costs 200 to call, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Since I need 25% equity to make a break-even call, and I have over 32%, calling is mathematically correct. Furthermore, the implied odds are high if I hit, as Minni has shown aggression throughout the hand.

I have top pair (J♠) with a very weak 4‑kicker on a fairly dry board (5♣ J♦ 3♠ 9♣ 6♦). Flash (SB) has been a passive caller — he completed/called small bets on flop and turn — so his range is wide and contains many worse Jx and small pairs that will call a modest river bet. Pot is 800; I want value from worse Jx/5x/3x while keeping the pot controlled and avoiding getting check‑raised off a weak kicker. I’ll make a small value bet (300, ~37.5% pot) to induce calls from worse hands but not overcommit. If he raises big, I’ll give strong weight to folding given my poor kicker and his line.

On the river, the 6 of diamonds completes a 6-high straight (2-3-4-5-6) for me using my 4c and 2c. While higher straights are technically possible if Minni holds 7-8 (9-high straight) or 4-7 (7-high straight), a straight is a premium hand in a heads-up match. Minni has shown aggression on every street, and her 300-chip bet into an 800-chip pot on the river is small enough that it likely represents a Jack-high hand, two pair, or a set looking for value or attempting a blocking bet. I will raise to 1000 to extract maximum value from those hands. Given the board texture and the action, I am highly likely to have the best hand.

I planned a small river value bet (300) to get called by worse Jx and small pairs, but Flash’s large raise to 1000 after passively calling flop and turn strongly polarizes his range toward value (J9/J6-type two pair, sets, or rare rivered two‑pair/boats) with very few natural bluffs on this dry board. Pot math: I must call 700 more to win ~2800, so I need ~25% equity. Given his line and the board texture, my J♠4♦ has well under that vs his raising range (my weak kicker rarely improves me vs two‑pair/trips), so this is a clear fold. With a deeper read that he bluffs or a much smaller raise I’d consider calling, but here I preserve chips and avoid a marginal call against a polarized line.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♣3♣ — a playable, suited ace with strong postflop playability and useful blockers. Stacks are very deep, so I want to take the initiative and build the pot while keeping sizing reasonable to avoid inviting massive 3-bets. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (300 total) to get value vs worse aces/pairs and to apply fold equity. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and tendencies; if he calls I’ll use my positional advantage to play the flop.

I am holding 3s 2d, which is mathematically the weakest starting hand in Texas Hold'em. Although I am in the Big Blind and only need to call 200 more into a 400 pot, 32o realizes equity very poorly and is rarely ahead of any opening range from the Button. Given the tournament stage and my stack size, there is no reason to defend such a weak hand.

I am on the button with Ten-Four offsuit. While it is only 50 chips to call the big blind, this is a very weak hand to play out of position or against an aggressive opponent like Minni. Given the current stack depths and the stage of the tournament, I prefer to fold and wait for a better opening rather than potentially bleeding chips with a marginal hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with 6♦3♦. In heads-up I should open a very wide range and take the initiative — 6-3 suited has decent playability in position (backdoor flush/straight equity and good postflop maneuverability). I’ll raise rather than limp to build the pot while in position and to deny the BB a cheap look. A raise to 300 (3.0x the BB total) is a standard, table-consistent sizing that applies pressure and keeps the fight heads-up. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll use position to c-bet favorable flops and realize equity on draws; if Flash 3-bets heavily I’ll be willing to fold this hand (I’ll consider a call only to small, capped 3-bets depending on size/read), and I’ll fold to shove-sized 3-bets. Raise to 300 to seize initiative and maximize postflop options.

I am holding 6-4 offsuit in the big blind against a 3x raise from Minni. While the pot odds are 2:1 to call, this hand has very poor playability and equity against a standard button raising range. Given that I am currently trailing in chips in this heads-up match, I prefer to wait for better spots to commit chips rather than defending with the bottom of my range.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit on the button. This is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. While I have position post-flop, the hand has very little connectivity or high-card strength. Given Minni's stack lead and recent activity, there is no reason to speculate with such a poor hand. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to use my position.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Qc5c — a suited queen with decent postflop play and card removal value. Pot is 150, I need 50 to call and min-raise is to 200. In heads-up I should be opening a wide range to take the initiative and steal the blind; raising gives fold equity and lets me play pots in position. With deep stacks (24k vs 15.8k) I can realize equity postflop, so I’ll make the standard min-raise to 200 (total committed). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing/reads (likely fold or proceed cautiously); if he calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops and use position to control the pot.

With 7d 3h in the Big Blind and needing to call only 100 more chips into a pot of 300, the pot odds are 3-to-1. However, 7-3 offsuit is one of the weakest starting hands in Texas Hold'em. Given the stack depths and the fact that I am out of position against Minni, who has been aggressive, folding is the most disciplined play to avoid being trapped in a marginal post-flop situation with a very weak hand.

I am on the button with 9h 7h, a strong speculative hand in a heads-up setting. Raising here is standard to take initiative and use my positional advantage throughout the hand. A raise to 300 is a standard sizing for this stack depth.

Heads-up in the big blind with K♦9♦ against a button open to 300. This is a playable, suited K in heads-up — good equity versus a wide opening range and lots of postflop playability (top pair, second pair, diamond/straight draws). Folding is too tight and a tiny 3-bet to 500 buys little fold equity while losing positional clarity; a larger 3-bet would be needed to meaningfully seize initiative, but K9s isn’t strong enough to polarize. Calling is the best line: we get correct pot odds (invest 200 to win ~600) and can proceed postflop based on texture — C-bet or raise on favorable K/diamond/connected boards, check/fold or pot-control when we miss, and fold to large aggression. I’ll call and evaluate the flop.

Heads-up out of position on an A‑high flop (5♣ 4♦ A♠). I hold K♦9♦ — no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond possibility (need both turn and river; ~3% to make a flush). Flash is a wide preflop raiser and will c‑bet this texture frequently, including with lots of Ax and broadway combos. Betting myself would be a thin bluff with minimal fold equity and would bloat the pot OOP with a hand that plays poorly vs the caller’s continuing range. I’ll check to control the pot, induce bluffs, and see how Flash reacts. Plan: call small continuation bets to realize equity or fold to large aggression; if checked to, take a free turn and only be aggressive if I pick up a King/diamond or the turn changes the board in my favor.

On an Ace-high board (5c 4d As), I have a significant range advantage as the pre-flop aggressor. I will place a continuation bet of 300 (half the pot) to capitalize on this advantage and pressure the opponent to fold their air or weak holdings. Even though I only have 9-high, the presence of the Ace makes it difficult for the big blind to continue without a piece of the board or a strong draw.

Flop: 5♣ 4♦ A♠. I have K♦9♦ out of position. Flash (button) is a wide preflop raiser and is c‑betting a small 300 into 900 — a fairly standard, low-frequency bet to seize the initiative. I have no pair and only a very weak backdoor diamond/straight prospect, so raising OOP would be too thin and polarizing with little fold equity. Folding is overly nitty given Flash’s wide c‑betting range. Calling is the best line: the bet is small enough to justify a float/realize-equity play (I can pick up a king or diamond or use position read on the turn to bluff), and I keep pots controllable OOP. Plan: call 300, see the turn; if I hit a king or a diamond or the turn gives a good bluffing card I can be aggressive, but I’ll fold to large turn aggression when missed.

I called the flop with K♦9♦ and checked the A‑high flop to control the pot. The turn (6♣) did not improve my hand — I have no pair and, crucially, no realistic draw (a flush was impossible after the turn because there was only one diamond on the board and I hold two). Flash checked to me, so betting out OOP with essentially zero showdown equity would be a thin bluff with poor fold equity against a preflop raiser who continues a wide range (many Ax and pairs). I’ll check to keep the pot small, see a free river where I can realistically improve to a pair (K or 9) and only bluff/lead if the river materially changes the board or gives a credible story. If Flash bets the river, I’ll fold most non‑improving cards and consider a call only to small sizing or when I picked up a pair.

On the turn, the 6 of clubs has improved my hand significantly, giving me an open-ended straight draw (needing an 8 or a 3). Since I maintained aggression as the pre-flop raiser and flop bettor on this Ace-high board, I will continue with a semi-bluff. Betting here allows me to capitalize on my range advantage (representing a strong Ace) while having multiple outs to the nuts if called. A bet of 800 into a 1200 pot puts maximum pressure on Minni's marginal pairs and weak draws, potentially ending the hand immediately while building a pot for my made hands.

Board A♠-5♣-4♦-6♣; I hold K♦9♦ and have no pair or real draw (turn killed the backdoor diamond possibility). Facing an 800 bet into a 2000 pot, calling would require roughly 28.6% equity (800/(2000+800)). I only have ~6 outs to pair (3 Ks + 3 9s) ≈ 13% to hit the river, well below the required equity. Villain’s line (preflop open, small flop c‑bet, turn bet) is consistent with many Ax and made hands that dominate K‑high; while some bluffs exist, I don’t have draws or blockers that make a raise profitable OOP. Raising would be a thin, high-variance bluff and calling is a marginal float with poor math. I’ll conserve chips and fold.

I'm on the button (small blind) heads-up with 4♠2♥ — a very weak hand. It's only 50 more to see a flop and I have position, so I prefer to complete rather than open-raise with junk. Calling keeps the pot small, preserves stack depth, and lets me evaluate the flop; I'll only continue aggressively if I hit or pick up a strong draw, and I'll fold to significant aggression (including a preflop 3-bet).

I am in the Big Blind with 9c 5c. Minni, on the button, has completed the small blind (limped). Since there is no further action to call, I have the option to check and see a flop for free. While 95s is a decent speculative hand, I am out of position for the rest of the hand and there is no need to bloat the pot pre-flop against a larger stack. Checking is the most standard and safe play here to realize my equity.

I have 9-high on a paired board of 2d 6d 6h. In a limped pot, there is little incentive to bet here without a made hand or a significant draw. Checking allows me to see a turn for free and evaluate Minni's reaction. Since I have no showdown value and no draw, I will play this passively and check.

Board is 2♦-6♦-6♥ and I hold 2♥4♠ — I have bottom pair (pair of twos) in position. Flash checked the flop, so I should prioritize pot control and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand that's vulnerable to any 6, overpairs, or diamond draws. A probe-bet only charges diamond draws and gets called by better hands; checking lets me realize equity and see a free or cheap turn to reassess. I’ll check back and proceed based on the turn card and opponent action.

I have 9-high with no draws on a 2d-6d-6h-Th board. The pot was limped and the flop was checked around, indicating neither of us likely has a strong hand. While a bet might occasionally take the pot down, I have zero equity and there is little reason to bloat a small pot with air. Checking allows me to see a river for free and potentially win a very small pot if Minni has even weaker air, or simply fold if she decides to bet.

I have bottom pair (2♥) on a board of 2♦-6♦-6♥-T♥. Flash has been completely passive (checked flop and turn), and the pot is small (200) relative to both stacks. This is a marginal holding that’s vulnerable to any 6, any Ten, and to overpairs; there are also diamond draws that a bet would only charge and that can outdraw me on the river. Betting here would bloat the pot for little value and expose me to check-raises by stronger hands. I’ll keep pot control and realize my equity in position — check and reevaluate on the river, calling reasonable bets and folding to large aggression if the runout is dangerous.

The board has run out 2d 6d 6h Th Jd and I have only 9-high. The hand was limped pre-flop and checked on every street so far. While it is possible Minni has nothing, my 9-high has almost zero showdown value. However, in a pot this small (200 chips), there is little reason to attempt a bluff, as any pair or even Ace-high might call. I will check and hope to either win a tiny pot if she has a lower air hand or simply concede the pot if she bets or shows a better hand.

Check. I have bottom pair (2♥) on a 2♦-6♦-6♥-T♥-J♦ board and Flash has been completely passive (checked flop/turn/river). The river brings a third diamond and a Jack — lots of hands now beat me (any 6, any Ten, any Jack, any diamond flush, or overpairs). The pot is small (200) and betting for thin value is unlikely to be profitable while risking a check-raise or getting called by better hands. I’ll keep pot control, check to showdown, and only call reasonable river bets (small value bets), folding to large aggression.

In heads-up play, T6o is a hand that can be opened for a raise to apply pressure from the button. While not extremely strong, it is within a reasonable opening range to take control of the pot early. I will raise to the standard 3x blind size.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 from the SB. I have 8♥6♦ OOP — a marginal hand but vs a wide SB opening range it has enough equity to continue. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% pot odds, and keeping the pot small with a call preserves my deeper stack and keeps postflop options open. 3-betting OOP with 8-6o is too thin (and a min‑3bet to 500 leaves awkward SPR), while folding here is too nitty in heads-up. Plan: call to see the flop, proceed cautiously — fold to heavy pressure on dry boards, look to realize equity or bluff when I pick up initiative or good turn cards.

I have 8♥6♦ on A♥ K♥ 4♦ — no pair and only a runner‑runner heart possibility (~4% to make a flush). The flop massively favors the raiser’s range (lots of A/K and hearts) and I’m out of position. Betting here would bloat the pot with a very weak hand and invite calls/raises from better Ax/Kx/heart combos. Best line is pot control: check and fold to any meaningful continuation bet. If Flash checks back I’ll see a free turn and re-evaluate; only consider calling an unusually tiny bet (<~1/4 pot) for pot odds, otherwise release the hand.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop comes with two high cards (Ace and King) that fit my perceived range perfectly. Although my actual hand (Ten-high) has no value, this is a standard spot for a continuation bet. A bet here puts maximum pressure on the opponent's wide big-blind defending range, which likely missed this board. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot to represent a strong hand and take the pot down immediately.

I checked the flop OOP with 8♥6♦ on Kh 4♦ Ah and now face a 300 c-bet into a 900 pot. Calling 300 here requires roughly ~20–25% equity to be +EV; my hand has almost no immediate equity — only a remote runner-runner heart flush (~4%) and a few pair outs that are unlikely to be good versus a c-bet range full of A/K and hearts. Out of position with no pair and only backdoor outs, calling to chase is a losing line and 3-betting would be too thin. I’ll preserve my stack and fold to this reasonable-sized continuation bet.

I’m on the button (small blind) with 3♠2♦ — a very weak offsuit hand, but in heads-up location and with deep stacks it’s cheap to see a flop. Pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to complete, bringing my total contribution to 100. I prefer to call rather than min-raise: raising invites 3-bets and bloats the pot with a hand that has poor playability. Calling preserves stack, lets me leverage positional advantage post-flop, and gives good pot odds to see a flop. If Flash 3-bets sizable pre-flop I’ll fold; post-flop I’ll proceed cautiously and only continue with meaningful equity or favorable board textures.

In the Big Blind with Jack of Spades and Four of Hearts, I have the option to check and see a free flop since Minni just completed the small blind. J4o is a weak hand, but it has some high-card value and I am in position for the rest of the hand. Checking is the most standard play here to keep the pot small.

I have bottom pair (fours) on an Ace-high board. While I have hit a piece of the flop, my hand is quite weak and vulnerable. Being first to act post-flop in this heads-up situation, I will check to see how Minni responds. This allows me to keep the pot small and see if I can get to a cheap showdown or improve on the turn.

I’m on the button with 3♠2♦ on a 7♠-4♠-A♦ flop. I have no pair — only a four-out wheel draw (any 5 completes A-2-3-4-5, ~8.5% on the turn, ~16.5% by the river) and a very slim backdoor spade flush (need both turn and river spades, ~5% by river). Flash checked the flop, which can be either weakness or pot control with an Ace. Betting here is thin — most worse hands will call and better hands (Ax, pocket pairs) will continue; bluffing into a checked Ace risks bloating the pot out of position if he wakes up. With position and decent chase equity, the correct line is to check and realize equity cheaply. Plan: check the flop, evaluate the turn — if a 5 or spade appears I can start building the pot or take the lead; on a blank I will be prepared to fold to significant aggression.

On the turn, the board is 7s 4s Ad 5h and I hold bottom pair (fours) with a Jack kicker. Minni checked back the flop, suggesting she likely doesn't have an Ace or a strong pair, although she could be trapping. The 5h is a somewhat blank card, though it creates some straight possibilities. Since the pot is small and my hand is weak, I will check again to control the pot and try to reach a cheap showdown. Betting doesn't accomplish much as I'm likely only called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me.

The turn 5 just completed a wheel for me: A-2-3-4-5 — a made straight on the turn. Heads-up this is a very strong hand vs Flash’s check-check line on flop and turn, which often contains Ax, medium pairs, or spade draws. I want to start building the pot and extract value from worse hands (A‑x, pairs, draws) without overbetting and scaring them off. A pot-sized bet (200 into 200) is a clear value bet that prices in calls from Ax and pocket pairs and charges spade draws; it keeps the pot manageable if Flash wakes up and raises. If Flash raises I’ll re-evaluate (call a reasonable raise for pot control / fold to extreme over‑aggression), but for now bet for value.

I have bottom pair (fours) on an Ace-high board. Minni checked the flop, which reduces the likelihood of her holding an Ace or a strong hand. Her turn bet of 200 into a 200-chip pot appears to be a standard stab to take down the pot. In a heads-up match, a pair is often worth a call in this situation, especially since I am getting 3:1 pot odds. I will call to see the river and decide how to proceed based on the final card and her subsequent action.

I hold bottom pair (fours) on a board that has an Ace and several cards higher than my pair. Minni limped pre-flop and checked the flop, then bet the turn when I checked to her. I called that bet to see the river. The river 8h doesn't significantly change the board, although it does complete a possible 6-9 straight or improve any 8x she might have been bluffing with. My hand has very little value for betting, but it does have some showdown value against missed draws or high-card hands. I will check to see what Minni does. If she checks behind, I may win a small pot; if she bets, I will have to evaluate based on the bet size.

I made the wheel (A‑2‑3‑4‑5) on the turn — a very strong hand. The only realistic hand that now beats me is any 6 (which makes 4‑5‑6‑7‑8 with the river 8). Flash’s line (check on flop, call my 200 turn bet, then check the river) looks like Ax, a pocket pair, or a missed draw more often than a slow‑played 6. A half‑pot bet should get calls from worse A‑x and medium pairs while folding out pure bluffs. I’ll bet for value (keep it small to induce calls). If Flash check‑raises big I’ll re‑evaluate (call a normal raise for pot control, but respect an extreme overbet/shove).

Minni limped from the small blind and the flop checked through. She then bet the turn and the river on a board of 7s-4s-Ad-5h-8h. While I have a pair of fours, it is bottom pair on a very coordinated board. The 5 on the turn and the 8 on the river both represent cards that are likely to be in a limping range and beat my hand. Furthermore, any 6 makes a straight. Although I am getting 3:1 pot odds, my hand is too weak to act as a reliable bluff-catcher in this spot against a line that looks like thin value or a straight. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.
In heads-up play, T4s is a strong enough hand to open from the button. I will raise to 300 to maintain an aggressive posture and attempt to take down the pot pre-flop or play the hand in position.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 (3x). I have Ad6s — an ace with a weak kicker — which is strong enough to defend the big blind in heads-up play but is marginal to 3-bet out of position. Calling 200 more (300 total) keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize equity and exploit a wide opening range, while avoiding committing more preflop OOP with a weak kicker. Plan: see the flop and proceed based on texture — if I hit an ace I’ll extract value, otherwise exercise pot control and fold to heavy aggression or a strong 4-bet/shove.

Heads-up vs the button who opened wide preflop. Flop 7c-5c-9c is very wet/monotone — lots of club flushes and connected hands in his range. I have Ad6s: only ace-high, no club, very little showdown value and poor redraws. With deep stacks and a small pot I want to keep the pot controlled and avoid getting barreled off a lot of equity. Plan: check and let him have the initiative — fold to sizable continuation bets or raises, consider calling very small probes but otherwise take a free card and reassess on the turn. Checking preserves stack and maximizes long-term EV from this marginal holding.

On a board of 7c 5c 9c, I have Ten-high with no clubs. This board texture is very connected and favors the big blind's calling range, which often includes middle connectors and clubs. Since I have no piece of the board and no flush draw, checking back is the most prudent play to control the pot and see if I can improve on the turn or potentially bluff later if the action allows.

Out of position with A♦6♠ on a monotone 7♣-5♣-9♣-4♣ board and the pot only 600. I have no club, only Ace-high with a weak kicker, and the opener’s range contains a lot of club combos and made flushes — his two passive checks make his range ambiguous (could be slow-played flush/pair or missed draws). Betting now would commit chips as a bluff into many hands that beat me and get called by any club; better to exercise pot control and preserve stack. I’ll check and see the river — call small probes if reasonable, fold to sizable aggression, and only bluff-catch or bluff on the river with a clear read.

The board now shows four clubs, and while I have improved to a pair of fours, I do not hold a club myself. My opponent has checked again, which could indicate weakness or a slow-play with a flush. Since any single club in my opponent's hand beats me, there is little value in betting. Checking back allows me to see the river for free and control the pot size with a marginal hand.

River: 7c-5c-9c-4c-5h. I hold A♦6♠ with no club — only Ace-high. With four clubs on board any club in Flash’s hand gives him a flush, and his preflop raising range contains a lot of club combos; he could also have 5x/7x full house/quads although those are rarer. Given his passive check-check line and the heavy club texture, a river bluff is unlikely to fold enough of his range and will get called by all flushes and many pairs/A-highs. Pot is small relative to stacks, so I preserve chips and take the free showdown. Action: check.

On a board with four clubs and a pair of fives, my two pair (fours and fives) is extremely vulnerable. Any single club in Minni's hand makes a flush, and any 7, 9, or 5 also beats me. Minni has checked three times, suggesting she either has a weak hand with no showdown value or is potentially trapping with a flush. Given the board texture, betting serves little purpose as a bluff since any club is unlikely to fold, and I have some marginal showdown value against her complete air. I will check back to reach showdown as cheaply as possible.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with Js Th — a very playable hand in heads-up play with straight and overcard potential. Stacks are very deep (~233bb) so I want the initiative and to play postflop in position. Opening here is standard: I’ll raise to 300 (total 300, 3x BB) to apply pressure to Flash’s wide BB defending range, build the pot when I hit, and pick up pots with fold equity. Completing to 100 surrenders initiative and gives the BB cheaper flops; a min-raise to 200 is too passive. If Flash 3-bets I’ll re-evaluate based on sizing and tendencies (call versus a medium 3-bet to realize equity, fold or 4-bet selectively vs very large 3-bets).

I have Ace-King offsuit, a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has opened from the button for 300. To maximize value and take control of the hand out of position, I will 3-bet. A raise to 1000 is consistent with my previous play with strong hands like pocket Tens and Ace-Ten suited, and it puts pressure on Minni's opening range.

I opened to 300 on the button with J♠T♥ and Flash responded with a small 3‑bet to 1000. With deep stacks (~230bb) and heads‑up dynamics, JTo is a very playable hand in position: it has good equity vs a wide 3‑bet range and plenty of postflop playability. The call is cheap relative to the pot (700 to call into ~1300, making the pot ~2000 — I need roughly 35% equity), so I’ll keep the initiative in position and see a flop rather than bloating the pot with a light 4‑bet. Plan: call, play the flop in position, c‑bet or check/fold depending on texture and opponent aggression.

On a monotone flop of [3c 9c Jc], I hold the Ac which gives me the nut flush draw along with two overcards (Ace and King). I was the pre-flop 3-better, representing a very strong range. Leading out with a continuation bet here allows me to maintain initiative, capitalize on the fold equity against hands that missed the flop or lack a club, and build the pot for the times I hit my flush. A bet of 700 into a 2000 pot (35%) is a standard sizing to keep the pressure on while not overcommitting if Minni raises.

I have J♠T♥ on J♣9♣3♣ — top pair with a decent kicker, in position. Flash 3-bet preflop (wide range heads‑up) and now posts a small flop bet 700 into a 2700 pot (~25% pot). That sizing looks like a probe/float or a small value bet rather than an obvious huge made hand. The board is club‑heavy so the main concern is a two‑club combo that already has a flush, but those are a minority of a 3‑betting range. Calling 700 here requires only ~21% equity (700 / (2700+700) ≈ 20.6%), which top pair easily has versus a wide c‑bet range. In position I prefer pot control and to let him barrel turn/river so I can extract value or fold to clear strength. A raise is possible to deny equity to single‑club hands, but a min‑raise is too small and a larger raise bloats the pot against the occasional made two‑club flush. Plan: call now, re-evaluate on the turn — bet for value if he checks, or proceed cautiously (or fold to heavy aggression) if a club or significant action shows up.

The turn 7s is a relative brick that doesn't change the board texture significantly. I still hold the nut flush draw with the Ace of clubs and one overcard. Having 3-bet pre-flop and led the flop, continuing with a second barrel is a strong play. It maintains the initiative and puts pressure on Minni's range, which likely consists of one-pair hands or smaller club draws that may find it difficult to call a substantial bet. If I am called, I still have significant equity to hit the nut flush on the river. I will bet 1700, which is approximately half the pot, to maintain a polarized representation and maximize fold equity.

I have top pair (J♠) with a decent but vulnerable T kicker on J♣9♣3♣7♠. Flash 3‑bet preflop and has been firing small/medium bets (700 on the flop, 1,700 on the turn into a 5,100 pot) — a line consistent with a wide, aggressive range containing thin value and bluffs, but only a small subset (hands with two clubs) are full flushes. Calling 1,700 gives me the correct pot odds (~20–25%) to continue with top pair in position, keeps the pot controlled, and lets me extract value or fold on the river with more information. A raise here bloats the pot against the very hands that already crush me (two‑club flushes, sets, better Jx) and has limited extra fold equity; folding is too nitty given my hand and the sizing. I’ll call 1,700, then re‑evaluate on the river — fold to huge show of strength that represents a flush/set, or bet for thin value if he checks.

On the river, I have improved to top pair with the King of Hearts, and I still hold the Ace of Clubs which acts as a crucial blocker to the nut flush. Throughout the hand, I have maintained initiative with a 3-bet pre-flop and barrels on both the flop and turn. However, Minni has called every bet on a monotone board, which strongly suggests she has a made hand—most likely a flush, a set, or two pair. While my top pair has showdown value, betting into a board with three clubs after being called twice is risky, as most hands that would call a third bet likely beat a single pair. By checking, I can potentially induce a bluff from missed straight draws or lower pairs, or get to a cheap showdown against hands I might beat like a Jack or a lower King. If Minni bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing carefully, but checking is the most prudent way to manage the pot and realize my hand's value.

I have top pair (J♠) with a T kicker on 3♣-9♣-J♣-7♠-K♥ — a decent but vulnerable hand because the flop contained three clubs (any two clubs = flush). Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop bet, larger turn bet, then a river check) looks like a wide, aggressive range that includes thin value, bluffs/missed draws and a minority of made flushes/strong hands. His check on the river is more indicative of weakness or pot control than a pure value shove. Betting a small, thin‑value size is the highest EV play here: it extracts from worse pairs and marginal Jx hands, keeps the pot manageable against flushes/strong hands, and still has fold equity. Pot is 6,800 — I’ll bet ~1/3 pot to get called by worse but avoid bloating the pot. If Flash check‑raises big (polarizing to flush/set/two‑pair) I’ll fold; if he calls I expect to win many showdowns against his missed/medium hands.

On the river, the King of Hearts gives me top pair with an Ace kicker. I also hold the Ace of Clubs, which is a critical card as it blocks all nut flushes for my opponent. After I 3-bet pre-flop and fired two barrels on the flop and turn, my check on the river might look like I have given up on a draw or am holding a marginal hand. Minni's bet of 2250 into a 6800 pot is relatively small (about 1/3 pot), offering me excellent pot odds of approximately 4:1. This means I only need to be right about 20% of the time to make calling profitable. While Minni could have a lower flush, two pair, or a set, the presence of the nut flush blocker and the realization of top pair on the river, combined with the favorable price, makes this a mandatory call in a heads-up scenario. I am catching bluffs or thin value bets from hands like Jack-X or a weaker King.
In heads-up play, 83s is a reasonable hand to open from the button to apply pressure and take control of the pot. Minni has shown some willingness to fold to aggression in recent hands.

I’m in the big blind with J♥T♠ facing a 3x open to 300 from the button. Pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me (300 total). J-T offsuit is a strong, playable heads-up hand with good equity vs a wide opening range, but it’s not a premium 3-bet candidate deep and out of position postflop. A tiny raise to 500 won’t fold out enough of his range and a larger 3-bet just bloats the pot with a marginal hand. Calling realizes equity, keeps the pot controllable, and lets me evaluate the flop (continue with pairs/draws, check-fold or pot-control on bad A-high/dry boards). I’ll call 200 more (300 total) and play the flop accordingly.

I’m out of position on a dry K♥‑5♦‑2♥ with J♥T♠ — I missed the flop and only have a weak backdoor heart (needs both turn+river, ~4%). Villain opened preflop so his range includes lots of Kx/Ax and broadways that connect well with this board. Betting here would be a thin bluff that bloats the pot out of position with little equity. I’ll check to control the pot, see how Flash reacts, and take a free turn card. Plan: if he checks back I can continue on a good turn; if he c-bets a small size I can call to realize equity, but I’ll fold to a large bet since my hand has very little showdown value.

I am the pre-flop aggressor and the flop (K-5-2) favors my perceived range significantly. A King-high board is ideal for a continuation bet since I am likely to have Kings or high pocket pairs in my raising range. Minni's check indicates weakness or a marginal hand. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot to try and take the pot down immediately.

Out of position on K♥‑5♦‑2♥ with J♥T♠. I’ve missed — I only have jack‑high and a runner‑runner heart (needs both turn+river, ~4%). Flash’s half‑pot c‑bet (300 into the 600 pre‑bet pot → pot now 900) gives me about 25% pot odds (300 to win ~1200). My chance to make at least a pair by the river is ~24% and I also have some showdown equity vs weaker broadway/Ax parts of his opening range. Raising is too ambitious OOP with a marginal hand and folding is too tight facing a small c‑bet after I already invested preflop. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable. Plan: fold to large turn aggression on blank cards, but if I pair or see a favorable turn (or he checks) I’ll continue appropriately.

Board K♥-5♦-2♥-Q♠; I hold J♥T♠ and have missed. I have no pair and my backdoor heart draw is dead on the turn — only outs are three Jacks + three Tens (~6 outs, ≈13% to hit the river). Pot is 1,200 and Flash has been the aggressor (raised pre, c-bet flop), so his range is heavy in Kx/Ax/Qx and strong broadways. Betting out OOP as a thin bluff will bloat the pot with essentially air and is unnecessary if he checked to me on the turn. I’ll check to control the pot, see what he does, and preserve stack depth for a river decision. Plan if he bets: call only very small bets (~≤200) because calling B into a 1,200 pot requires break-even equity B/(1,200+2B) (200→12.5% ≈ my ~13%); fold to larger turn bets (300+), since my equity is too low. If he checks back, I get a free river to try to improve or bluff selectively if the card and read allow.

The Queen of Spades on the turn is an excellent card to continue my bluff. It's a 'scare card' that strengthens my perceived range as the pre-flop aggressor. Since Minni called a bet on a King-high flop, she likely has a marginal piece of the board like a 5, a 2, or a small pocket pair. A second barrel here puts immense pressure on those weak hands, and based on previous hands (like #81 and #83), Minni has shown a tendency to fold to turn aggression on boards with multiple high cards. I will bet 800 to maximize the chance of a fold.

I hold J♥T♠ on K♥‑5♦‑2♥‑Q♠ and have missed — my only clean outs are three Jacks and three Tens (6 outs, ≈13% to pair by the river). Pot is 2,000 and Flash’s turn raise is 800, so calling 800 to win 2,800 requires ≈28.6% equity. My actual equity (~13%) is far below the break‑even point, so a call is -EV unless he’s bluffing an implausibly large portion of the time. His line (open, flop aggression, now a sizable turn raise) is consistent with Kx/Qx/Ax and strong broadway hands that I’m behind to — I have no fold equity OOP and my backdoor draw is dead. Raising as a bluff OOP with no real equity is irresponsible; folding preserves my stack and allows me to wait for a better spot. I’ll fold.

Heads‑up on the button (I’ve posted SB 50) with J♦3♣ — a weak offsuit holding but well within a heads‑up opening range. I want to take the initiative and avoid giving the big blind a free flop; the table’s typical open sizing has been ~3x BB. I’ll raise to 300 total (I’ll be committing 300 chips including my SB). This sizing gives decent fold equity, keeps SPR manageable with a marginal hand, and lets me play postflop in position. If Flash 3‑bets large I’ll heavily favor folding; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously — c‑bet favorable flops, control the pot on connected/strong‑looking boards.

I am holding 7c 5s in the big blind. Minni has raised to 3x from the button. While I am getting 2:1 odds on a call, 75o is a very weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially with the deep stack sizes we currently have. Given Minni's recent displays of aggression and the difficulty of realizing equity with this hand, I will choose the conservative route and fold.

I am holding 8h 3c on the button (small blind) in a heads-up situation. This is one of the weakest hands in poker with no real connectivity or high card value. While I have the chip lead and could attempt a steal, folding here preserves my stack and avoids playing a junk hand out of position post-flop should Minni call. There is no reason to commit more chips with this specific holding.

Heads‑up on the button with Jh‑4c and 172bb effective — I should be opening a wide range rather than limping. A min‑raise to 200 (2bb) is standard: it applies pressure to the big blind, keeps the initiative and preserves my positional advantage postflop. J4o is marginal but fine in a heads‑up open range; if Flash 3‑bets big I can fold and wait for a better spot, if he calls I’ll play aggressively in position and c‑bet appropriate flops to capitalize on my range advantage. Raising also prevents giving him free equity and keeps the pot manageable with deep stacks.

KJs is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Since I am in the Big Blind and Minni has min-raised from the Button, I will 3-bet to 700. This builds a value pot with a strong hand and allows me to take the initiative despite being out of position for the remainder of the hand.

I opened to 200 heads‑up with J♥4♣ and Flash 3‑bet to 700 (I must call 500). With deep effective stacks (≈170bb me, ≈220bb him) and heads‑up dynamics I should defend a wide range and use position postflop. J4o is marginal but playable in position; calling keeps the pot manageable, preserves postflop leverage, and gives me the correct pot odds to continue against a likely fairly wide 3‑bet range. Pot = 900; calling 500 (total committed = 700) requires ~35.7% equity to break even, which is reasonable versus a mixed 3‑bet range from an aggressive BB. I avoid 4‑betting with a weak hand that lacks strong blockers (would bloat the pot OOP and commit me with dominated holdings). Plan: call to see the flop, c‑bet or apply pressure on favorable textures, fold to heavy turn/river action when clearly behind, and exploit positional advantage.

In this heads-up 3-bet pot, I have flopped middle pair (Jacks) with a strong King kicker on an Ace-high board. As the pre-flop aggressor, I will lead out with a continuation bet of 500 (approximately 35% of the pot). This action is consistent with representing a range that includes strong Aces (like AK or AQ) while also providing protection for my current hand against overcards. It allows me to maintain the initiative and gauge Minni's strength; if she calls, I can move into pot-control mode on the turn unless the board improves my hand further.

Flop: 2♥ J♣ A♠ — I hold J♥4♣, which is a pair of Jacks but with a very weak kicker. Flash is the preflop 3‑bettor and now bets 500 into a ~1,900 pot; calling 500 requires only ~21% equity (500 / (1900+500) ≈ 20.8%). Versus his 3‑bet/c‑bet range I beat lots of bluffs and many broadway hands and have decent equity overall, but I’m behind any Ax. Raising here would be thin: I have poor kicker, limited fold equity versus a 3‑bettor, and could get into a bloated pot when dominated. In position I prefer to call, control the pot size, and re‑evaluate on the turn — fold to clear strength (Ace/large bet), or extract value/pressure if he shows weakness. Call to see the turn and plan accordingly.

On this Ace-high board, my second pair of Jacks with a King kicker is a solid hand for pot control but likely behind if I face heavy action. Having been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, checking the turn allows me to keep the pot manageable and evaluate Minni's response. It also protects my checking range, as I can still have showdown value or potentially induce a bluff from missed draws. If Minni checks back, I can likely take the hand to showdown; if she bets, I will decide based on the sizing, but for now, checking is the most prudent move for pot control.

I turned two pair (Jacks & Fours) — a very strong heads‑up holding. Flash was the preflop 3‑bettor who c‑bet the flop and then checked the 4 on the turn; that line is consistent with either pot‑control with an Ace/top pair or bluff/catch — but there are relatively few hands that legitimately beat me (A4, AJ, sets). I want to extract value from Ax and single‑J/one‑pair hands while keeping worse hands and thin calls in, so I’ll make a constructive value bet rather than check and invite a free river or a delayed bluff. A ~50% pot bet (≈1,200 into 2,400) accomplishes that: it folds pure air, gets called by worse Ax/Jx and some draws, and keeps the pot manageable if I’m behind. If Flash check‑raises big I’ll strongly consider that he has a very strong made hand and will re‑evaluate (likely fold to an all‑in or very large raise).

On the turn, I have second pair with a King kicker. I checked to control the pot size and to see how Minni would react on an Ace-high board. Her bet of 1200 into a 2400 pot is a standard sizing that could represent an Ace, but it could also be a bet with a weaker Jack, a pocket pair like 88-TT, or a semi-bluff. Given the pot odds (3:1) and the strength of my hand in a heads-up context, folding would be too tight. I will call to see the river and evaluate the final action, keeping the pot manageable as originally intended by my check.

I have a pair of Jacks with a King kicker on an Ace-high board that now features a Queen on the river. The Queen is a significant overcard to my pair and likely improves many of Minni's calling range hands, such as KQ, QJ, or QT, and of course, any Ace she might have. Since I am first to act, checking is the most appropriate move. It allows me to reach showdown as cheaply as possible with a medium-strength hand that now has limited value as a bet. If Minni checks back, I can see the showdown; if she bets, I will have to evaluate the sizing and the likelihood that she is bluffing with missed draws (like the spade flush draw that didn't get there) versus value betting an Ace or a Queen.

I have two pair (Jacks and Fours) on the river (Jh,4c vs board 2h Jc As 4s Qd) — a very strong heads‑up holding. The hands that legitimately beat me are relatively narrow (AJ, A4, A2, AA, JJ, or quads/44) but Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, check on turn, call my raise) also contains a lot of single‑Ace and single‑Jack hands and some bluffs. He checked the river, showing some passivity/weakness; I should be extracting value from worse one‑pair hands (AK/AQ/KQ/Qx, some missed draws) while still keeping worse hands and thin calls in. A ~50% pot value bet (~2400 into 4800) is the right balance — big enough to charge one‑pair hands and get value, but small enough to be called by those worse hands. If he check‑raises very large (polarized to the nuts), I will re‑evaluate (he likely has a hand that beats me and I would fold to an overcommitting jam); otherwise I expect a call and collect. Action: bet 2400.

On the river, the board is 2h Jc As 4s Qd. I have Kd Jd, which was second pair on the flop but has now been demoted to a weak showdown hand by the appearance of the Queen on the river. The action sequence is telling: Minni called a 3-bet pre-flop and then called a continuation bet on an Ace-high flop. When I checked the turn to control the pot, she led out for half the pot and has now fired another half-pot bet on the river. This line strongly suggests she has an Ace, or at the very least, improved to a better pair with the Queen (like KQ or QJ). While there is a missed spade flush draw on the board that she could be bluffing with, her range for calling a 3-bet and a flop c-bet on an Ace-high board is heavily weighted toward Aces. Given the pot odds, I need to be right more than 25% of the time to make this call profitable. Considering the board texture and the way the hand played out, my pair of Jacks is likely beaten by any Ace or Queen in her range. I will fold to preserve my stack for better opportunities.

In heads-up play, Ace-Four offsuit is a strong hand to open from the button. I want to build the pot and take the initiative. Raising to 300 is consistent with previous sizing and exerts pressure on the Big Blind.

Heads-up, deep stacks (~195 BB effective) and Flash opened to 300 from the SB/button. I hold 3♣2♣ — a weak but suited, connected hand that plays well heads-up and has good implied odds. The pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more (call to 300) — about 2:1 immediate pot odds plus large implieds. I’ll call to see a flop rather than 3-bet with this junk; calling keeps my range wide and lets me realize equity on club/low/connected boards. Postflop plan: continue aggressively if I hit a club, straight draw, or two-pair; fold or pot-control on dry A/K/Q boards and respect significant pressure out of position.

I flatted pre with 3♣2♣ to keep a wide HUSG range, but the flop Q♥ K♦ 7♥ is very bad for my holding. I have no pair, no hearts and only a tiny backdoor club/straight chance — essentially air. The flop heavily favors the opener’s range (lots of Kx/Qx/Ax and heart draws) and I’m out of position, so bluffing here is high-risk with no blockers. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid getting bloated OOP. Plan: fold to a sizeable bet, consider calling a very small stab to realize backdoor equity or float versus a predictable opponent, and only become aggressive if I pick up a club or a 2/3 on the turn.

On a board of Qh-Kd-7h, I have Ace-high. This board is quite coordinated and likely hits a significant portion of the Big Blind's calling range (Kings, Queens, and heart draws). However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I want to maintain the initiative with a standard continuation bet. A bet of 300 (half pot) is large enough to fold out some of the opponent's air hands (like small pocket pairs or non-heart connectors) while keeping the pot manageable if I'm called. If I encounter resistance (a check-raise), I can comfortably fold. If called, I will likely check the turn to realize my equity and look for an Ace.

Heads-up, deep stacks and Flash's flop c-bet is very small (300 into 900). I hold 3♣2♣ which is essentially air on Q♥ K♦ 7♥ — I have no hearts and cannot make a five-card flush or a five-card straight with only two cards left. My only realistic outs are the three remaining 3s and three remaining 2s (6 outs) for ~24% to pair by the river (with a tiny extra chance for two-pair/trips). Calling 300 into a 900 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (break-even ~25%), so this is roughly a breakeven/marginal spot but justified versus a wide c-bet frequency from the button. I’ll call to realize equity and keep my range wide; plan is to fold to sizable turn pressure if I miss, but continue or become aggressive if I pair or see a very favorable, non-heart low card or if the turn betting is unusually small.

Out of position on Qh Kd 7h 5s with 3♣2♣ — essentially air. I have no hearts and no realistic straight/flush chances; only six clean outs (three 2s + three 3s), ~24% to pair by the river. Pot is 1,200 and there’s no bet to face on the turn. Betting as a bluff here is thin: I don’t block Q/K/heart combinations and a bet will often be called by better hands, risking a big OOP pot with very little equity. Safer line is pot control — check and see the river. Plan: fold to any sizeable turn/river pressure unless I pair, but consider calling very small river bets (<= ~1/3–1/4 pot) given pot odds. Action: check to keep the pot manageable and preserve the chance to realize equity on the river.

On the turn 5s, the board texture remains relatively unchanged from the flop. My opponent called a flop bet on a board featuring two high cards (King and Queen) and a flush draw. This indicates she likely has a piece of the board (a Queen or a King) or a draw. My Ace-high has very little showdown value, but betting again is unlikely to force a fold from better hands like a Queen or a King at this stage. By checking back, I control the pot size and take a free card to try and improve to an Ace on the river, or potentially pick up the pot if she checks again on a non-heart river.

Heads-up, deep stacks (~190+ BB), pot 1,200, board Qh Kd 7h 5s Qd and I hold 3♣2♣ — no pair, no hearts, no meaningful blockers. Flop c‑bet then turn check by Flash is consistent with Kx, Qx, heart draws or missed bluffs. The river paired the Q, which gives trips to any Q and improves many value hands; I have essentially zero showdown value and very limited fold equity (no queen or king blockers). Bluffing into this range OOP is thin and likely to be called by better hands. I’ll check and preserve my stack — if Flash bets a very small amount (<= ~1/3 pot) I can consider a call as a bluff‑catch, but I won’t lead out with a bluff into this line with nothing.

On the river Qd, the board is now Qh-Kd-7h-5s-Qd. My Ace-high has some showdown value in a pot that was checked on the turn and river. Minni called a flop bet, which suggests she has a piece of the board or a draw. If she has a missed draw (like hearts), I win. If she has a 7, a King, or a Queen, she is unlikely to fold to a river bet after checking twice. Betting doesn't accomplish much because I won't get better hands to fold and I won't get worse hands to call. Checking back allows me to see the showdown and potentially win with Ace-high without risking more chips.

Heads-up on the button with AhTc and deep stacks (~190BB). This is a strong, playable ace that does well raised for value and to seize initiative — I want to isolate the big blind and avoid seeing flops for free. Standard 3xBB sizing (raise to 300) builds the pot, folds out the weakest hands, and gets value from worse A's and broadways. Postflop plan: c-bet on ace/high or favorable boards, check/plan to pot-control on dry, unhelpful boards; re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets large.

In Hand #92, I am in the Big Blind with Jack of Hearts and Eight of Hearts (Jh 8h), which is a solid suited connector in a heads-up situation. My opponent, Minni, has raised to 300 from the Button/Small Blind. The pot is currently 400 and it costs me 200 more to call. Given that we are heads-up and my hand has good playability post-flop, especially with the potential for flushes and straights, calling is the standard and most prudent play here. I have a healthy stack of over 20,000 chips, and I want to see a flop without unnecessarily bloating the pot pre-flop with a marginal hand.

On a flop of 6c-5s-4h, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7) and two overcards. This board texture is generally better for the Big Blind's defending range than the Small Blind's opening range. However, standard play as the first actor is to check to the pre-flop raiser. I will check to see Minni's reaction and determine if I want to continue based on the bet size.

I have position and initiative after a preflop raise. The 6c-5s-4h flop is very dry and misses a big chunk of the BB’s calling range (broadways, Ax, small pairs). With AhTc I have one-card top (the ace) but no real draws, so a standard c-bet is the +EV line to fold out weaker holdings and extract value from worse Aces/Kx/broadways. I’ll bet ~58% pot to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable; if Flash raises big I’ll assume strength and re-evaluate, if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn.

On a flop of 6c-5s-4h, I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 7 for an eight-high straight) along with two overcards. My opponent, Minni, has bet 350 into a pot of 600. This board is very coordinated and generally hits my Big Blind defending range better than her opening range. With the gutshot and the potential for my overcards to be good (or to provide bluffing opportunities on later streets), I have sufficient equity to call the 350 bet. I will call to see a turn card, keeping the pot controlled while retaining my drawing potential.

On the turn, the 8c gives me top pair (8s) with a Jack kicker to go along with my gutshot straight draw (needing a 7). The board is highly coordinated (4-5-6-8), which means any 7 has a straight. As the player out of position (Big Blind), I will continue with a check-call strategy to control the pot size while holding a hand with significant showdown value and drawing potential. If Minni bets, I have a very comfortable call; if she checks behind, I can evaluate the river.

I c-bet the dry 6♣-5♠-4♥ flop with A♥T♣ to take the initiative and fold out weaker holdings. Flash’s call on the flop represents a wide calling range (Ax, broadways, small pairs, and the odd made straight), and his check on the turn looks like weakness or pot-control rather than a raise/lockdown. The 8♣ on the turn is a relatively blank card for his calling range — it does pair some 8x and brings a second club to the board, but I still have no pair and cannot make a flush. I want to continue representing a strong Ace and apply pressure to Kx/Qx and small pairs that called the flop, while keeping the pot controllable with no pair. Bet ~50% pot is the best line: it folds out many worse hands, retains fold equity, and keeps options open for the river. Plan: if he folds I take it; if he calls I re-evaluate on the river; if he raises big I’ll concede and fold this air hand.

On the turn, the 8c gives me top pair (8s) with a Jack kicker. However, the board is extremely coordinated (4-5-6-8), meaning any 7 in the opponent's hand makes a straight. My opponent, Minni, has continued to bet, putting 650 into a 1300 pot. With top pair and a gutshot straight draw (any 7), I have too much equity to fold, but raising is too risky given how many hands in her range (like 77, 97, or A7) could have me beat. I will call to see the river and evaluate based on the final card.

On the river, the King of Diamonds (Kd) is an overcard to my pair of Eights. The board remains very coordinated with a straight possible for any player holding a Seven. Having called two streets of value from Minni, I have a medium-strength showdown hand with my pair of Eights. Leading into the aggressor here doesn't achieve much, as I'm unlikely to get called by worse hands and might get raised by better ones (Kx, straights, or sets). I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or to evaluate Minni's aggression on this final street.

Board: 6♣-5♠-4♥-8♣-K♦. I have A♥T♣ — Ace-high, no pair. I led the flop and turn and Flash called both streets, so his calling range is wide (Ax, broadways, small pairs, some 7x/8x combos). The river K creates the biggest threat (Kx now beats me) but Flash checked, which can be either cautious value or a missed hand. Checking back guarantees a showdown where many of his calling hands (small pairs, some Ax) will still beat me, while a small bet can both fold out pure air/broadway hands and get called by worse Aces and medium pairs that I beat. I want to apply mild pressure without bloating the pot with only Ace-high — a third of the pot is a good compromise. I will bet ~900 (~35% pot) to fold enough of his missed/broadway hands and extract thin value from worse Aces and pairs. If he raises, I’ll fold (I’m too weak to continue); if he calls I can accept showdown.

On the river, the King of Diamonds is an overcard to my pair of Eights, and the board remains highly coordinated with several straight possibilities (any Seven). Minni has bet 900 into a pot of 2600, which offers me roughly 3:1 pot odds. This means I only need to have the best hand about 25% of the time to make calling profitable. While her three-street aggression is concerning, my pair of Eights is a decent bluff-catcher in a heads-up match, and the small sizing of her river bet suggests I should not fold. I will call to see if my pair is sufficient to win the pot.

I am on the button with J3o. While it's a weak hand, in a heads-up situation, I'm already in for 50 chips and only need to call 50 more to see a flop. However, Minni has been playing relatively aggressively. Given my stack advantage and the poor quality of this hand, folding is a safe option to avoid getting tangled in a pot with a marginal hand out of position post-flop, though calling to see a cheap flop is also viable. I'll choose to fold here and wait for a better spot to utilize my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve already posted the SB). Qc7s is well inside a standard heads-up open-raise range: suited Q-x has decent playability and benefits a lot from acting last postflop. Calling 50 surrenders initiative and gives the BB correct odds to see a cheap flop; I want to take the lead, deny free equity, and build a pot when I’m often ahead. I’ll raise to 300 (about 3x the BB) — a clean, standard size that applies pressure, keeps my range wide and balanced, and still leaves plenty of room to fold or continue postflop if Flash 3‑bets. If he calls I’ll leverage position to c-bet or probe; if he 3-bets big I can drop Q7s without risking too much.

In heads-up play, King-high hands have significant value and should rarely be folded to a single raise from the button. Minni's 3x raise is standard, and I am getting 3:1 pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600) to see a flop. While K5o isn't a premium hand, it is well within the defending range in the Big Blind. Calling is the most balanced option here, as it allows me to see a flop and realize my equity without over-committing to a marginal hand pre-flop.

On a flop of 5c-6s-4d, I have hit middle pair with a King kicker (K5). This is a solid hand in a heads-up pot, but the board is very connected and favors the caller's range slightly more than the pre-flop raiser's in some configurations. However, as the player out of position, the standard play is to check to the aggressor to see how they react to this low, straight-heavy texture. Donk-betting here would likely only be called by better hands or draws that have significant equity against me. By checking, I can maintain a balanced range and evaluate Minni's continuation bet size.

Heads‑up on the button after I opened preflop and Flash just checked the flop. Board 5c‑6s‑4d is connected and hits a flat‑caller's range, but my Qc7s has very strong semi‑bluff equity — an open‑ended straight draw (3s or 8s) plus three remaining queens for a top‑pair candidate (roughly 11 clean outs, ~40% to improve by river). Against a wide BB calling range a half‑pot c‑bet both folds out many single‑pair hands and speculative holdings and extracts value while keeping the pot controllable if I miss. I’ll make a standard, balanced continuation bet here: 300 into the 600 pot. If I get called I’ll reassess on the turn (barrel with improvement or good turn cards, check back or fold to heavy resistance if I miss).

I have hit middle pair (Fives) with the best possible kicker (King) on a low, connected board (4-5-6). In a heads-up match, this is a very strong hand to defend on the flop. Minni's half-pot bet is a standard continuation bet that she could be making with a wide range of hands, including overcards, straight draws, or air. Folding here would be far too tight given the strength of middle pair in this dynamic. Raising is an option, but it risks bloating the pot and forcing out her bluffs while keeping me in against her stronger holdings or draws. By calling, I keep her range wide, maintain pot control, and see how the turn develops. I am getting 3:1 on a call, which is excellent price to continue.

On the turn, an Ace appears, which is a major scare card given that Minni was the pre-flop aggressor. My hand, a pair of fives, has now been demoted to third pair. In a heads-up pot, checking to the aggressor on an overcard is the standard defensive play. It prevents me from bloating the pot with a marginal hand and allows me to see how Minni handles the Ace. If she checks back, I might still have the best hand; if she bets, I will need to consider her sizing and the board's connectivity before deciding whether to continue.

Check. The turn A♣ is a very disadvantageous card for my flop c-bet: it heavily improves a lot of hands in Flash’s calling range (any A, many Ax, and it completes some backdoor possibilities). I currently have only queen-high with Qc7s and only one card to come — I have about 11 nominal outs (3 queens + 4 threes + 4 eights) but that is only ~11/46 ≈ 24% to improve on the river and many of those outs are non‑clean vs an A. Flash called my flop C‑bet, so his range is weighted to hands that will call a turn bluff or have improved, meaning my fold equity is low. Betting again would bloat a pot of 1,200 when I have limited equity; checking keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize my equity in position, and lets me see whether Flash is taking the passive line or will bluff. If he checks back I get a free river to try to hit or bluff more credibly; if he leads the river I’ll decide call/fold based on sizing and his line. Given stack sizes and the board texture, the lowest EV line is to throw more chips away here — I’ll check.

The board finished 5-6-4-A-Q. I hold a pair of fives with a King kicker. Minni's check on the turn after the Ace appeared strongly suggests she doesn't have a top pair. While my hand is weak, it has decent showdown value in a heads-up pot. Betting here doesn't accomplish much, as better hands will call and worse hands will fold. By checking, I can reach showdown cheaply or evaluate if she decides to bluff. I will check and hope my pair of fives is sufficient to win the pot.

I hold Qc7s — the river Qh gives me top pair (queen with a 7 kicker), but the turn A♣ is a clear worry because any A× in Flash’s calling range beats me. Flash’s line (call flop c‑bet, check turn, check river) looks passive/weak: likely small pairs (4s/5s/6s), missed straight draws, or medium one‑pair hands rather than frequent value Aces. Betting a small amount extracts value from those worse hands and missed draws while limiting loss to Aces. The pot is 1,200, so a ~1/3‑pot thin value bet (~400) maximizes calls from worse hands and keeps the pot controllable against the hands that beat me. If I get raised I’ll fold (his raise likely represents an Ace or a very strong/creative bluff); if called I go to showdown. Action: small value bet 400.

On the river, the board is 5c-6s-4d-Ac-Qh. I have a pair of fives with a King kicker. The most significant action in this hand was Minni's check on the turn when the Ace appeared. As the pre-flop aggressor, she would almost certainly bet an Ace for value and protection on such a connected board. Her check-back strongly suggests she does not have a top pair. When the Queen hits the river and I check a third time, her 400-chip bet (1/3 of the pot) looks like either thin value with a six or a bluff with a missed straight draw (like 78 or 89). I am getting 4:1 odds on a call, meaning I only need to have the best hand 20% of the time to break even. Given the suspicious turn check and the nature of heads-up play, my pair of fives is a viable bluff-catcher here.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is the statistically worst starting hand in Texas Hold'em. While I am on the button in a heads-up situation and only need to call 50 more chips to see a flop, this hand has extremely poor playability and equity. With a comfortable chip lead and plenty of hands remaining, there is no reason to invest even a small amount in such a weak hand.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with 2♦6♠ — a very weak hand, but in heads-up play the opening range should be wide. Pot is 150 and it only costs 50 to see the flop, but a min-raise to 200 is a standard, low-risk steal that uses my positional advantage and creates fold equity. I’ll raise to 200 total to apply pressure while keeping the pot controllable. Plan if called: proceed cautiously postflop — c-bet small on dry, favorable flops; check and defend or fold to strong resistance on wet/heavy boards. If Flash folds I take the 150; if called I’ll use position and pot control with marginal holdings.

I am holding 9s 4s in the Big Blind. Minni has min-raised to 200. I need to call 100 more, giving me 3:1 pot odds. Suited hands, even with weak kickers like a 4, are generally worth a defend in heads-up play against a small raise. I will call to see the flop and evaluate from there.

On this monotone board of hearts (Jh 8h 7h), I have 9s 4s, which gives me a gutshot straight draw but no flush potential and no showdown value. In heads-up play, the Big Blind acts first post-flop. I will check to the aggressor to see how they react to this highly coordinated board.

Flop is J♥ 8♥ 7♥ — a monotone, very coordinated heart board. I hold 2♦6♠: no hearts, no pair, no meaningful straight draw. Although I was the preflop aggressor, this texture heavily favours the BB’s calling range (lots of hearts, pair+draw combos), so a bare c‑bet would be a thin, high-risk bluff. I’ll check to control the pot and avoid getting exploited. Plan: if Flash checks back we see a turn and can continue on safe cards; if he bets I’ll fold to any normal/large c‑bet and only entertain a call to a very tiny probe (≈<1/3 pot) where pot odds make it reasonable to see a turn.

On the turn 6d, I have picked up an open-ended straight draw (needing a 10 or a 5). The board remains monotone hearts, which is dangerous since I hold no hearts. Minni checked back the flop, which typically indicates a lack of a made flush or a strong Jack. By checking again, I am looking to see a free river card to realize my equity or evaluate Minni's response to the board getting even more connected. If she checks behind, I get to see the river for free; if she bets, I can decide based on the sizing whether to call with my straight draw.

Flop (J♥ 8♥ 7♥) was highly favorable to the BB’s calling range (lots of two‑heart combos and top pairs), so I checked the dry/hostile texture to control the pot. Turn (6♦) pairs my 6s — that’s an improvement but still a very weak, bottom pair with no hearts. Flash checked the turn, which looks like weakness or pot control rather than a value bet for a made flush; however his range still contains many hands that crush my pair. With position and deep stacks I want to keep the pot small and get to showdown cheaply with a marginal made hand. Betting here would be thin for value and could induce raises/calls from better hands. I’ll check to control the pot and see the river; plan to call a very small probe but fold to a normal/large turn bet.

I have 9-high on a board that is extremely coordinated (monotone hearts and now a paired 6). My straight draw failed to materialize. Minni has checked back the flop and turn, which indicates she likely doesn't have a monster, but my 9-high loses to almost every hand in her range, including any pair or Ace-high. In a pot this small (400), a bluff is unlikely to be profitable given the board's texture. I will check and expect to lose at showdown or fold if she bets.

I have 6♠2♦ and the river is J♥ 8♥ 7♥ 6♦ 6♣ — that gives me trips (three sixes). Opponent’s range that beats me is relatively small (flushes from two hearts, some straights, and a few full‑house combos), while I beat most single pairs and many bluffs. Both players checked down earlier, so a small value bet will extract calls from worse hands (Jx/8x/7x, some straights that don’t have hearts, and other medium holdings) without bloating the pot or folding out too many callers. I’ll make a modest value bet (~1/3–1/2 pot) to maximize value while keeping the risk controlled. If Flash check‑raises large I’ll strongly consider he has a full house/flush and will re‑evaluate (likely fold to an overbet shove, call smaller raises depending on sizing).

On a board of Jh 8h 7h 6d 6c, my 9-high has absolutely no showdown value. Minni has bet 150 into a pot of 400. While her line of checking the flop and turn suggests she doesn't have a powerhouse, she likely has at least an Ace-high or a small pair that decided to take a thin value bet on the river. Since I cannot win at showdown and a bluff-raise is not advisable given my passive line throughout the hand, I must fold.

I am on the button with 8s 5c, which is a weak hand. However, we are heads-up, and the cost to see a flop is only 50 chips more. While I could raise to try and steal the blinds, Minni has been fairly active. I will simply complete the small blind to see a flop cheaply and evaluate my hand then. Folding is an option, but in a heads-up scenario, 8-high has some playability for the price.

Heads-up vs a limp from the button (Flash). J♣Q♠ is a strong hand in heads-up and performs well both as a value hand and as a hand to take the initiative with. I don't want to check and let him see a free flop with an excessively wide limp range, so I raise to isolate and take control of the hand. I choose a raise to 400 total (4xBB) — large enough to charge weak holdings and get fold equity, but small enough relative to our ~18k stack to leave maneuvering room postflop. If he folds we scoop; if he calls we plan to continuation-bet favorable flops but remain cautious OOP and re-evaluate against a substantial 3-bet.

I originally limped with 8s 5c hoping to see a cheap flop. Minni's raise to 400 changes the pot odds and the playability of my hand. 8-5 offsuit is a very weak hand that does not hit many boards strongly. Since I am in a comfortable chip lead and don't want to commit more chips to a hand with low equity in a raised pot, I will fold.

Heads-up on the button (I act last postflop) with Qc4h. In heads-up play my opening range is wide — Q4o is marginal but playable in position. I want to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and avoid playing a multiway or giving a free flop, so I’ll open-raise rather than limp. I’ll size to 3x the big blind (to 300 total) — bigger than the 2x min-raise to get more fold equity against the BB while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand; if Flash calls I’ll play the flop in position.

I have been dealt Ace-King suited, a premium hand in heads-up play. Minni has been opening frequently from the Button. I want to build a pot for value and gain the initiative. A 3-bet to 1000 is a standard size that forces Minni to pay to see a flop or fold their marginal holdings.

Flash 3-bet to 1,000 after my 300 open. I’m on the button (in position) with Qc4h — a marginal but playable heads-up hand. Calling the extra 700 (1000 total) keeps the pot manageable, preserves my positional advantage, and lets me realize equity against what is likely a fairly wide/aggressive 3-bet range from Flash. Pot odds (700 into a 1300 pot => ~35% needed) are borderline but acceptable given position and the ability to float or barrel on favorable flops. A 4-bet here would be overplaying Q4o (not enough blockers or equity to make a profitable isolated 4-bet) and folding is too passive heads-up. Plan: call, evaluate the flop — continue when I pair a queen/4, pick up backdoor clubs/draws, or can credibly bluff; fold to large, committed aggression if I completely miss the board.

On a low, connected board like 6s-5h-4d, the texture generally favors the defender's range more than the 3-bettor's. However, as the pre-flop aggressor with Ace-King, I still hold a range advantage with overpairs (77-AA) that Minni likely doesn't have after just calling the 3-bet. I will lead out with a continuation bet of 800 (40% of the pot) to maintain initiative, represent an overpair, and potentially take the pot down immediately. If called, I have two overcards and backdoor potential. If raised, I will have to evaluate carefully as this board hits many 'rundown' type hands.

I have Qc4h on 6s-5h-4d — bottom pair (a 4) with a decent kicker in heads-up and I’m in position. Flash 3-bet preflop and now made a small flop stab (800 into a 2800 pot). Calling 800 requires ~22% equity (800/(2800+800) ≈ 22%), which bottom pair vs a wide 3-bet/c-bet range comfortably exceeds. Raising here would be overplaying a marginal hand and folding is too passive in heads-up. Plan: call to realize position and keep pot manageable, then re-evaluate on the turn — fold to very large/committed aggression if I miss, but continue or extract value if I improve (two pair/trips) or can credibly bluff on later streets.

The Ace of Spades on the turn is a very favorable card for my range as the pre-flop 3-bettor. It gives me Top Pair Top Kicker (TPTK). Since Minni called my flop lead on a very connected low board, they likely have a pair or a straight draw (like 78 or 3x). I need to continue betting for value against weaker Aces (like AQ or AJ) that Minni might have floated with, and to charge their draws. A bet of 2,000 chips into a 3,600 pot (approx 55%) is a standard value sizing that protects my hand while building the pot.

I’m on the button with Qc4h and currently have bottom pair (4s) on 6s-5h-4d-A♠. Flash 3‑bet preflop (range skewed to high cards/pairs) and continued with a small flop stab that I called. The turn Ace significantly improves the portion of his range that beats me (Ax/top pair, sets, strong pair+), and he now bets 2,000 into a 3,600 pot (pot now shown as 5,600). Calling 2,000 requires ~26% equity (2,000/(5,600+2,000) ≈ 26%). I only have ~5 clean outs (three Qs + two remaining 4s) ≈ 11% to improve on the river, well below the required equity. A raise would be overplaying a marginal hand (I don’t block enough of his value combos and would be commiting too much with just a single pair). Given the sizing, the board texture and his line, his range is weighted to value here and I’m priced wrong to chase. I fold and preserve my stack for a better spot.

I am on the button with Ace-Nine offsuit (Ac 9s), which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. With a significant chip lead of approximately 23k to 16k, I want to continue playing aggressively and applying pressure. I will put in a standard open raise of 3x the big blind to 300 to take control of the hand and potentially win the pot pre-flop or build value against a wider big blind defending range.

Heads-up in the big blind facing a button open to 300 (pot 400, I must put in 200 more). I hold pocket fours (4h4c) and stacks are deep (~163 BB), so this is a textbook set-mining spot. Calling 200 into a 400 pot gives ~33% break-even equity — vs a wide button opening range my pair has enough equity and excellent implied odds if I hit a set. 3-betting would bloat the pot and lose implied odds for a hand that plays poorly OOP multi-street; folding surrenders too much +EV. Plan: call to 300 total, play cautiously out of position on dry boards, and extract maximum value if I hit a set.

I called preflop to set‑mine; the flop (Qs 7h 9h) missed my pocket 4s. I still have some showdown value heads‑up against a wide button opening and a small backdoor heart, but out of position and with deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot with a bet. I’ll check for pot control and to induce a c‑bet: call small–medium continuation bets to see a cheap turn and fold to large multi‑street aggression unless I improve to a set. If Flash checks back we reach showdown cheaply.

I have middle pair (nines) with an Ace kicker on a board that contains a Queen and several draws (hearts and straight possibilities). Minni has checked to me after calling my pre-flop raise. By betting here, I can get value from weaker hands like a pair of sevens or various draws, while also protecting my hand from giving a free card to overcards or flush/straight draws.
