A bet of 300, which is half the pot, is a standard size to maintain pressure and gather information about Minni's range.

I called preflop to set‑mine with pocket 4s and checked the flop to control the pot OOP. The board (Q♠ 7♥ 9♥) completely missed me, but Flash’s 300 into a 900 pot gives ~3:1 pot odds (need ~25% equity). Versus a wide button c‑bet range my pocket pair has enough showdown equity and excellent implied odds if I hit a set. Raising would bloat the pot and lose implied odds; folding surrenders too much +EV. I’ll call 300 to see the turn, folding to heavy multi‑street pressure or a direct heart/queen that strongly hits his range, but continuing to call small‑to‑medium bets and extracting max value if I improve.

I’m OOP with 4♥4♣ on Q♠ 7♥ 9♥ 9♦. I called preflop and the small flop c‑bet to set‑mine; the turn paired the board and I face no bet (pot ~1,200, to‑call 0). I only have 2 outs to hit a set on the river (~4.3%), so betting or raising here would bloat the pot OOP with a very weak hand and poor multi‑street playability. Flash checking the turn looks like either weakness or a slowplay — either way my best line is pot control. I’ll check the turn, see a cheap river, and then decide: fold to heavy river pressure, call small river bets for showdown, or extract value only if I actually hit the set.

I have improved to three-of-a-kind (nines) with an Ace kicker on the turn. This is a very strong hand. The board is somewhat coordinated with both flush and straight draws possible. Minni has checked for the second time, suggesting she either has a marginal made hand like a Queen or a Seven, or she is on a draw. To extract value from those hands and protect against the various draws, I will place a bet of approximately two-thirds of the pot. A bet of 800 chips is large enough to build the pot and charge her for any draws while still being a size she might call with a pair of Queens.

Heads‑up in the big blind with 4♥4♣ on Q♠7♥9♥9♦. The pot is 2000 and Flash has bet 800; calling 800 would make the pot 2800, so I need ~28.6% equity to justify a call. I only have two outs to hit a set (~4.3%), and I currently beat only pure bluffs and tiny underpairs. Flash opened preflop, c‑bet the flop and is barreling the turn — a line that often contains Qx, 9x (now trips), or value hands that dominate my 4s. Raising OOP with such a weak holding is poor with deep stacks and will get called or shoved by better hands. Fold and preserve the stack for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with T8 suited hearts — a playable, fairly strong heads-up hand. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50 to see the flop, but vs one opponent I want to take the initiative and avoid playing passively out of position postflop. A standard 2x BB open (raise to 200 total) applies pressure to many of BB’s weaker holdings, gets value from worse hands, and preserves SPR for postflop play. If Flash 3-bets large I’ll fold this holding; if it’s a small 3-bet I can consider calling to realize equity. Raising also keeps my range aggressive and prevents giving the BB cheap flops.

I am in the Big Blind with 8c 3c and Minni has raised to 200. I only need to call an additional 100 to see the flop, giving me 4:1 pot odds. In heads-up play, 83 suited is strong enough to defend the Big Blind against a minimum raise, as I only need roughly 20% equity to make the call profitable. I will call to see the flop.

On a paired board of 6c-6h-Kh, I have 8-high with some backdoor flush potential (having two clubs in hand with one on the board). I am first to act on the flop against the pre-flop aggressor. Standard procedure in this situation is to check and see how the opponent reacts, as this board likely misses most of my defending range and fits the raiser's range better. I will check and evaluate based on the bet size if they choose to continuation bet.

Heads-up on a paired King‑high flop (6c 6h Kh). I opened preflop and have Th8h — no made pair but a strong 4‑card heart draw (9 outs, ~35% to hit by river) plus some extra equity from pairing T or 8. As the preflop aggressor I should continuation-bet a large portion of my range; this hand is a textbook semi-bluff: it builds the pot when I have fold equity and charges hands that want a free card. Flash has shown a tendency to call down, so I’ll choose a sizing that keeps worse hands and Kx/low pairs in the pot while denying free cards to other draws. Bet half-pot (200 into 400). Plan: if called, re-evaluate on the turn and barrel when I improve or get good fold equity; if I face a large raise I’ll give credit to a strong made hand and fold, while a small raise I can consider calling to realize my draw equity.

Minni's half-pot bet on a King-high paired board (6-6-K) represents a strong range, including Kings and pocket pairs. My 8-high with a weak backdoor flush draw has very little equity (roughly 15-20% against a standard C-betting range) and poor playability on future streets. Calling would be a 'float' with very few outs to the best hand. Folding is the most prudent play to conserve chips.

I am holding 7-3 offsuit on the button. While it is heads-up play and I have position post-flop, 7-3 is one of the weakest hands in the deck. I have a healthy chip lead and there is no reason to commit chips with such a low-equity hand, even with the small investment required to call the big blind. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with T6o I want to take the initiative and leverage positional advantage post‑flop. Against one opponent in the big blind I should be opening very wide; a standard 2x BB (min-raise to 200) accomplishes that while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. This sizing applies pressure, preserves fold equity versus Flash’s likely defending range, and lets me c‑bet and realize equity from position on most flops. I avoid overcommitting with a marginal hand by using a small, standard raise rather than a large overbet or limp. Therefore I raise to 200.

I am in the Big Blind holding K-2 offsuit. Minni, who has been relatively active, has min-raised to 200 from the small blind. I need to call 100 more into a pot of 300. This gives me 3:1 odds. K-2 isn't a premium hand, but heads-up against a min-raise, it has enough equity to see a flop, especially as the chip leader. I will call and see if I can connect with a King or use my positional advantage (though I am out of position post-flop) to navigate the hand.

On this Ace-Queen high board, I have completely missed with my King-high. This board favors Minni's pre-flop raising range significantly. As the Big Blind, I will check to the aggressor and see how they react. I do not have enough equity to lead out or call a significant bet.

Heads‑up on the button I was the preflop aggressor; Flash checked the flop, which is a sign of weakness or pot‑control. The flop Ad Qd 4s is hostile to a wide calling range — many hands in Flash’s defending range (Ax, Qx, diamonds, pocket pairs) connect well — and my hand (Ts6d) has no made pair and only a backdoor diamond (needs both turn+river diamonds) and a few low pair outs. With position and initiative I want to apply pressure but not bloat the pot with a marginal hand, so a small continuation bet is the right play: about one‑third pot to fold out unpaired broadways and weak Kx/Jx hands while keeping the pot manageable if called. Plan: bet 150; fold to a large raise (he’s likely strong), and if called proceed cautiously on the turn — check/fold unless I improve.

On an Ace-Queen high board, my King-high has very little equity against Minni's range, especially since she was the pre-flop aggressor. Even with favorable pot odds of nearly 4:1, calling with no draw and no pair on such a coordinated board is unlikely to be profitable out of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am holding 8-3 offsuit on the button. This is one of the weakest hands in the game. Although I am in position, the hand lacks connectivity, suit, and high-card value. I have a healthy chip lead over Minni, and there is no reason to play junk hands and potentially gift chips back. Folding is the most disciplined and standard play here.

Heads-up on the button I have positional advantage postflop and should open a wide range. T♥6♦ (Ten-six offsuit) is playable in this spot — I want fold equity and to take the initiative rather than limp. A standard 3x open to 300 (small relative to our ~163bb stack) denies the BB good odds to see a cheap flop, keeps the pot manageable for postflop play, and lets me leverage position. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold and preserve chips; if he calls I can play the flop in position and extract value or apply pressure.

In this heads-up situation, I'm holding King-Trey offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind (300) from the button. While a King has decent raw equity in heads-up play, K3o is quite weak and plays poorly out of position. Given that I have a comfortable chip lead and have recently lost several medium-sized pots by calling down with marginal hands like K5s and J8h, I will choose to fold here. I'd rather wait for a hand with better post-flop playability or a stronger King to defend my blind against a 3x raise.
I have AKo, which is a premium starting hand in heads-up play. As the button, I want to take control of the hand and build the pot early. A standard raise to 300 is appropriate here.

Heads-up in the big blind vs a button open to 300 with 7s6d. This is a marginal but playable hand in heads-up — decent connectivity and good postflop equity — and my stacks are deep (~160bb effective), so defending is standard. Calling gives ~2:1 pot odds (risk 200 to win ~400) and allows me to realize equity out of position; 3-betting OOP with 7-6 offsuit is too loose and folding is overly tight heads-up. I’ll call 300 total to see a flop and proceed cautiously: continue with pairs, strong draws or good turn equity, and control the pot or fold to heavy pressure on dry high-card boards.

I missed this dry, high-card flop (7♠6♦ on A♦ J♣ 8♥). I have no pair and essentially no realistic draw, while the raiser’s opening range contains a lot of A/x and Jx that dominate me. Out of position with deep stacks, I want pot control and to avoid getting blown off the hand by a standard c-bet. I’ll check to keep the pot small, plan to fold to a normal/large continuation bet (> ~1/2 pot), call only very small probes (≤ ~1/3 pot) to see a cheap turn, and only pursue a delayed bluff or semi‑bluff if the turn gives real equity (e.g. a 9 or 5 that creates an OESD) or the opponent shows weakness.

I have flopped top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a dry Ace-high board. Since Minni checked to me, I should lead with a value bet. I want to extract value from weaker Aces, Jacks, and potential gutshot straight draws. A bet of 400 into a 600-chip pot is a standard sizing to build the pot while keeping her range in.

Heads‑up with deep stacks — defending 7♠6♦ preflop was fine. The flop A♦ J♣ 8♥ misses me and Flash’s c‑bet of 400 into a 1000 pot (~40%) is consistent with a lot of Ax/Jx and some small-frequency bluffs. Calling 400 is cheap relative to my 16k stack and keeps the pot manageable while allowing me to realize equity: a 9 or 5 on the turn gives an OESD and a 6/7 pairs me. I’ll call to see the turn and plan to fold to significant turn aggression unless I pick up a real improvement or see clear weakness (or a very small turn probe). I won’t check‑raise or lead here with such a weak hand OOP.

Check. On A♦ J♣ 8♥ Q♦ with 7♠6♦ I have nothing and no realistic draw — with only one diamond in my hand and two on board the best I can make by the river is four diamonds at most, and a straight is impossible with one card left. Flash’s line (preflop raise, flop c‑bet) is consistent with A/x, J/x, Qx or some medium-strength hands; checking the turn is standard. Bluffing here has very poor fold equity against that range and will get called or raised by most hands that beat me. With deep stacks and a small pot, the correct line is pot control and to conserve chips: check and fold to meaningful aggression, re-evaluating only on a river that actually improves me.

I have top pair with the best possible kicker (TPTK) on a board of 8h-Jc-Ad-Qd. While the Queen is a card that could have improved Minni's range (specifically hands like AQ, QJ, or a gutshot like KT), I still likely hold the best hand against her range of weaker Aces and Jacks. I will bet 900 into the 1400-chip pot to continue extracting value from those weaker pairs and to charge any diamond flush draws or straight draws that may have picked up equity on the turn. Checking back is an option to control the pot, but TPTK is generally too strong to check here in a heads-up match.

Pot is 2300 and I must call 900. That requires ~28% equity to break even (900/(2300+900) ≈ 28%). With 7♠6♦ on A♦ J♣ 8♥ Q♦ I have essentially no real draws — only 6 raw outs to make a single pair (~6/46 ≈ 13%), and many of those outs are poor because Flash’s line (PF raise, flop c‑bet, turn aggression) heavily favors Ax/Jx/Qx or other strong made hands that still beat a lone 7 or 6. I have no blockers to credible bluffs and raising as a bluff OOP here is unlikely to succeed. Given the pot odds, my actual equity, and the opponent’s range, folding and preserving a healthy stack is the correct play.

Heads-up on the button (SB) I should be opening a very wide range. 7♣6♦ is a playable, connected hand with decent postflop playability and blockers — good for stealing the BB or seeing a cheap flop in position. Pot is 150 and it costs me 50 to complete; calling would be 100 total, but I want fold equity and to take the initiative. I'll make a small but standard heads‑up open: raise to 200 total (i.e. add 150 more chips). If Flash folds I win the 150; if he calls I can play postflop in position; if he 3‑bets heavily I will concede this marginal hand rather than get entangled out of position.

I am holding 3s 4c, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Although the price to call is only 100 chips, playing such a hand out of position against Minni, who has been active and aggressive, is generally a losing strategy. With a comfortable chip lead and about 45 hands remaining in the tournament, there is no need to defend the big blind with garbage cards. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity.

In heads-up play, the button (Small Blind) should play a very wide range of hands to maintain pressure and utilize positional advantage. While 8 of clubs and 2 of clubs is a weak hand, it is suited, providing some post-flop playability. I will put in a standard 3x raise to 300 to attempt to win the blinds immediately or control the pot in position.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I hold A♥9♣ — a strong, but not monster, hand in this spot. With deep stacks and being out of position postflop, I prefer to keep the pot controllable and realize my equity rather than inflate the pot with a marginal kicker. Calling 200 more (total commitment 300) lets me see a flop cheaply against a wide opening range and play postflop for value when I pair my Ace or pick up draws; I can fold to heavy aggression on coordinated boards or seize initiative when I improve. A small 3-bet is an option later as part of a balanced strategy, but here a call is the lowest-variance, +EV line.

Heads-up on a dry T♠-3♦-6♣ flop with A♥9♣ out of position. I have Ace-high with no meaningful draw (flush is impossible with only one club on the flop), so my priority is pot control and realizing equity against the button’s wide opening range rather than bloating the pot with a marginal hand. Checking keeps the pot small, lets me call a standard continuation bet to see a turn, and preserves options to fold to heavy aggression or take the lead if I pair my Ace. With deep stacks I’ll call small-to-medium c-bets (~<=1/2 pot) and fold to large raises; if villain checks back I can take a free card or show down.

On a board of T-3-6 rainbow, I have missed completely with my 82 suited, having only backdoor flush and straight possibilities. Minni has checked to me. While this board is fairly dry, it hits many of the hands Minni would call a pre-flop raise with from the Big Blind (specifically hands with a Ten or mid-range connectors). However, as the pre-flop aggressor in position, a continuation bet (C-bet) is a standard play here to try and take the pot down immediately. I will bet 300 (half the pot) to test her strength.

Dry flop T♠-3♦-6♣, I have A♥9♣ — Ace-high with only a backdoor club. Villain opened from the button (wide) and fired a small c-bet (300 into ~900 ≈ 1/3 pot). This is the classic spot to control the pot out of position: calling realizes my showdown equity against a wide checking/CB range, keeps weaker hands and bluffs in, and preserves options on the turn. Raising OOP with a weak kicker bloats the pot unnecessarily and can get me into trouble vs better hands or a shove. Plan: call 300, fold to heavy turn aggression if I don’t improve, and extract value or raise only if I pair the Ace or pick up a strong draw.

Check. I have A♥9♣ — only Ace‑high with no realistic draw (the flop/turn texture is very dry and a club flush is impossible from my hand). Villain opened wide preflop and fired a small 300 c‑bet on the flop; calling was the pot‑control/realize‑equity line. The 7♦ on the turn doesn’t improve me and mainly helps a few 7x combos or small connectors; it doesn’t merit bloating the pot out of position. With deep stacks I’ll keep the pot under control: check to induce bluffs or get a free river to show down, call reasonable river bets, and fold to a large turn shove or overbet.

On a turn card of 7d, the board is now Ts-3d-6c-7d. Minni has checked to me for a second time after calling my flop continuation bet. While a second barrel could potentially force a fold from weaker pairs (6x, 3x, or small pocket pairs), Minni's flop call suggests she likely has a Ten or a drawing hand. With 8c 2c, I have zero showdown value and almost no equity to improve to a winning hand. Given the board is becoming more connected, a check-back is the most prudent play to control the size of the pot and see a free river card. If she checks again on the river, I can re-evaluate a potential bluff then.

Board: T♠-3♦-6♣-7♦-7♠. I hold A♥9♣ — Ace‑high with no pair or meaningful draw. Villain opened wide preflop and fired a small flop c‑bet that I called; both checked the turn, which shows caution/weakness. The river pairs the board and gives little additional fold equity for a bluff; betting here would risk getting called by any one‑pair hands and is a thin bluff versus this opponent. I’ll take the lowest‑variance line: check to showdown. If Flash bets a small amount I can call for value/control; I’ll fold to a large overbet/shove.

On this board of Ts-3d-6c-7d-7s, I have 8-high and no showdown value. Minni checked to me on the river after calling a flop bet and checking the turn. While her checks on the turn and river might suggest weakness, her call on the flop indicates she likely has a piece of the board, such as a Ten, a Six, or a small pocket pair. Given my check on the turn, my range doesn't strongly represent a Seven, and a river bluff is unlikely to force a fold from a Ten or even a Six on such a paired board. Since I cannot win at showdown, but the likelihood of a successful bluff is low against a player who has already shown interest in the pot, I will check back and concede the hand to minimize losses.

Heads-up on the button with 4♦2♦ — suited, low but fine in a heads-up opening range. Pot is 150 and I only need to call 50; however I want to take the initiative and apply pressure from position. With deep stacks (≈164 BB) I can realize equity post‑flop and play lots of flops profitably. I’ll raise to 3x the BB (total 300) — slightly above the minimum raise to gain more fold equity and avoid giving extremely cheap odds to a 3-bet. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll continuation-bet favorable/dry flops and play cautiously vs heavy aggression; fold to large 3-bets but consider calling small 3-bets with implied odds.

With Queen-Seven offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing a standard 3x raise from Minni on the button. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call (200 to win a total pot of 600). In a heads-up match, Q7o is well within the defending range. I have a significant chip lead and can afford to see a flop to see if I connect. I will call and evaluate the flop.

I have Queen high and a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5 to complete a 3-4-5-6-7 straight). On this low, coordinated flop, checking to the pre-flop raiser is the most standard and balanced play. I will evaluate my next move based on Minni's action and the sizing of any potential bet.

I have middle pair (4♦) on a 4♥–3♠–6♥ flop after raising pre‑flop and seeing a check from the BB. In heads‑up this is a strong holding — many of Flash’s calling hands (overcards, floats, gutshots) are behind and there are some heart/straight draws that I want to charge. I’ll take the initiative with a standard c‑bet of ~1/2 pot: 300 into 600. Size is big enough to get value and deny equity to draws while keeping the pot controllable with deep stacks. If he calls I’ll reassess on the turn (continue with safe cards, pot‑control or check behind vs aggression). If he raises substantially I’ll fold to heavy aggression (he likely has 6/4, a strong heart draw or a very strong hand); consider calling only small, noncommittal raises.

I have Queen-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a 5) on a low, coordinated board. Minni's 300 bet into a 600 pot provides 3-to-1 pot odds. Given my position in the Big Blind and the potential to improve on the turn with a 5, or even hitting a Queen, I have sufficient equity to continue. Folding now would be too conservative, and raising doesn't seem necessary yet as I can evaluate the turn card first.

The turn card is the Queen of Hearts, giving me top pair. While this card is excellent for my hand, it also brings a potential flush with three hearts now on the board. I will check to Minni, the pre-flop aggressor, to see how she reacts to this overcard. This allows me to control the pot size and catch any bluffs or semi-bluffs she might continue with, while also being cautious of a completed flush or a better Queen.

Turn brought Q♥, a scary overcard that also makes a three‑heart board (4♥–3♠–6♥–Q♥). I currently have a marginal made hand — middle pair (4♦) with a weak kicker — which is vulnerable to Qx, 6x/3x, and any two‑heart combos that already have a flush. Flash checked the turn after calling my flop c‑bet; that line is consistent with a wide calling range (overcards, single‑heart hands, one‑pair undercards, floats) but could also be a slowplayed strong hand. With deep stacks but a relatively small pot (1,200), I want to control the pot size and avoid getting check‑raised or committed when behind. I’ll check back to keep the pot manageable and to give myself the option to call a reasonable river bet or realize equity at showdown. Plan for river: if Flash checks I’ll go to showdown; if he bets small (≈≤1/3 pot, ~≤400) I’ll call to deny bluffs and get value vs worse pairs/floaters; if he bets medium‑large (>~1/2 pot, ~>600) I’ll fold unless the river pairs me or otherwise gives a clear improvement.

On a board with four hearts, any single heart in my opponent's hand makes a flush, which beats my top pair of Queens. Minni checked the turn when the third heart appeared, which could indicate she doesn't have a high heart or was playing a made flush slowly. However, with the fourth heart arriving on the river, the board is extremely dangerous. I have no heart in my hand. Checking is the most prudent action to reach a showdown as cheaply as possible. If I bet, I am unlikely to be called by a worse hand that doesn't have a heart, and I would be forced to fold to a raise. I will check and see if Minni checks behind or attempts a bet.

I raised preflop and c-bet the flop with 4♦2♦ and got a call; both of us then checked the Q♥ on the turn. I have a marginal one-pair hand (pair of 4s, weak kicker). The river 9♥ completes four hearts on board (4♥–3♠–6♥–Q♥–9♥), so any single heart in Flash’s hand gives him a flush. Flash checked the river — that makes a flush less likely but could be a slowplay. Given the heavy flush potential, the presence of many hands that beat a lone 4 (Qx, 6x, any heart), and the small pot (1,200) relative to our deep stacks, I want to control the pot and avoid getting raised off a marginal holding. A river bet is unlikely to fold enough better hands and risks a costly raise/call; checking gives me showdown and the option to call a small bet. Therefore I check to go to showdown.

I am on the button with 6s 5s, a strong suited connector that plays very well post-flop, especially in heads-up play with deep stacks. Raising is the standard play here to take control of the hand and build a pot. A raise to 300 follows my established sizing pattern.

Facing a button/SB open to 300 heads-up. I have Q♠9♦ in the big blind — a strong defending hand heads-up but out of position. Pot is 400 and I only need to call 200 (300 total committed) — ~2:1 pot odds to see a flop against a wide opening range. 3-betting OOP with Q9o is thin (min-3bet to 500 is possible but unnecessarily inflates the pot and gives the SB better position postflop), so I’ll defend by calling, see the flop, and proceed cautiously if I don’t improve.

I have Q♠9♦ on 4♣ 9♥ 2♠ — top pair with a good kicker in a heads-up pot. The flop is dry and unlikely to produce many strong draws; Flash opened a wide range from the button, so many of his hands are worse (overcards, weaker pairs, A‑highs) than my pair. Checking here surrenders initiative and gives free cards to hands that can improve, so I want to extract value and charge any backdoor outs. I’ll make a constructive half‑pot bet (300 into 600). Size is big enough to get value from worse hands and force marginal hands to pay to see turn, but small enough to keep weaker calls in. If he raises, I’ll give credit to a 9/two‑pair/set and re‑evaluate (likely fold to a large raise, call a small one); if he calls, I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and continue extracting when appropriate.

On the flop of 4c 9h 2s, I have 6s 5s, which gives me a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3) and two overcards to the 2 and 4. Minni has led out for 300 into a 600 pot. This 'donk bet' often represents a marginal piece of the board or a draw, but can also be a protective bet for a medium pair. My hand has decent equity with the gutshot and the potential to improve to a pair that might be good. Given the pot odds (300 to call for a total pot that will be 1200), I am getting 4:1, which is a fair price to see a turn card, especially with the potential to bluff later streets if the board texture changes or if I hit my draw.

Turn reasoning: I led the flop with a 300 bet and got called, so Flash’s calling range is wide — it includes 9x, 4x, underpairs, A/Kx, and occasional 2x or draws. The turn (2♣) pairs the board, which massively reduces my relative hand strength: I now have one pair (9s) with a Q kicker and am crushed by any 2x (sets) or any 9x (two pair). With the pot at 1,200 and plenty of villain hands that still will bluff or value-bet small, I want pot control and the ability to call reasonable bets while folding to heavy value lines. Checking preserves showdown equity, induces bluffs, and keeps the pot from bloating when I’m likely behind. Plan: check here, call small-to-medium turn bets (which often come from worse pairs or bluffs) and fold to large overbets or shoves that represent trips/two-pair. If he checks back, I go to the river and re-evaluate.

On the turn, the board pairs the 2, and Minni checks after leading out on the flop. This check is often a sign of weakness, suggesting she may have been bluffing the flop or is now in pot-control mode with a marginal hand like a 4 or A-high. While I have a gutshot straight draw (needing a 3), I currently have no showdown value with 6-high. By betting now, I can put pressure on her marginal holdings and take down the pot immediately. A bet of 700 into the 1200 pot represents a strong hand (like a 9 or even a slow-played 2) and should be enough to force a fold from her weaker range.

Turn paired the board (2♣) and I checked for pot control — my Q♠9♦ is now top pair but vulnerable to any 2x or a 9 with a better kicker. Flash’s 700 into the ~1,200 pot (pot now 1,900) is a medium/size bet that can be value (sets, two‑pair, 9x, 4x) or a bluff/merge (underpairs, A/K high). Calling 700 to win 1,900 gives ~2.7:1 pot odds (≈27–28% equity required), and versus his wide button range I have sufficient showdown equity to continue. Raising OOP with a medium‑kicker top pair is thin — it bloats the pot and gets me in trouble against strong lines — and folding surrenders too much. I’ll call the 700, keep the pot controlled, and re‑evaluate on the river: fold to a polarizing shove but call or value‑bet small against non‑committal lines.

I have Q♠9♦ (top pair, decent kicker) on 4♣ 9♥ 2♠ 2♣ 7♣. Villain (button) opened pre, called my flop lead and then bet the turn; he then checked the river — a fairly non‑committal line that includes a lot of worse hands (A/Kx, underpairs, some worse 9x) as well as the occasional strong hand (2x trips, two‑pair, or a club flush). Checking behind gives up value to the weaker part of his range; a large bet bloats the pot with a vulnerable one‑pair hand and invites jam/raise. I want a small value extraction that keeps worse hands in and folds out pure air, while allowing me to fold to a polarizing raise. I’ll bet ~30% of the pot (800) to get called by Ax/Kx/underpairs and some worse 9x; if he raises I’ll assume a polarized value range and fold.

I have 6-high and zero showdown value. The board is 4c 9h 2s 2c 7c. I attempted to take the pot away on the turn with a semi-bluff when Minni checked to me, but she called. Her decision to lead out on the river for 800 (roughly 30% of the pot) after check-calling the turn suggests she has a made hand, likely a 9, a small pair like 4x, or perhaps she improved to a pair of 7s. While I am getting good pot odds to call (4.25:1), calling is not an option as I cannot win the pot at showdown. A raise would be a pure bluff, and given Minni's 'stop-and-go' line and her tendency to call down in previous hands, it is unlikely she will fold a pair here. The most disciplined play is to fold and move on to the next hand.

Heads-up I’m on the button (small blind) with Kc2h. In heads-up play you open a very wide range from the SB to seize initiative and exploit the BB’s defending frequency — K2o is playable. Pot is 150 and the min-raise is to 200; I’ll make the standard 2x-style open (200 total) to gain fold equity, play the postflop game in position, and keep the sizing small relative to our deep stacks (I have ~171 BB). If Flash 3-bets big I’ll strongly consider folding K2o; if he just calls I’ll proceed to use position to realize equity and apply pressure.

I am in the Big Blind with Qs 4s and Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button. It costs me 100 more to call into a pot of 300, giving me 3:1 odds. While Q4s is not a premium hand, it has decent playability in a heads-up situation, especially with the suitedness allowing for flush possibilities. Folding would be too tight given the price and the fact that we are heads-up. Calling allows me to see a flop and realize my equity.

I have flopped a flush draw with Queen-high. Being out of position as the Big Blind against the pre-flop aggressor, the standard play is to check and see how Minni acts. I have significant equity with the draw and can comfortably call a standard continuation bet or potentially check-raise to apply pressure.

I have Kc2h on Ts 2s Kd — I hold two pair (Kings and Deuces), a very strong hand heads-up. Flash checked; his range includes Kx (single pair), Tx (trips), pocket pairs, and plenty of spade combos with a flush draw. I should bet for value and protection: charge spade draws and get called by worse Kx/Tx and weaker pairs while keeping the pot manageable with deep stacks. I choose a bet around 60% of the pot (250) — big enough to deny free cards to flush draws and extract value, but not so large that only sets/trips continue. Plan: call small raises and re-evaluate on the turn; respect a large check-raise (likely trips/set or a strong disguised hand).

I have flopped a flush draw with Queen-high on a King-high board. Minni's bet of 250 into 400 is a standard continuation bet. With nine outs to a flush and potentially an overcard to some of her range, I have enough equity to call. I'll play this passively for now to control the pot size while out of position and see if I can improve on the turn.

On the turn, the Nine of Spades completes my flush (Queen-high). Since I am out of position against Minni, who has been the aggressor pre-flop and on the flop, the best play here is to check. This allows Minni to continue her aggression with her made hands (like a King or a Ten) or bluffs, giving me the opportunity to check-raise for value. If she checks back, I can still value bet most river cards.

I have Kc2h on Ts 2s Kd 9s — two pair (Kings and Deuces) heads-up, which is a very strong made hand. Flash checked the turn after calling my flop bet; his range contains worse kings, single-pair Tens, 9x, medium pairs, some pocket pairs (TT/99) and a limited number of completed spade flushes (only combos with two spades). I want to extract value and deny equity to runner-runner/paired cards, so betting is correct. I size to ~2/3 pot (600) to charge Kx/T x/9x and to make draws pay, while keeping room to fold or re-evaluate if he check-raises big (which would indicate sets/trips or a disguised flush). If he just calls I plan to re-evaluate on the river versus his range.

I have successfully completed a Queen-high flush on the turn. My previous strategy of checking to the aggressor worked, as Minni has fired a second barrel of 600 into the 900 pot. While the Ace-high flush is a possibility, a Queen-high flush in heads-up play is a very strong hand that warrants a raise for value. I want to build the pot now in case the board pairs or a fourth spade falls on the river, which could kill the action or lead to a more difficult decision. A raise to 1800 (a 3x raise of her bet) is a standard value sizing that can get called by sets, two-pair, or even a strong King with the Ace of Spades.

Board Ts 2s Kd 9s — I have two pair (K2), a very strong heads-up hand but vulnerable to sets (TT, 99) and any completed spade flush (two spades in villain's hand). Flash called my flop bet and then check‑raised my turn big to 1800, which polarizes his range toward strong made hands (sets/trips, K9 two‑pair, or a spade flush) but can also include some turn‑converted bluffs (spade combos that now overbet or other creative plays). Folding two pair here is too weak given the cheap price to continue and my position/stack depth. A shove or large reraise would commit a lot of chips when I’m often behind vs his value range, so I prefer to call, control the pot, and evaluate the river. I’ll call the 1200 now to see the river and then fold to an overbearing line if the river completes obvious crush cards (spade/club that pairs the board in a way that strongly favors his line) or call/raise for value if the river is safe and his sizing is weak.

I have a Queen-high flush on a board of Ts-2s-Kd-9s-Jc. The only hand that realistically beats me in this heads-up scenario is the Ace-high flush (As). After I check-raised the turn and Minni called, the pot has grown to 4500. On the river, the Jack of Clubs is a relatively safe card, though it does complete potential straights. However, since I hold a flush, I am mostly concerned with whether Minni has the Ace of Spades. Given the action, she likely has a strong hand like a straight, a set, or two-pair. If I check, she might check back her marginal value hands to see a cheap showdown. By betting, I can extract value from those hands. A bet of 2200 (approximately half the pot) is a standard value-seeking sizing that remains affordable enough for her to call with a King, a straight, or a set.

I will CALL the 2200. Reasoning: On the turn Flash’s big check‑raise polarized his range toward strong value (sets/trips, spade flushes, and two‑pair like K9/KT), so I’m concerned, but I still hold a very respectable showdown hand heads‑up: two pair (K2). The river Jc is a relatively “safe” card — it doesn’t complete additional spade draws and doesn’t produce many new hands that dominate me. Flash’s river sizing (2200 into a 6700 pot, ~33%) is relatively small and looks like a thin value/induce sizing that will get called by worse Kx or some medium pairs; it also gives me decent pot odds. I need ~24.7% equity to justify a call (2200 to win ~8900), and two pair should have that or better against a polarized turn check‑raise range that contains some bluffs/medium hands. Raising would be committing chips when I’m often behind to flushes/trips and folding surrenders too much equity, so I’ll call and see showdown.

I am on the button with Qd-7h. This is a mediocre hand, but in a heads-up game, it's generally strong enough to open or call a small blind. The pot is currently 150 (50/100 blinds) and it costs me 50 to call. However, standard professional strategy in heads-up play suggests opening with a raise to put pressure on the big blind or folding if the hand is completely unplayable. Q7o is above the median hand strength for heads-up play. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and potentially win it pre-flop, consistent with my previous aggressive playstyle.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300 from the SB. I have A♥2♦ in the big blind — a playable ace with decent equity and useful blocker value against a wide opening range. Pot is 400 and calling requires 200 more (300 total commitment). With deep stacks I prefer to keep the pot controllable and see a flop out of position rather than make a small 3-bet (min-raise to 500) which would inflate the pot with a marginal kicker and create difficult OOP spots. Folding is too weak given Ace-high. I will call and evaluate on the flop.

Heads-up vs a wide opening range. I have A♥2♦ — ace-high with a useful blocker but no pair. The flop Q♠9♦6♣ is dry and misses a lot of the opener’s range, so I have good bluffing/fold-equity here. Pot is 600 and stacks are deep, so I want to apply pressure without inflating the pot too much: a half-pot c-bet targets many Jx/8x/7x/air hands and folds out weak pairs. Plan: bet ~50% pot (300). If he folds I take it down; if he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn and fold to large raises unless I improve or pick up clear equity.

In this heads-up scenario, I have flopped top pair with a mediocre kicker (Q7o on a Q96 rainbow board). Minni has decided to lead out (donk bet) for 300 into a 600 pot. In heads-up play, top pair is a very strong holding. Minni's lead could represent a wide range of hands: a weaker Queen, a Nine, a straight draw (like JT or 87), or even a pure bluff. Raising here might force out her bluffs or weaker hands that I currently beat. By calling, I keep the pot manageable, maintain the strength of my hand relative to her range, and allow her to continue betting with worse hands or draws on the turn. I have a significant chip lead and do not need to overplay a marginal top-pair hand at this stage.

I c-bet the flop 300 and was called — I hold A♥2♦, which is only ace-high and the turn 5♦ does not improve me. The caller’s range after a flop-call is weighted toward Qx/9x and medium pairs; 5♦ mainly brings rare 7-8 straights and a backdoor diamond possibility I can’t realistically make. Out of position with marginal showdown value, I want to control the pot and avoid building it with a weak hand. I’ll check to keep the pot manageable, fold to large aggression, and reevaluate on the river (calling small bets or bluff-catching only when appropriate).

On the turn, the 5d is a relatively blank card, though it does complete a possible straight for 87. Minni checked after leading out on the flop, which often indicates a realization that her flop bet didn't force a fold or she is pot-controlling a marginal hand like a Nine. While I have top pair, my kicker (7) is weak, and betting here risks a check-raise from a stronger Queen or a completed straight. By checking back, I control the size of the pot, maintain my lead, and can more comfortably evaluate the situation on the river. This line keeps her range wide and allows me to reach showdown or catch a bluff on the final street.

Check to take it to showdown. I have A♥2♦ on Q♠9♦6♣5♦3♥. I c‑bet the flop and was called, then checked the turn and my opponent checked back — that line signals weakness or medium strength (Qx/9x/pocket pairs) and the 3♥ river changes very little. With only ace‑high I have almost no value-bet targets and a bluff now would be thin: many caller hands (Qx/9x/medium pairs) will call, while the hands I can fold out (Kx, Tx, air) aren’t worth risking more chips. Pot is small relative to stacks, so preserving chips and taking the showdown is the highest EV line. I’ll check and see a showdown rather than bluffing the river.

I have top pair with a 7 kicker. The board is relatively disconnected, and most potential draws like 87 or 74 failed to materialize. Minni led out on the flop but has checked both the turn and the river. This line typically indicates a weak hand or a middle pair (like a 9 or 6) that is looking for a cheap showdown. While my kicker is weak, it is highly likely that I have the best hand given her passivity on the last two streets. A small value bet of 600 (half the pot) is designed to get called by a 9, a 6, or a pocket pair like 88, while allowing me to fold without too much loss if she were to surprisingly check-raise.

River decision: board Q♠ 9♦ 6♣ 5♦ 3♥, I hold A♥2♦. I checked the turn intending to take this to showdown; Flash now bets 600 into a 1800 pot (1/3 pot). Calling 600 gives me pot odds of 600/(1800+600)=25% — I only need ~25% equity to justify a call. Villain’s line (call flop, check turn, small river bet) is consistent with a lot of thin value hands (Qx/9x/middle pairs/occasionally a made straight) and some missed-draw bluffs. My Ace blocks a portion of Ax bluffs and A2 has reasonable showdown value heads-up. Raising would be a thin, high-variance bluff with poor fold equity against the sorts of hands that call flop; folding would be too tight given the cheap price. Therefore I will call 600 to realize equity and shop for showdown.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the small blind) with J♥T♥ and deep stacks (~115BB effective). This is a very playable, high-equity heads-up hand that works well in position. Limping/calling 50 is too passive — I want the initiative and some fold equity. I’ll raise to a standard 3x BB (300 total) to: 1) seize position and build the pot with a hand that plays well postflop, 2) deny free or cheap flops to weak holdings in the big blind, and 3) isolate Flash whose range has shown a lot of activity (wide and aggressive at times). Sizing rationale: 300 is big enough to get folds and discourage multiway action but small enough relative to my 114BB stack to leave flexible postflop options. Plan if called: use position to c-bet favourable flops, float and apply pressure on turns when appropriate, and control pot size with marginal hands. Plan vs 3-bet: call small-to-medium 3-bets to see a flop in position; re-evaluate or fold to very large 3-bets depending on sizing and opponent tendencies.

I am holding 3d 7s, which is an extremely weak hand with very poor playability, especially out of position. While I have a significant chip lead and only need to call 200 more, there is no reason to defend such a wide range with trash hands. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage against Minni.

In a heads-up situation on the button, I have a significant chip lead and want to maintain pressure. Although 9-3 suited is a weak starting hand, raising to 300 is a standard opening play to try and take down the blinds or define the hand early against the short stack.

Flash opened to 300 from the button (SB). Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more to call. I hold A♥2♥ — a suited ace heads‑up, which has good equity and playability but is marginal for a 3‑bet OOP. With ~115bb effective and being out of position postflop, I prefer to call and keep the pot manageable: I can realize equity with the nut ace, backdoor hearts and wheel combos, and apply pressure on favorable flops. I’ll fold to large 4‑bets, and play aggressively (c‑bet/raise) when the flop favors me or pot‑control when it doesn’t. Action: call the 300 total.

Flop: 6♠6♣Q♥. I hold A♥2♥ — that’s effectively the board pair (pair of 6s) with the best possible kicker (A) and a very remote backdoor heart flush (need both turn+river hearts). Heads‑up the preflop raiser’s range includes a lot of Qx (which beats me), some pocket pairs (including 66 or 6x that make trips) and lots of hands I currently beat. Out of position and with deep stacks, I don’t want to inflate the pot with a marginal hand that is often behind vs the raiser’s value hands. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me call a reasonable bet to realize equity (and keep his bluffs in), and preserves options on the turn: call small/medium bets, fold to extremely large raises that represent strong Qx/trips, and on a favorable turn (A or heart) can start betting for value or raise. Plan: check and reassess on villain action.

On a paired board like 6-6-Q, the pre-flop aggressor has a significant range advantage. Since Minni checked, a continuation bet is the most effective way to take down the pot, as it's unlikely she connected with this specific texture. I have 9-high with very little showdown value, so I want to end the hand here.

Board 6♠-6♣-Q♥, I hold A♥2♥. Villain (button) bet 300 into the ~600 pot (pot now 900); I must call 300. I have only Ace-high (no pair) but I block some strong Qx combos (AQ) and have a very small backdoor heart/straight potential. Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)), which is reasonable vs a button c-bet range that includes Qx, underpairs/sets and a fair number of bluffs/overcards. Folding is too passive — I’m getting decent pot odds and I keep bluffs in. Check‑raising as a bluff is thin: most value hands (Qx, 66) won’t fold and I have poor fold equity OOP. Plan: call the flop to see the turn and re-evaluate — if villain barrels very large on a blank I’ll fold; if the turn brings an Ace or a heart I can become more aggressive; if villain checks I can consider a delayed c‑bet to take the pot. Action: call 300.

Check. With A♥2♥ on 6♠6♣Q♥J♠ I have only Ace‑high (no pair) and only a very remote backdoor heart flush — the turn jack didn’t improve me. Flash’s range that c‑bet the flop will contain a lot of Qx, some pocket pairs and some bluffs; out of position I have poor fold equity and betting as a bluff is thin. Checking keeps the pot controlled and lets me realize my equity vs bluffs while preserving the option to call a reasonable river bet. Plan: check and see his action — call small/medium bets on later streets (I beat many bluffs), but fold to a large polarizing barrel that strongly represents two‑pair/set/Qx/Jx.

On this turn, the Jack of Spades (Js) doesn't significantly change the board texture, but it does potentially connect with some of Minni's flop-calling range (like AJ, JT, or T9). Having zero equity with 9-high and no draws, I have to decide between a second barrel or checking behind. Given that Minni called the flop c-bet, she likely has a piece of the board (a Queen or a pocket pair) or an Ace-high float. Since I am the chip leader, I don't need to force a bluff on this specific street after being called. I will check behind to see a free river and potentially evaluate a bluffing opportunity then, or simply concede the pot if she leads.

Heads‑up on the river (board 6♠ 6♣ Q♥ J♠ K♠). I hold A♥2♥ — only Ace‑high, no pair and basically no showdown value vs a realistic c‑betting preflop raiser. Flash c‑bet the flop then checked the turn, which is consistent with a lot of medium‑strength hands (Qx, underpairs, occasional bluffs). The river K is a scary card for some of those hands but he still didn’t bet it — meaning a fair portion of his range is probably weak or pot‑controlling. Pot = 1,200. A half‑pot bluff of 600 has a ~33% breakeven fold frequency (600/(1200+600)), which is comfortably low — I expect Flash to fold at least that often from his turn‑check range. I also block some A‑combos (reducing some strong Q/A combos). Plan: apply pressure with a half‑pot river bluff to pick up the pot now. If he raises I have virtually no value and will fold to a raise. Action: bet 600.

On a board of 6-6-Q-J-K, I have 9-high with absolutely no showdown value. Minni called my pre-flop raise and my flop continuation bet, then led out for 600 on the river after we both checked the turn. Her lead into the pre-flop aggressor suggests she has at least a pair or better, or perhaps she's turning a missed draw into a bluff. Regardless, I cannot win by calling. While I could attempt a raise to represent a slow-played 6 or a straight, the board is too connected and likely hit her range. Given my significant chip lead, it's better to fold and wait for a more advantageous spot than to risk chips on a pure bluff against a player who has shown interest in the pot.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with A♠3♠ — a very strong heads-up hand: nut ace, good blocker value and suited for flush equity. With only two players and deep stacks, I want initiative and position postflop, so I’ll open rather than just completing. A 3x BB open (raise to 300 total) is a standard, constructive size: it builds the pot with a hand that plays well in position, applies pressure to weaker holdings, and keeps stronger hands from getting good odds to see a cheap flop. If Flash calls I’ll c-bet favorable flops (A-high, spade-heavy boards) and be ready to control pot size on coordinated boards; if he 3-bets large I’ll respect the action and fold or proceed cautiously depending on sizing (I won’t commit a large portion of my stack without clear equity).

9h 6s is a weak hand to defend from the big blind against a 3x open raise, especially out of position. While I have a significant chip lead, I prefer to avoid marginal situations with low-equity hands and wait for better spots to pressure Minni's remaining stack.

Holding Jack-Five offsuit on the button in a heads-up situation, I will put in a standard raise to apply pressure on the big blind. This hand is strong enough to open in this position to pick up the blinds or take control of the pot early, especially given my chip lead.

Heads-up vs a button open to 300. I hold K♦8♣ in the big blind — a playable hand against a wide opening range. I only need to add 200 more (300 total committed) for good pot odds and I prefer to keep the pot manageable with deep stacks and play the flop OOP rather than 3-bet a marginal hand. A min‑raise would bloat the pot and won’t fold out many of Flash’s wide opens; calling preserves fold equity on many flops and lets me proceed based on the texture (bet/protect top pair or fold to significant aggression). Call 300 to see the flop and evaluate further.

Heads‑up vs a wide SB open; I have top pair (Kd) with a weak 8‑kicker on K♠‑5♣‑J♣. The board has two clubs so there are club draws in the opponent’s range and many of Flash’s holdings (Jx, Qx, Kx, club draws) will continue if given free/cheap cards. I want value and protection — charge club draws and weaker Kx/Qx while getting called by worse hands. A 2/3‑pot bet (~400 into 600) is the right size: it prices drawing hands up, extracts from worse kings/queens/attempted floats, and keeps the pot manageable vs deeper stacks. Plan: if called I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn; if I face a large raise I’ll fold or proceed very cautiously since my kicker is weak.

I have flopped two pair (Jacks and Fives) on a board of Ks-Jc-5c. My opponent has led out with a donk bet of 400 into the 600 pot. While this could represent a King, I am currently beating all single-pair hands. Given the presence of flush and straight draws (clubs, QT), I should raise for value and protection to capitalize on my strong holding while the board is still relatively safe.

Board: Ks 5c Jc. I hold Kd8c — top pair but a very weak kicker and only a backdoor club (needs running clubs). I sized the flop to 400 to charge club draws and worse Kx; Flash’s raise to 1100 polarizes him to value (K with a better kicker, sets like JJ/55, very strong two-card club combos) and semi-bluffs (two-club draws/overcards). Pot is 2100 and I must call 700 more (1100 total) — about 25% equity required. Folding is too nitty given the pot odds and heads‑up dynamics, and raising OOP with a poor kicker would bloat the pot and commit me to awkward decisions against a deeper, aggressive opponent. I’ll call 700 more (1100 total) to see the turn and re-evaluate: if I face heavy turn aggression without improving I will often fold; if I improve or villain shows weakness I’ll extract value.

Turn: Ks 5c Jc 6c. I have K♦8♣ — top pair with a very weak kicker and only one club in hand. Flash polarised the flop by raising my bet to 1,100, so his range contains strong value (better Kx, KJ two‑pair, sets) and club-based semi-bluffs. The turn completing a third club massively increases the likelihood that his raising range now contains made flushes. Betting as first actor here would bloat the pot with a hand that is frequently behind (sets/two‑pair/flush) and will get called by worse Ks and Qx; it also turns weak kickers into expensive mistakes if he raises. I prefer pot control and information — check and let Flash act. If he bets a small/medium size I can call to see the river; if he barrels large I will fold most of the time given my poor kicker and the completed club texture. If he checks back I can evaluate the river and extract thin value only on safe runouts.

On the turn, the 6 of clubs completes a possible flush. While I have two pair (Jacks and Fives), the board has become significantly more dangerous. Minni called my raise on the flop, which indicates a strong range like a King, a Jack, or a flush draw. By checking here, I control the pot size and avoid a potential check-raise if Minni has completed the flush. This allows me to see a river for free and decide how to proceed based on the final card and Minni's action, especially since my two pair is no longer the likely best hand if the betting continues heavily.

The river (Ks 5c Jc 6c 7c) completed my club flush (8♣ + 5♣ J♣ 6♣ 7♣), but it is a vulnerable, non‑nut flush. Any club 9 or higher (9♣, T♣, Q♣, K♣, A♣) in Flash’s hand beats me — that’s a meaningful fraction of his two‑card combos, and his preflop open + flop check‑raise makes club/high‑club holdings more likely than a random hand. Betting first here would either fold out almost all worse hands (so little value) or get called/raised by better clubs and force me into a tough spot. With both of us checking the turn, I’ll exercise pot control and avoid bloating the pot with a second‑best flush. Check and take it to showdown (and call a small river bet; fold/cautiously respond to a large raise).

The board now has four clubs, meaning any single club in my opponent's hand beats my two pair (Jacks and Fives). Minni checked on the turn when the third club arrived and checked again on the river with the fourth club. While it's possible she is trapping with a flush, it is more likely she has a hand like a King or a Jack without a club and is afraid of the board. Since I have decent showdown value against her non-club range but almost no chance of being called by a worse hand if I bet, checking back is the most prudent play to see the showdown.

I'm on the button (small blind) with Ad6h — a playable Ace heads-up hand. Pot is 150 and calling to 100 surrenders initiative and fold equity; folding wastes the 50 SB. I will raise to 300 (my usual 3x BB open) to take the initiative, get value from worse Aces and broadways, and extract fold equity from weaker hands. The size is consistent with table dynamics and preserves postflop playability with position against a wide-calling opponent.
In heads-up play, 65o is a standard defense from the Big Blind against a 3x button raise. I am getting 2:1 pot odds to see a flop and have a significant stack lead to navigate post-flop.

On a board of Jd-Kh-Ks, the texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor's range. Holding 6-high with no real draws or showdown value, I will check to the raiser to see how they react. Leading out on this board is rarely productive for the Big Blind.

I opened to 300 preflop from the button with Ad6h and the BB called; flop is J♦ K♥ K♠ (pot ~600) and Flash checks. I have only Ace‑high with no real draw, so I’m not going to build a large pot with no pair. Heads‑up the preflop raiser should c‑bet frequently, but this paired high board hits the caller’s range (many Kx) — so frequency should be tempered and sizing kept small. A small continuation bet (~1/3 pot) achieves two things: folds out pure air/overcards and gains value from Jx or worse A‑x that will call, while keeping the pot manageable if I face resistance. Plan: bet 200; if Flash folds I take the pot, if he calls I proceed cautiously on the turn, and if he check‑raises big I’ll fold (I’m beaten often).

On a paired board of Jd-Kh-Ks, my 6-high has no potential to improve to a winning hand and no equity against any part of Minni's betting range. While the bet size is small (1/4 pot), I have no reason to continue with a hand that has zero showdown value and no draws. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my stack advantage.

I have King-Nine of spades on the button. This is a strong starting hand in a heads-up situation. I will follow my standard opening strategy and raise to 3 big blinds (300 total) to exert pressure and build the pot with a hand that has good post-flop playability and equity.

Call to 300. A♥9♥ is a strong heads-up defending hand — cheap to see a flop vs a single opener. I only need to put in 200 more to call into a 400 pot (pot will be ~600 after my call), so I get very favorable pot odds (~3:1) and decent equity against a wide button opening range. I prefer a flat call here because I’m out of position postflop (button acts last), so a small 3-bet would gain little fold equity and commit more of my deep stack unnecessarily. Plan: see flop and play based on texture — continue aggressively on A/heart/connected boards, check-call or pot-control on dry boards, and fold to extreme aggression when I miss and face heavy pressure.

Check — pot control and realize equity OOP. I called preflop with A♥9♥ to see a flop versus a wide button opener; on Q♠ J♦ 3♦ I have no pair but top-card Ace and a backdoor heart/runner-runner equity. The board is relatively dry (two diamonds are the only suit draw) and Flash will c-bet often, so checking keeps the pot manageable and lets me call reasonable c-bets rather than inflate the pot out of position with a marginal hand. Plan: call small-to-medium c-bets (~≤1/2 pot) and fold to very large, polarizing pressure; if I pair the Ace, pick up a heart, or see clear weakness on the turn I'll convert to value/raise. For now I check (0).

On a board of Qs-Jd-3d, I have King-high with a gutshot straight draw (a Ten makes a straight) and a backdoor spade flush draw. Minni checked, which is standard for the Big Blind. This is a good texture to continuation bet as I have some equity and can represent a wide range of hands that connected with the Queen or Jack. I will bet 300 into the 600 pot to try and take it down immediately or build the pot for when I potentially hit my straight or improve to a pair of Kings.

Call 300. I checked the flop to keep the pot under control OOP with A♥9♥; Flash’s small c‑bet (~1/3 pot) on Q♠ J♦ 3♦ is exactly the type of bet I can call profitably. Calling 300 into the 900 pot gives roughly 3:1 pot odds (≈25% equity needed) — A9♥ has meaningful showdown value plus backdoor heart/runner equity against a wide button opening/c-betting range. Raising OOP here would bloat the pot with a marginal hand and surrender flexibility; folding is too tight given the price. Plan: call and re-evaluate on the turn — if I pair the Ace or pick up a heart I can apply pressure for value; if the turn is a heavy improvement for villain’s range or he fires a large polarized bet, I’ll fold.

I have A♥9♥ on 3♦ Q♠ J♦ A♦ — I’ve paired the Ace, giving me top pair but with a weak kicker. Flash is a button raiser who c‑bet the flop small, so his range is wide (Qx/Jx, Ax, Kx, suited connectors and some diamond combos). The turn Ace both helps me (I now beat most Qx/Jx and bluffs) and creates a three‑diamond board, so two‑diamond holdings in his range now have a made flush and some Ax combos (AQ/AJ) now beat me. Betting is the right line to extract value from Qx/Jx and charge single‑diamond and straight draws while keeping the pot manageable; checking gives him a free river to improve. I’ll bet about half the pot (≈600). If he raises big I’ll assume a very strong hand (two pair/sets/flush) and fold; if he calls we proceed to the river and re‑evaluate.

I raised pre-flop and continuation bet the flop with King-high and a gutshot straight draw. The turn Ace of Diamonds is a highly coordinated card that completes potential diamond flushes and likely hits the Big Blind's range (Aces). When Minni leads into me (the aggressor) on this scare card, she is representing significant strength. I only have King-high and a gutshot (4 outs to a straight), and even hitting a Ten might not be enough if a flush is present. Given the pot odds and my lack of equity against a range that leads here, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my chip lead.

Heads-up on the button with 7♥6♣ — a playable, connected hand that performs well in position. I want initiative and fold equity versus Flash’s wide big-blind defending range, and with deep stacks (~146bb) I can maneuver post-flop. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x BB): this builds the pot while keeping room to play post-flop in position. If Flash 3-bets large I can fold; if he calls I can pressure and realize equity on connected flops.

I am holding pocket Jacks, which is a premium hand in a heads-up situation. Minni has been quite active with button raises, and with JJ, I have a clear value-raising opportunity. I will 3-bet to 1,000 to build the pot and take the initiative in this hand.

I opened to 300 on the button with 7♥6♣ — a speculative, connected hand that plays very well in position with deep stacks (~143bb). Flash 3-bet to 1000; folding is too nitty (giving up position and equity), while 4-betting as a bluff with 76o is too big a risk versus his wide but often strong 3-bet/5-bet continuing ranges. Calling 700 more (1000 total committed) gives ~35% immediate pot odds, keeps the pot controllable, and lets me realize equity and apply positional pressure post-flop. Plan: see a flop in position, continue aggressively on favorable textures (connected/low boards or when I pick up a pair/draw), and fold to heavy action on dominated Ace-high boards or obvious strength.

I have pocket Jacks on a 9-8-10 board, giving me an overpair and an open-ended straight draw. This is a very strong position, although the board is highly coordinated. I need to bet here for value against top pairs and to protect against various draws that Minni might be holding. A bet of 1,200 (60% of the pot) is a standard sizing to maintain initiative and build the pot.

Flop: 9♥-8♦-10♣. I hold 7♥6♣ — I’ve already got a made 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-10), which is extremely strong on this texture. The only realistic hands that currently beat me are very specific two-card combos that make a higher straight (e.g. J7 or QJ), and those combos are a small portion of a preflop 3-bettor’s range. Flash’s line (3-bet preflop then a relatively small 1.2k c-bet into a ~3.2k pot) looks like a wide continuation/probe — many of his continuing hands (AQ, KQ, KJ, top pairs, small pairs) are behind my straight. I want to extract value and deny free cards that could pair the board or complete awkward backdoor draws, so I’ll raise for value. I choose the minimum legal raise to 2400 total commitment — big enough to charge worse hands and get called by hands I beat, while keeping the pot manageable against the rare combinations that dominate me. If Flash jams or raises big, I’ll re-evaluate (likely call most shoves given how small the combo count that actually beats me is); if he calls I’ll continue to extract on turns that are safe and re-check on J/Q turns that could produce higher straights.

I hold pocket Jacks on a highly coordinated 10-9-8 board, providing me with an overpair and an open-ended straight draw (any 7 or Queen). My 3-bet pre-flop likely narrowed Minni's range, but hands like 10-10, 9-9, or 8-8 are still possible, as are two-pair combos like T-9 or 9-8. Minni's raise on the flop is a 'min-click,' which could be a value-raise or a semi-bluff with a draw. By calling, I maintain control of the pot size while out of position, keeping her range wide and allowing myself to realize the equity of my straight draw and overpair on the turn without committing my entire stack against a potentially made hand like a set.

The turn 3s is a blank that doesn't change the board texture. After calling Minni's min-raise on the flop, I am now out of position and should check to the aggressor. My hand is still very strong with an overpair and an open-ended straight draw (any Queen or Seven), but I must be cautious of made straights (QJ, J7) or sets (TT, 99, 88) that Minni could have. Checking allows me to control the pot size and react to her next move.

I hold 7♥6♣ and already have the 10-high straight (6-7-8-9-10) on 9♥-8♦-10♣. The turn 3♠ is a harmless card. Flash checked, which looks like weakness or pot-control after calling my flop raise — his continuing range includes many hands I beat (TX, 9x, pocket pairs, KQ/AQ type hands) and only a very small number of combos (J7, QJ) actually make a better straight. I want to extract value and deny free rivers, so I’ll bet for value. I size the bet to ~60% pot (4,000) — big enough to charge worse hands and draws but not so large that I fold out most callers. If Flash check-raises or jams, the combos that beat me are rare and I’m prepared to call. If he just calls, I’ll re-evaluate on the river (checking back J/Q rivers that can produce higher straights).

I hold pocket Jacks, giving me an overpair and an open-ended straight draw (any Queen or Seven) on a highly coordinated board of 10-9-8-3. Minni's aggressive line—min-raising the flop and then betting 4,000 into a 6,800 pot on the turn—indicates a very strong range, likely including sets, two-pair, or even a made straight. However, with my overpair and eight clean outs to a straight, I have substantial equity against her entire value range and completely dominate any semi-bluffs or overplayed top pairs. Since Minni has only 7,225 chips left behind her 4,000 bet, she is effectively pot-committed. Raising all-in for 11,225 total on this street is the most effective play to capitalize on my equity and ensure all the chips get into the middle while I still have a massive draw and an overpair.

I have the made straight (6-7-8-9-10) on 9♥-8♦-10♣-3♠ with 7♥6♣. The turn 3♠ is harmless. Flash’s huge check-raise is polarizing, but the only realistic hands that beat me here are very narrow (combinations containing a J plus a Q or a J plus a 7 that make a higher straight). Most of his continuing range (pairs, sets, KQ/AQ, missed overcards, KJ, etc.) is behind my straight. The pot is 22,025 and I must call 7,225, so I only need ~24.7% equity to make the call. Given the small combo count that actually beats me and the large number of worse hands and bluffs he can have, this is a clear +EV all‑in call. I will call the 7,225.

Holding pocket deuces on the button in a heads-up situation, I have a made hand. While it's the lowest possible pair, it's still significantly ahead of most random hands in a heads-up range. I will raise to 300 to take the initiative and build the pot, or potentially take down the blinds immediately. Given the recent large loss in Hand #118, I need to play carefully but cannot afford to become too passive with playable hands.

Heads-up in the big blind with K7s facing a standard button open to 300. Calling 200 to defend is cheap but leaves me out of position postflop against a wide BTN opening range; I prefer to seize initiative and apply pressure with a balanced 3-bet. I’ll raise to 1000 (about 3x his open) — large enough to earn meaningful fold equity against his weaker opens, uses the K-blocker to reduce some strong Kx combos, and still keeps the stacks deep enough to maneuver postflop. If he 4-bets big I can fold or re-evaluate depending on sizing; if he calls I’ll play cautiously OOP but continue to leverage position/stack depth on favorable runouts.

Holding pocket deuces in a heads-up situation, I have a made hand that is a favorite against many of the overcard combinations in Minni's 3-betting range. While 22 is the lowest pair, it still carries significant value. Minni has a large chip lead and has been playing aggressively. Folding to a 3-bet here would be too tight, especially given the pot odds (calling 700 more to play for a 2000 chip pot). I will call the raise to see a flop, primarily looking to hit a set or navigate a low, favorable board.

I have K7s on Qd‑7h‑2c — middle pair with a decent kicker but out of position. Flash called my 3‑bet preflop, so his range is wide and contains a lot of Qx, Kx and medium pocket pairs plus plenty of air/secondary hands. The flop is very dry and vulnerable to Qx (which beats me) and to check‑raises from stronger made hands; betting here risks getting raised off my hand or bloating the pot OOP with only a single pair. I’ll check to control the pot size, induce bluffs or thin value bets from worse, and re‑evaluate on the turn. Plan: call a standard c‑bet to realize equity and fold to a large polarizing raise that commits most of the effective stack.

I have flopped bottom set with 2h2s on a Qd-7h-2c rainbow board. This is a monster hand in a heads-up 3-bet pot. Minni checked to me, which could indicate a missed hand like AK or AJ, or a trap with a strong Queen or an overpair. I need to build the pot now to get value from any of her holdings that might call, such as AQ, KK, AA, or even 7x. A bet of 800 (40% of the pot) is large enough to build the pot while still looking somewhat like a continuation bet or a probe, potentially enticing a check-raise or a call from her mid-strength range.

I 3‑bet preflop to 1000 so Flash’s calling range is wide (broadways, Kx, Qx, pockets, lots of air). The flop Qd‑7h‑2c gives me middle pair (7s) with a King kicker — decent heads‑up but out of position. I checked to control the pot and induce bluffs; Flash’s small 800 into a 2800 pot (~28%) is a probing c‑bet that will include a fair amount of air and worse pairs, not just strong Qx. Raising OOP with one pair here would bloat the pot and put me in difficult spots against Qx or big pockets. Calling keeps the pot manageable, allows me to realize equity and see a turn before committing more, and lets me fold to a large turn raise if he shows strength. I’ll call the 800 and re‑evaluate on the turn.

I have K7s (second pair) in a 3‑bet pot and I’m out of position on Qd‑7h‑2c‑Th. I checked the flop to control the pot and called his small 800 probe — his flop sizing was probing and contains a lot of air, Qx, Kx and medium pairs. The turn Ten doesn’t improve me beyond my single pair and actually helps some of his continuing combos (Tx, some Qx). Raising OOP with just middle pair would bloat the pot and put me in very difficult spots versus his stronger hands; betting thin for value will also fold out many worse hands that I want to keep (and get me called by many better hands). I’ll keep the pot manageable and preserve my options: check and allow him to barrel/bluff or show weakness. If he bets a small/medium size I’ll call and reevaluate on the river; I’ll fold to a large polarizing shove or overbet that commits most of the effective stack.

I have flopped a set of deuces, which remains extremely strong on this turn. Minni's check-call on the flop followed by a check on the turn likely indicates a Queen or an overpair playing pot control. While Pocket Tens would have improved to a better set, I cannot play fearfully given the strength of my hand in a heads-up match. I will bet 2,200 to extract value from her marginal hands and draws, and to set up a potential all-in on the river to recover chips after my previous major loss.

Call. I have K♠7♠ — second pair with a decent kicker, out of position. Flash 3‑bet called pre and check‑raised the flop, so his continuing range is wide (Qx, Tx, some Kx, medium pairs and lots of bluffs/air). His turn sizing (2,200 into 5,800, ~38%) is a small/medium probe rather than a polarizing shove. Pot odds: calling 2,200 to win ~8,000 requires ~27.5% equity. Given the range and price, my hand has reasonable showdown value and beats his bluffs and many underpairs. Raising OOP would bloat the pot and commit with a marginal hand; folding is too tight for the price. I’ll call 2,200 and re‑evaluate on the river — fold to a large shove/overbet that commits most of the effective stack, otherwise proceed to extract or check‑back depending on the river card and his action.

Check. I have K♠7♠ — second pair with a decent kicker in a 3‑bet pot and I’m out of position. Flash called my 3‑bet pre, barreled the flop and turn, so his continuing range contains a lot of Qx/Tx, some Kx and medium pairs plus bluffs. The 8♣ on the river is a blank that doesn’t improve me meaningfully and won’t fold many of the hands that actually beat me. A river bet will largely get called by hands that beat me (Q/T pairs) and won’t extract much value from the types of worse hands I actually beat, while also exposing me to a costly raise/all‑in given his ~6,750 behind. With the pot ~8,000 and no river bet to call, the prudent line is to check and take this to showdown; if he then jams a large overbet I’ll fold because his shove range is heavily weighted to hands that dominate my pair.

I have bottom set (222) on a board of Qd-7h-2c-Th-8c. This is a very strong hand in a heads-up 3-bet pot. Minni has check-called on both the flop and the turn, which strongly suggests she has a high-value one-pair hand like AQ, AA, or KK, or perhaps a hand like JJ or Ten-x. With the pot at 8,000 and my remaining stack at 6,750, I have less than a pot-sized bet remaining. Since Minni checked to me for a third time on the river, I must move all-in to extract maximum value from her likely calling range. While a straight (J9) or a higher set (TT) is theoretically possible, I am ahead of the vast majority of hands she would play this way.

Fold. I have second pair (7s) with a K kicker on Q♦‑7♥‑2♣‑T♥‑8♣. The pot is 14,750 and Flash shoved his remaining 6,750; calling 6,750 requires roughly 31.4% equity (6750 / (14750+6750)). Flash’s line — opening from the button, calling my 3‑bet, c‑betting the flop, barreling the turn and now shoving the river — is heavily weighted toward value hands (Qx, Tx, sets and some completed straights like J9). There are relatively few believable bluffs in that sequence and his all‑in leaves no implied odds to chase marginal equity. My kicker is weak and I’m out of position, so I don’t have the required equity against his continuing range. Preserving ~25.25k and waiting for a clearer +EV spot is the correct play here, so I fold to the river shove.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Qc9s — a hand I want to be playing aggressively in position. Open-raising takes the initiative, denies odds to the BB's speculative hands, and builds a pot when I'm often ahead of his calling range. I’ll raise to 300 (3x the BB) — a standard, comfortable size given deep stacks (my 25,200 vs his 14,650). Plan: if Flash 3-bets a small/standard size I’ll consider calling to play postflop in position; if he 3-bets huge or 4-bets all-in I’ll be prepared to fold this medium-strength holding. If called, proceed with standard continuation betting on favorable flops and pot-control on dry, marginal boards.

Defending the big blind with King-high in a heads-up situation. Given the pot odds (2:1) and the wide opening range of an opponent on the button, K3o has enough equity to warrant a call to see a flop. I will look to hit a King or a favorable board texture to continue.

On this flop of 8-5-2, I have King-high with no direct draw. In a heads-up pot where I defended the big blind, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. Betting here doesn't achieve much as I'm unlikely to fold out better hands, and checking allows me to evaluate Minni's continuation bet sizing and potentially see a turn card cheaply if she checks back.

I opened preflop and took the initiative; Flash checked the dry, low flop (8s 5h 2h). With Qc9s I have two overcards but no pair or strong draw, so this is a textbook heads-up c-bet spot to fold out missed hands and deny equity to heart draws. I’ll size up to ~2/3 pot (400) — big enough to get value/folds from Ax/Kx and other air, and to deny correct odds to draws, but small relative to our deep stack so I can fold to heavy resistance. If he calls I’ll proceed cautiously on the turn (continue on safe cards, give up to large raises or when hearts pair); if he check-raises big I’ll let this one go.

On a board of 8s 5h 2h, my K3o has very little equity. Minni's continuation bet of 400 into 600 (66% pot) is a strong sizing. While I have a King as an overcard, I am out of position with no real draws and no backdoor potential. Calling here would likely lead to difficult decisions on later streets if I don't improve immediately. Given the current stack depths and the fact that I've recently won significant pots, there is no need to make a marginal defense with King-high. I will fold and wait for a more advantageous situation.

I am on the button with Ac5c, which is a strong starting hand in heads-up play, especially being suited with an Ace. I have roughly 144 big blinds, while Minni has over 250 big blinds. I've been aggressive lately (Hand #119, Hand #118), and Minni has shown both the ability to call down and to fold to pressure. Raising here is the standard play to take control of the pot and leverage position.

Heads-up vs the button (SB) who opened to 300 — their range is wide. I’m in the BB with K2o (K♥2♠). Pot is 400 and I need to put in 200 more, so calling to 300 gives me ~33% pot odds. With deep stacks I don’t want to bloat the pot out of position with a marginal hand and a weak kicker, but K2 has a useful king-blocker and reasonable equity vs a wide open. Plan: call to see a flop; continue with top pair or good draws, and be prepared to fold to heavy aggression on dry, unconnected boards. A 3-bet is possible as a bluff, but I prefer the lower-variance line and to keep the pot manageable.

Check. Heads-up out of position on Qh Th 4s with K♥2♠ — I have only king-high, no made pair, and just a very unlikely backdoor heart flush (I hold K♥ with two hearts on board; need both turn+river to hit ≈4%).
