I have ~12–13% to pair a king by the river. Flash opened wide from the button, so his range contains lots of Qx/Tx, broadways and draws that will c-bet for value or as a continuation. Betting OOP with a marginal K-high bloats the pot and will often get called or raised by worse (and folded to by better), so I prefer pot control. Plan: check to keep the pot small, fold to heavy aggression (large c-bet / raise — e.g. >~1/2 pot) since my raw equity isn’t enough to continue, but consider calling small c-bets (cheap floats) to realize equity and see a turn; if I pair a King on the turn I’ll play more aggressively. If Flash checks back I take a free turn to reassess.

I have Ace-high with Ac5c and some backdoor potential (clubs and a low straight) on a board of Qh-Th-4s. This board texture is quite coordinated and likely connects with a fair portion of the Big Blind's calling range. However, as the pre-flop aggressor, I should lead out with a continuation bet to maintain initiative and potentially take the pot down immediately. If Minni calls, I have some equity to improve on the turn, and if she raises, I can comfortably fold as I only have Ace-high on a dangerous board.

Call 300. Flash’s flop stab is small (~1/3 pot) and he opened a wide button range preflop, so he has lots of missed hands and bluffs in his c-bet range. I have K♥2♠ — only king-high but it blocks some Kx/broadway combos and has a tiny backdoor heart possibility. Calling keeps the pot manageable out of position, gets correct pot odds (~300 into 900 → need ~25% equity) to realize against a wide range, and preserves the option to fold to strong turn aggression. Raising OOP with a marginal hand and weak kicker is too high-variance; folding to a 300 c-bet here is too nitty. Plan: call, see the turn, continue if I pair a K or pick up meaningful equity, otherwise fold to large bets.

Board is Qh Th 4s 7h; I hold K♥2♠ — no pair but I now have a four-card heart combination (K♥ + three hearts on board). That gives me ~9/46 ≈ 19.5% to make a flush on the river and another ~6.5% to pair a King, so my raw equity vs a wide button range is meaningful (~25% combined). Flash opened wide and fired a small flop c-bet, then checked the turn — a line consistent with a wide, often weak or medium-strength range. Pot (1200) is tiny relative to our stacks, so a controlled semi-bluff is appropriate: I can fold to a strong raise but a half-pot bet will fold out many unpaired broadways and some marginal pairs while giving me good equity if called. Plan: bet ~half-pot (600). If he folds I take the pot; if he calls I go to the river with ~20% to hit the flush and will value/lead if I complete or check/defend carefully if I miss; if he raises, I will fold K-high rather than bloat a pot OOP with marginal equity.

The turn 7h is a highly coordinated card that completes many possible flushes and some straights (like J9). Minni's decision to lead out (donk-bet) for 600 into 1200 after calling the flop suggests she has either completed a strong hand or is semi-bluffing with a heart in her hand. My Ace-high (Ac5c) has no hearts and no real equity to improve to a winning hand on the river. Attempting a bluff here against a deep-stacked opponent who is showing initiative is too risky. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button with Jc8c — a playable, suited hand with good postflop playability and position. With deep stacks and the initiative preflop, prefer to open-raise rather than limp; raising steals the blinds often and gives fold equity against a wide big-blind defense. A standard 3x-ish open to 300 (total) is size-consistent with the table, pressures weaker hands, and leaves plenty of room to maneuver postflop. If Flash folds we take the pot; if he calls I can use position to c-bet favorable flops and control the pot on dry boards. Calling 50 is too passive and folding is overly tight here.

I am holding 7-2 offsuit, which is statistically the weakest hand in poker. Although I am getting 2:1 odds to call the raise from the Big Blind, this hand has very poor playability and I will be out of position for the rest of the hand. Given that I have a healthy stack and there are only about 30 hands left in the tournament, there is no reason to defend with the worst possible starting hand against an aggressive opponent who covers my stack.

I am on the button in a heads-up situation. Although 7-4 suited is a relatively weak hand, it has some playability and potential. In heads-up play, it is standard to open a wide range of hands from the button to exert pressure and take control of the pot. I will raise to my standard 3x opening size to see how Minni reacts.

Facing a button open to 300 heads-up. I’m in the big blind with 5♠3♦ — a weak offsuit holding, but heads-up opening ranges are very wide and the pot odds are attractive (call 200 into a 400 pot). Stacks are deep (I ~26,150 ≈ 261 BB; Villain ~13,450 ≈ 134 BB), so there are implied odds to see flops, but I’m out of position which makes bloating the pot with air unwise. I’ll defend and see a flop with a plan to control the pot: call to 300, continue only with clear improvement or strong draws, and fold to heavy aggression on dry, high-card boards. I’ll reserve 3-bets for better hands or balanced bluff spots rather than re-raising this hand OOP.

I flopped trips (3♦ with 3♠3♣ on the board and Q♣?) — actually board is Q♥ 3♠ 3♣ and my 3♦ gives me three of a kind. Heads‑up vs a preflop raiser this is an extremely strong hand. The only realistic hands that can currently beat me are full houses involving a queen + the remaining 3 or pocket queens — possible but relatively rare. With deep stacks I want to build the pot and keep lots of worse hands (Qx, Kx, Ax, medium pairs, draws) in the hand. A medium value bet (~half pot) is the right compromise: it extracts from top‑pair and worse hands, protects against giving free cards, and keeps weaker hands from folding to a big sizing. If he raises large I’ll re‑evaluate (likely call or commit depending on size/read); if he calls I’ll continue extracting on later streets.

On a board of Qh-3s-3c, Minni's lead of 300 into a 600 pot (a donk bet) is an aggressive move. While this paired, top-heavy board generally favors my pre-flop raising range, my actual hand (7h 4h) is extremely weak. I have 7-high with only backdoor heart potential. While I could float or raise as a semi-bluff, Minni has shown in previous hands that she can be 'sticky' and follow through on later streets. Given the stack sizes and the fact that I'm trailing in the tournament, I'll choose the disciplined path and fold this low-equity hand rather than getting involved in a potentially expensive bluffing war.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with K♥J♥ — a strong, playable hand for an open raise. Pot is 150 and it’s only 50 to complete; with deep stacks (I’m ~265 BB) I want to take the initiative and build the pot in position while retaining post‑flop maneuverability. Standard heads‑up sizing of ~2.5–3x the BB gives good fold equity versus a wide BB defending range but doesn’t commit too much preflop. I’ll raise to 300 total. Plan: if Flash calls I’ll c‑bet appropriate flops (bet dry ace/low boards for fold equity, check very connected boards); if he 3‑bets small I can call and use position postflop, fold to very large 4‑bets or polarizing action.

In this heads-up situation, I am holding 6-4 offsuit in the Big Blind. Minni has raised to 3x the big blind from the Button/Small Blind. While pot odds are somewhat attractive (calling 200 to win a total pot of 400), 6-4 offsuit is an extremely weak hand that performs poorly out of position, especially against an aggressive chip leader like Minni. I currently have around 133 big blinds, so there is no immediate pressure to defend with bottom-tier hands. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity to challenge Minni's stack.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-3 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in heads-up poker. Although I am in position, Minni has a significant chip lead and has been playing aggressively. There is no reason to invest chips with such a low-equity hand when I need to look for better opportunities to close the gap in the remaining 25 hands. Folding is the standard and most disciplined play here.

Heads-up on the button with K♦8♣. In heads-up play this is a playable, often-opening hand — I want initiative and fold equity rather than just completing to the big blind. Flash has shown aggression preflop but also calls big pots; with a deeper stack I can play postflop. I raise to 300 (3x the big blind) to isolate, take the lead, and deny cheap flops. Plan: continuation-bet favorable flops, play pot control on dangerous boards, and re-evaluate if Flash 3-bets big (fold to very large 3-bets, consider calling smaller 3-bets).

I am holding a pocket pair of threes (3h 3c) in the Big Blind. Minni has raised from the Small Blind/Button to 300. With a stack of 13,200, I have 132 big blinds, and Minni has twice that. Pocket pairs have significant value in heads-up play, but they often play best when they hit a set. Given the current pot odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600), calling is the most standard play here to see a flop and realize my hand's equity or set-mine without bloating the pot out of position against a larger stack. 3-betting is an option, but keeping the pot manageable with a small pair is generally preferred unless I want to turn it into a bluff or merge my range.

I have a small pocket pair (3h 3c) on a paired board (2s 2d Jd). While my hand is currently ahead of most of Minni's unpaired overcard range, it is very vulnerable and loses to any Jack or a deuce. Checking to the pre-flop aggressor is the standard procedure here to see how she chooses to continue. This allows me to control the pot size and decide whether to call a continuation bet based on the sizing and her betting tendencies.

Heads-up on the button I opened preflop and kept the initiative. Flop 2♠ 2♦ J♦ misses a lot of BB’s calling range (many broadways, unpaired hands) while only really strong for sets or Jx. I hold K♦8♣ — an overcard to the board plus a backdoor diamond redraw and position. Flash checked, showing weakness, so I want to seize the initiative and use fold equity. I’ll continuation-bet ~2/3 pot (400) to fold out weaker overcards and unpaired hands, build the pot when I’m likely ahead, and keep the option to fold to a polarizing check-raise (sets/Jx). If he calls I’ll reassess on the turn and proceed more aggressively on favorable cards (K or safe blanks) or pot-control/fold to strong resistance.

I have a small pocket pair (3h 3c) on a paired board (2s 2d Jd). While my hand is vulnerable to any Jack or a higher pocket pair, it is currently ahead of all of Minni's unpaired overcard bluffs (like AK, AQ, or KQ). A 400 bet into a 600 pot is a standard continuation bet. Folding now would be too tight and exploitable, so I will call to see the turn and re-evaluate based on the next card and her betting sizing.

I have just turned a full house (threes full of deuces). This is an incredibly strong hand, nearly the nuts, as the only hands that beat me are pocket Jacks or a quad deuces, both of which are statistically unlikely. On the flop, I checked and called a bet from Minni. Now that the turn has improved my hand significantly, I want to maximize value. Since Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand and in many previous hands, checking again is the best play to induce another bet from her, especially if she has a Jack, a pocket pair, or is semi-bluffing with a flush draw. If she checks behind, I can lead for value on the river, but a check here is most likely to keep her range wide and build a larger pot.

Heads-up on the button with K♦8♣. I c-bet the paired flop (2♠ 2♦ J♦) and Flash called, so his range contains Jx, pocket pairs (including 22), and lots of weaker unpaired hands. Turn 3♠ is a harmless blank and Flash checks, showing weakness — perfect spot to apply pressure. I have little showdown value but good fold equity; a ~2/3-pot double-barrel credibly represents a Jx/Kx and will fold many unimproved hands. Bet 900 to take the pot now; if he check-raises or fires a big raise I’ll strongly discount my hand (fold to large resistance), and if he calls I’ll reassess on the river.

I have turned a full house (threes full of deuces), which is an extremely strong hand on this board. The only hands currently beating me are pocket Jacks or the case deuce with a better kicker, both of which are statistically unlikely. Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, and her turn bet of 900 into 1400 indicates she either has a strong Jack, a pocket pair, or is continuing a semi-bluff with a diamond draw. By calling instead of raising, I keep her entire range in the pot and allow her to continue bluffing or value-betting on the river. Raising now might cause her to fold her air, whereas calling maintains the deception of my hand and sets up a significant value bet or check-raise on the river.

The board has completed as 2s-2d-Jd-3s-Jc. My pocket threes have improved to a full house, threes full of jacks (3-3-3-J-J). On this board, the hierarchy of full houses is Jacks full, then Threes full, then Deuces full. Therefore, I beat any hand containing a Deuce (2-2-2-J-J) or any pocket pair lower than Jacks, but I lose to any hand containing a Jack (J-J-J-2-2). Minni has been the aggressor throughout the hand, which is consistent with both a Jack and a wide range of semi-bluffs or other value hands. By checking the river, I am inducing Minni to continue bluffing with missed draws (like diamonds) or to value bet with a Deuce. If I bet, I likely only get called or raised by hands that beat me (a Jack), whereas checking allows me to capture value from her bluffs.

River: 2♠ 2♦ J♦ 3♠ J♣ — the board is two pair. My K♦8♣ has no showdown value beyond the board; I can’t beat any full house/trips (Jx, 2x, 3x) and only win if Flash folds. Flash called my flop (400) and turn (900), which strongly indicates a real made hand (very likely Jx or a pocket pair) and he’s shown sticky calling tendencies earlier. His river check looks like pot control with a hand that beats me or a trap, so bluffing here has low fold equity and high downside. I’ll preserve my stack and check.

I am on the button (Small Blind) with Jack-Nine offsuit. This is a solid holding in heads-up play, typically well within the range for an open-raise to apply pressure and take control of the hand. Minni is the chip leader, so I want to play a controlled pot. Raising to 300 (a 3x open) is standard here to build the pot while retaining the ability to navigate post-flop with position.

I have pocket Aces (Ad Ah) in the big blind facing a button open to 300. Heads‑up this is the single best preflop hand — I want to build the pot and charge weaker Ax, broadways and suited connectors while denying cheap flops to speculative hands. Calling 200 lets the button see a cheap flop; a min‑raise would be too small and easily exploited. With deep effective stacks (~14.6k) I’ll 3‑bet for value but not commit my stack — raise to 1,200 (about 4x the open). This sizing extracts value, applies pressure to draws/mediocre hands, and keeps options postflop; if Flash 4‑bets large I’ll re‑evaluate based on their sizing and tendencies.

I opened with Jack-Nine offsuit from the button, which is a standard play in heads-up. Minni responded with a 4x 3-bet to 1,200. While I have position for the rest of the hand, J9o is a marginal holding that doesn't play particularly well against a large 3-bet sizing from the chip leader. Given the tournament context and the strength of the raise, folding is the most prudent play to preserve my stack and wait for a better opportunity.

Heads-up on the button (I posted the SB and will have position postflop). J♣3♣ is weak offsuit but in heads-up I should open a very wide range to apply pressure and steal the blind. The pot is 150 and the min-raise is to 200; I'll raise to the minimum (200 total) — a standard 2x BB open that maximizes fold equity while keeping the pot manageable with a marginal hand. Plan: fold to a large 3-bet, but if called use positional advantage to continuation-bet favorable flops and play cautiously when I miss.

I am in the Big Blind with 5s2h, which is one of the weakest hands in poker. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Button. While the price to call is only 100 more to see a flop, 5-2 offsuit plays extremely poorly out of position and I would likely be folding to most continuation bets anyway. I will preserve my stack and wait for a better spot to engage.

Q9o is a solid starting hand in a heads-up game, well within the top tier of opening hands from the button/small blind. Raising to 300 has been my standard opening size in recent orbits and allows me to maintain aggression and take control of the pot early against Minni.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) — his open to 300 is wide. J♥2♣ is a weak, but not worthless, hand in the big blind heads-up: pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more, giving ~33% pot odds to see a flop. With Flash's wide raising range my hand has reasonable equity and can hit a jack, a deuce, or two-pair/straights/backsdoors. I’m out of position postflop so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal 3-bet bluff; instead I’ll defend the blind cheaply, keep the pot controllable, and play cautiously on the flop — fold to large aggression if I miss, value/raise when I improve. Call to 300 total.

Flop: 7h 5c 8s — I have Jh2c (no pair, J-high). Flash opened wide from the button preflop, so his range is broad, but I’m out of position and have very little equity (only 6 clean outs: 3 jacks + 3 deuces). There’s no realistic flush/straight draw for me. Best line is pot control: check and see what Flash does. If he checks back I’ll take a free showdown with my marginal hand. If he makes a small probe bet (roughly ≤ ~1/3–1/2 pot) I can consider a call/float to try to take it away on a later street or realize my outs; if he barrels a larger sizing I’ll fold most of the time because my OOP equity is too weak. If I hit a pair on the turn (J or 2) I will begin to extract value; otherwise keep folding to big aggression.

I have one overcard (Queen) to the board and a gutshot straight draw needing a six. While this coordinated board texture (7-5-8) likely connects well with a big blind's defending range, I will lead with a standard half-pot continuation bet of 300. This maintains my pre-flop aggression and forces Minni to have a piece of the board to continue, while also building a pot in case I improve on the turn.

Flash opened wide preflop and makes a small flop bet (300 into a 900 pot, ~1/3 pot). My J♣2♣ has no pair but can hit a J or 2 (6 clean outs) and has ~24% to pair by the river — the immediate pot odds on a call (risk 300 to win 1200) require ~20% equity, so a call is +EV here. I’ll call to realize equity and keep the pot manageable out of position; plan is to fold to large, committed turn aggression if I miss, to float and try to take the pot on later streets if he shows weakness, and to extract value if I pair up on turn/river.

Board is 7♥ 5♣ 8♠ 4♠ and I hold J♥2♣ — I have no pair and only 6 outs. After the turn I have ~6/46 ≈ 13% to improve on the river, which is far below the equity required to profitably call a standard bet here (e.g. 300 into a 1200 pot needs ≈20% equity). I’m out of position vs a wide button range that contains many pairs and the few straights (6x) that already beat me. Best play is pot‑control: check and avoid bloating the pot with a marginal hand. If Flash bets a meaningful amount I’ll fold; if he checks back I’ll see a free river and re-evaluate (value if I pair, or look for a river bluff only if the situation favors it).

The turn 4s creates a very coordinated board (4-5-7-8), where any 6 now makes a straight. My Q9o has very little showdown value, but it does hold a 9 which blocks some straight combinations. However, this board texture heavily favors a Big Blind defending range, which contains many mid-low connectors. Minni has called the flop and checked the turn. By checking back, I avoid bloating the pot with a weak hand on a dangerous board while getting a free look at the river to potentially improve to a pair of Queens or Nines, or to evaluate a bluffing opportunity if the river is a safe card.

River: board 7♥ 5♣ 8♠ 4♠ 9♦, I hold J♥2♣ — I missed completely (no pair, no backdoor). Flash opened wide preflop, c‑bet small on the flop, then checked turn and checked the river. That line looks like weakness/pot‑control or a marginal pair; while a tiny river bluff could fold out some air, my hand has virtually no showdown value and limited fold equity vs a wide, calling button. Pot is small relative to stacks and I want to avoid creating a spot where I get called or check‑raised with nothing. Opt to take the free showdown and preserve chips — check.

The river 9d gives me top pair (Nines) with a Queen kicker. The board is highly coordinated (4-5-7-8-9), and any 6 in Minni's range makes a straight. Since Minni checked the river, I have significant showdown value. While I could attempt a value bet to get called by an 8 or a 7, the board texture is dangerous; a check-raise from Minni would be difficult to call. Checking back ensures I realize my equity and win the pot if my top pair is the best hand, which is likely given her passive line on the turn and river.

Heads-up on the button (I’ve already posted the SB 50). Q♦6♥ is marginal but perfectly playable in position — heads-up I should be raising a wide range to take the initiative and exploit my positional advantage. Flash has shown a mix of preflop aggression and folds, so I want a sizing that gives fold equity and keeps the pot manageable with a medium-strength holding. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x the BB) — large enough to apply pressure and consistent with my usual opens. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold; if he calls I’ll play the flop in position and look to continue against weak ranges.

K6o is a solid defending hand in the Big Blind against a standard button open. The pot odds are favorable (calling 200 to win 400 already in the pot), and King-high holds significant equity against a wide opening range. I will call to see the flop.

On a board of Ah-Ts-Qh, my King-high with a gutshot straight draw (needing a Jack) is relatively weak against Minni's likely opening range, which contains many Aces, Queens, and Tens. This board texture heavily favors the pre-flop aggressor. I will check to see how Minni acts; if she bets, I'll have to evaluate the price to chase my four-outer, but for now, checking is the standard defensive play.

I have top pair (Q) with a very weak kicker (Qd6h) in position on Ah Ts Qh. Flash checked the flop after calling preflop, so his range is wide and contains many worse queens, tens and pocket pairs, plus lots of one‑card hearts with backdoor potential. I want to take the initiative, charge draws and worse pairs, and deny free cards that can improve his equity. A half‑pot c‑bet (~300 into the 600 pot) achieves that: it extracts value from weaker hands and makes drawing hands pay to continue. If Flash raises big I can fold (my kicker is poor and I’m likely behind to Ax or two‑pair+), if he calls I’ll re‑evaluate on the turn and proceed cautiously if an Ace or heart appears.

The board texture (Ah-Ts-Qh) heavily favors Minni's pre-flop raising range, which is likely to contain many Broadway combinations and Aces. My King-high has almost no showdown value here. While I do have a gutshot straight draw to the nuts (needing a Jack), the pot odds do not justify a call. I need to call 300 to win a total pot of 1200, requiring 25% equity. My gutshot only has about an 8.5% chance of hitting on the turn. Chasing a thin draw on such a coordinated board against an aggressive opponent is not profitable in the long run. I will fold and preserve my stack.

In heads-up play, Queen-Seven suited is a strong starting hand on the button. I have 147.5 big blinds and should play aggressively to narrow the gap between my stack and Minni's. A standard opening raise to 300 is consistent with my previous play and allows me to take control of the hand early.

Facing a 3bb open to 300 from the button while I’m in the big blind with Ac3h. Pot is 400 and I need to call 200 more (total 300) — ~33% pot odds. Heads-up A3o has good equity versus a wide button opening range, and with deep stacks I can realize implied odds. I’m out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot with a marginal kicker by 3-betting; calling keeps the pot manageable and lets me see a flop where I can proceed aggressively on favorable textures. Call 300 total.

We have top pair (A♣) with a weak 3-kicker on A♠ K♠ 5♦. Villain opened wide from the button preflop, so his checking the flop is consistent with many worse Axs, A2-A5, Kx, and lots of air/float candidates. I want to extract value from worse aces and thin Kx hands while denying free cards to backdoor spade or gutshot combos, so a modest half-pot bet is best. Betting 300 keeps the pot controllable out of position, gets value, and lets me fold or re-evaluate if I face a large check-raise (or call a single raise to continue depending on sizing).

In this heads-up situation, I raised pre-flop with Queen-Seven suited and Minni called from the Big Blind. The flop comes Ace-King-Five, which is a board that generally favors my pre-flop raising range. However, Minni has led out with a 'donk bet' of 300 into the 600 pot. This lead often indicates a hand that wants to define its value (like a King or a Five) or a draw (like spades). While I have the Queen of diamonds for some backdoor flush potential, I have no direct piece of this board and my Queen-high has very little showdown value. Given the board texture heavily connects with a standard Big Blind calling range (which includes many Ax and Kx hands), and I have zero current equity, I will fold and wait for a better spot rather than floating or raising as a bluff against a showing of strength on an Ace-high board.

Heads-up on the button (I’m the SB) with J♣6♠ and very deep stacks — this is a spot to be aggressive. J6o is marginal but playable in position: raising takes the initiative, picks up the pot when BB folds, and gives me post‑flop leverage. I don’t want to be overly passive and let the BB see a cheap flop, and a standard 3x sizing reduces frequency of light 3‑bets compared to the min-raise. I’ll raise to 300 total (3x BB). If Flash 3‑bets big I can fold this hand; if he calls I’ll play postflop in position and use fold equity and position to pressure.
In a heads-up match, 96 suited is a strong enough hand to defend the big blind against a standard 3x raise. I am getting 2-to-1 odds to call and the hand has good post-flop playability.

I have flopped bottom two pair (Nines and Sixes) on a relatively coordinated board (9s-Jd-6h). In a heads-up situation, this is a very strong hand. I will check to the pre-flop aggressor, Minni, to induce a continuation bet. This allows me to capture value from her bluffs and semi-bluffs, while also giving me the opportunity to check-raise to protect my hand against potential straight draws like Q-T or T-8.

Flop: 9♠ J♦ 6♥ — I have J♣6♠ for two pair (Jacks and Sixes) in position. Heads‑up this is a very strong hand but vulnerable to rare sets (JJ/99/66) and to turn cards that complete straight draws (QT, K-T, etc.). Flash checked, so his range is wide: single J’s, 9x, 6x, pairs, and straight/overcard draws. I should charge worse hands and protect against draws while leveraging position. A value/protection bet of roughly 2/3 pot is ideal to extract from Jx/9x/6x and charge QT/KT type draws while keeping many worse hands calling. I’ll bet 400 into the 600 pot. Plan: if called, evaluate the turn — continue for value on blanks and be cautious on Q/T/K/8 or a third spade; if I face a large check‑raise I’ll assume a set or very strong hand and re‑evaluate (fold or pot‑control depending on sizing).

I have flopped bottom two pair (Nines and Sixes) on a J-9-6 board. In a heads-up match, this is a premium hand. Minni has made a standard continuation bet of 400 into a 600 pot. To maximize value and protect against various straight draws (like Q-T, T-8, or 7-8) and overcards, I will put in a check-raise. A raise to 1200 is approximately 3x her bet and builds a significant pot while I likely have the best hand.

I have J♣6♠ on 9♠ J♦ 6♥ — two pair (Jacks & Sixes) in position. I bet 400 into the ~600 pot and Flash check‑raised to 1,200 (pot 2,200), so it costs me 800 to call (min‑raise to 2,000). Two pair is very strong heads‑up; the only clear hands that currently beat me are sets (99/JJ/66) and very specific turn/river straights. Calling 800 into 2,200 requires ~26.7% equity — well within what my hand has versus a realistic polarized check‑raise range (some sets/overpairs but also single pairs, draws and bluffs). I prefer to call to control the pot size, keep bluffs/semi‑bluffs and worse one‑pair hands in, and re‑evaluate on the turn (fold to extreme polarization/shove on a scary card or continue extracting value on blanks).

On the turn, the King of Diamonds is a bit of a scare card as it completes the most obvious straight draw (Q-T) and could have improved Minni to a better two pair (like K-J or K-9). However, I have also picked up a diamond flush draw to go with my bottom two pair. After my check-raise on the flop, checking the turn allows me to control the pot size and see how Minni reacts to the overcard. If she checks back, I get a free look at the river. If she bets, I still have significant equity to call and see if I can improve or if my two pair remains the best hand.

The big flop check‑raise put Flash on a polarized/strong range (sets, two‑pair combos, big single pairs and some strong draws like Q10). I currently have two‑pair (J6) but the turn K♦ is a dangerous card — it gives villain Kx two‑pair combos and completes some Q10 straights. Flash checked the turn, which is ambiguous (could be a trap or pot‑control). Betting here would inflate a pot (3,000) and risk a large check‑raise or committing a lot of chips when many hands in his range now beat me. Pot control is the prudent line: I’ll check behind to avoid getting forced off or bloating the pot on a marginal two‑pair, keep bluffs/semi‑bluffs in, and evaluate the river. If he bets the river I’ll re‑assess based on sizing and board texture (call smaller bets, fold to large polarized shoves).

The board has become quite dangerous for my bottom two pair (Nines and Sixes). The King on the turn and the Ten on the river have completed several possible straights (Q-T and 7-8) and created several better two-pair combinations (K-J, K-9, K-T, J-T). When I check-raised the flop, I represented a very strong hand, but checking the turn after the King appeared showed some concern. Minni's check-back on the turn could indicate a medium-strength hand like a Jack or a King looking for pot control. Given the board texture, my hand is now essentially a bluff-catcher. Betting here doesn't achieve much as better hands won't fold and few worse hands will call. I will check to see if I can get to a cheap showdown or evaluate based on her bet size.

I have two pair (J6) heads‑up — a strong hand but vulnerable. Flash’s flop check‑raise was polarized (sets, strong pairs, or bluffs/semi‑bluffs) and his checks on the turn and river either represent slow‑played strength or pot‑control with marginal hands. The K on the turn and the T on the river bring a number of hands that now beat me (Kx two‑pair combos, completed QT straights, and sets), so betting into a checked pot risks bloating it and getting raised off a marginal two‑pair. Pot control is the prudent line: I’ll check and go to showdown. If Flash unexpectedly bets, I’ll call reasonable sizing for value/investigation but fold to a large polarized shove that’s unlikely to be a bluff.

I am on the button with 94 offsuit. This is a very weak hand with poor playability post-flop. Given the current stack situation and the fact that we are approaching the final hands of the tournament, I will fold this hand and wait for a better opportunity to commit chips.

Heads-up on the button with Ah3c — this is a hand I should be raising. In heads-up play I open a wide range and want to take the initiative with position. Raising to 300 (≈3x the BB / standard sizing) builds the pot, applies pressure to the big blind and uses the ace blocker to reduce strong ace combinations. Flash is an active opponent so this size keeps the pot manageable against aggression; if he 3-bets big I will fold or re-evaluate based on sizing, if he calls I'll c-bet favorable flops and use position to realize my equity.

I am in the Big Blind with 8h 4c. Minni, who has a larger stack and has been active, raises to 300. 84-offsuit is a very weak hand, even in a heads-up situation. Calling would put me out of position against a stack that is nearly double mine with a hand that has poor playability and realization of equity. Given we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 134/150), I should preserve my stack for better opportunities rather than defending with such a weak hand.

I am in the Small Blind with 9-2 offsuit, which is one of the weakest starting hands in poker. Although I only need to call 50 more to see a flop and I will have position post-flop, this hand has very poor playability and equity. Given that I am currently trailing in chips and there are only 15 hands remaining, I prefer to fold and wait for a more favorable hand to commit chips to rather than risk being outplayed in a small pot with trash cards.

Heads-up on the button with T9o — a very playable, connected hand. I have deep stacks (271 BB) and position postflop, while the BB defends very widely. I want the initiative and to isolate, so I’ll raise rather than complete the small blind. Raising to 300 (3x the BB) is a standard heads-up open size: it builds the pot when I’m often ahead, pressures weak hands, and keeps my range balanced versus a calling BB. Postflop plan: if called I’ll use position to c-bet favorable textures and control the pot on dangerous boards; if Flash 3-bets big I’ll usually fold T9o, but I’ll call small 3-bets depending on odds and stack dynamics.

I have pocket sevens, which is a very strong hand in heads-up play. Minni has been opening frequently from the small blind. I will 3-bet to 1000 for value and to take the initiative, as my hand is significantly ahead of her wide opening range. This also helps define her range and potentially win the pot pre-flop.

Flash 3-bet to 1000 after my open to 300. The pot is ~1300 and I must call 700 (total 1000) to continue. I have T♦9♠ on the button with very deep stacks and position — this hand plays well postflop and has reasonable equity vs a wide/aggressive 3-bet range Flash has shown. Calling gives me ~35% pot odds (700 into a 2000 pot) and long-term implied odds to realize straights/top-pair hands in position. 4-betting with an unsuited connector is thin and unnecessary; folding is too nitty given the stack depth and opponent tendencies. Plan if called: use position to c-bet favorable low/connected textures, check/control or float on dry Ace-high boards, and fold to heavy turn/river aggression when I miss. Therefore I will call, keeping the pot controllable and leveraging position.

I have an overpair to the lower cards on the board, though the Queen is a concern. As the pre-flop aggressor in a 3-bet pot, I should lead out with a continuation bet to maintain initiative and get value from hands like lower pairs, A-high, or straight draws (like 67 or 32). A bet of 800 (40% of the pot) is a balanced sizing that applies pressure without overcommitting if Minni has a Queen or a better pocket pair.

Call 800. Facing Flash’s small c‑bet (800 into a ~2000 pot) on 4♦‑5♣‑Q♠, a call is the lowest-variance +EV line: I only need ~22% equity (800 to win 3600) and T♦9♠ has reasonable equity vs a wide 3‑bet range plus good playability in position. The flop is fairly dry (no flush draw, no pair) so a raise is a thin bluff vs a preflop 3‑bettor who can continue with many hands; calling lets me realize equity, float and apply positional pressure on the turn, or fold to significant turn/river aggression if I miss. I’ll call and re-evaluate the turn action based on whether I improve or the opponent shows strength.

The turn card is an Ace, which is a major scare card given that I have pocket sevens. While I held an overpair to the low cards on the flop, the board now contains two overcards (Queen and Ace) that hit Minni's calling range for a 3-bet pot significantly. By checking, I am looking to control the pot size and avoid further commitment against a range that likely contains many Ax or Qx hands. Betting here would likely only get called by hands that beat me, and checking allows me to see how she reacts to the Ace.

Check. I have T♦9♠ on 4♦‑5♣‑Q♠‑A♦: no pair and only ~6 outs to a pair (~13% on the river). Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, flop c‑bet, then check the Ace turn) is ambiguous — it contains both value (A/Q combos, sets) and missed bluffs — but the turn Ace actually strengthens a lot of his plausible value hands and reduces my fold equity. Betting the turn as a bluff is thin: many hands in his 3‑bet range that he’ll check (Ax, Qx, medium pairs) will call, and I don’t want to inflate the pot with such a weak holding. Checking keeps the pot under control, lets me realize my equity, and preserves the option to bluff or value‑bet on the river if the card is favorable (or to fold to strength). If he bets the river and I haven’t improved I’ll fold to meaningful pressure; if I pair a T/9 or get a river that credibly attacks his missed range I can fire for value/bluff accordingly.

The river is a Six of Clubs, making the final board 4d-5c-Qs-Ad-6c. My pair of Sevens is now the third-best possible pair on the board, trailing only Queens and Aces. Given the action, where Minni checked back the Ace on the turn, she might have a middle pair like a Jack or Ten, or perhaps a lower pair like Fives or Sixes that she was trying to get to showdown with. However, she could also be trapping with a weak Ace or a Queen. Betting here doesn't accomplish much, as better hands (Ax, Qx) will call and worse hands will likely fold. By checking, I allow for the possibility of winning at showdown against her missed draws (like 78 or 23) or smaller pocket pairs, and I avoid losing more chips if she has me beat.

Board: 4♦-5♣-Q♠-A♦-6♣. I hold T♦9♠ — no pair, no meaningful blocker to Ace or Queen. Flash’s line (preflop 3‑bet, small flop c‑bet that I called, then check on the Ace turn and check river) is ambiguous and contains both value (Ax, Qx, sets) and some missed c‑betters. The turn Ace strengthens many of his value combos and reduces my fold equity on the river; I don’t block the Ace or Queen so I can’t credibly represent the strongest parts of his checking range. Betting as a bluff here is too thin — many hands that would call a river bet (Qx, Ax, small pairs) still beat me, and a large sizing to force folds is unnecessarily risky given effective stacks (~11k) and the pot (3,600). I’ll check and go to showdown (and fold to any sizable bet), keeping the pot small with a missed hand.

I am on the button with T3o, a very weak hand. Given the tournament stage and my stack size relative to Minni's, there's no reason to play this hand. I will fold and wait for a better spot to utilize my chips.

Heads-up from the button (I’m the SB) with T♠J♣ — a strong, connected broadway hand with good postflop playability. Pot is 150 and I only need to put 50 more to call, but raising to the min (200 total) is standard: it gives me initiative, folds out weaker holdings from the BB, and builds a pot I can play in position. Opponent (Flash) has a healthy stack (≈14.4k) so a small 2x open keeps my range wide and avoids giving cheap 3-bet odds; if he 3-bets large I can reevaluate (often fold or call depending on sizing), if he calls I play postflop with position advantage. Raise to 200 balances value and fold equity — best long-term play heads-up with this holding.

In this heads-up situation, I'm getting 3:1 odds to call with 8d4d, which is a decent suited connector-style hand. Minni's 2bb open is relatively small and I am in the Big Blind, so I should be defending a wide range. Calling allows me to see a flop with a hand that has potential to make flushes or straights while keeping the pot manageable.

The flop of 9c-Qs-Kd is very strong for the pre-flop raiser's range, as it contains three Broadway cards. My hand, 8d4d, has missed this board entirely, offering only a marginal backdoor diamond flush draw. Given that I am out of position and have no showdown value or significant equity, checking is the most standard and prudent play. I intend to fold to any significant aggression from Minni.

I have T♠J♣ on K♦ Q♠ 9♣ — that’s a made Broadway straight (9-T-J-Q-K). It’s effectively the current nut (only an A-high straight or a rare straight‑flush can beat me). BB checked, so I should extract value and charge any backdoor draws. I’ll bet for value and protection: ~3/4 pot is large enough to get calls from Kx, Qx, pocket pairs and weaker broadways, but still small enough to be called. If I get raised strongly I’ll assume sets/two‑pair and re-evaluate (likely call a reasonable raise, fold to very large overbets depending on sizing).

The flop of 9-Q-K is extremely unfavorable for my hand, 8d4d, as it hits the pre-flop raiser's range very hard while I have no direct pair or draw other than a weak backdoor diamond flush. Calling a 300 bet into a 700 pot with 8-high and virtually no equity is a losing play. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

I am on the button with Q5o. In heads-up play, Q-high is a reasonable hand to open for a small raise from the button to put pressure on the big blind. Minni has been quite aggressive lately and currently holds a significant chip lead (25,550 to 14,300), so I need to be careful. However, folding here would be too tight for a heads-up game with only 12 hands remaining. Raising allows me to take control of the pot early or see a flop with position if called.

I have 7♦2♦ — the worst possible starting hand. Flash opened to 300 from the button and I’m out of position in the big blind. Calling 200 more into a ~600 pot (~2:1) gives poor odds for this hand’s equity and 7-2s plays terribly multi‑street OOP. A 3‑bet bluff is thin (no useful blockers or postflop playability) and not necessary given my healthy stack. Prefer to conserve chips and wait for a better spot — fold.

Heads-up on the button (also the small blind) with Q♥3♦ — a marginal hand but fine to open in position. Limping gives the BB a free flop and surrenders initiative; I want to take the lead and gain fold equity. Flash has shown mixed preflop aggression but will fold to pressure often, and a 2xBB open is standard here. I’ll make the minimum raise to 200 total to open the pot, keep the sizing small relative to our deep stacks, and plan to c-bet many flops or fold to a large 3-bet if necessary.

With King-Five offsuit in the big blind against a small open from Minni (raising to 200), I am getting excellent pot odds to see a flop. The call is only 100 more into a pot of 300. Given our stack sizes and the late stage of the match, defending the big blind with a King is a standard and profitable play, especially against a button open.

On a flop of A-5-2, I have middle pair with a King kicker. While the Ace is a scary card, Minni's button raising range is very wide, and I have significant showdown value. I will check to the aggressor to see how she proceeds. If she bets a standard amount, I plan to call at least one street to see if the turn improves my hand or if she checks back on later streets.

Heads-up I was the preflop aggressor and Flash checked the Ace-2-5 rainbow flop. Q3o is a miss, but in position I should c-bet frequently to take advantage of a wide BB calling range and win the pot now. I’ll make a half-pot continuation bet (200 into the 400 pot) — big enough to get folds from weak hands and non-Ax pairs, but small enough to keep the pot manageable if called. If Flash raises large I’ll give up (little equity vs Ax/two-pair); if called I’ll re-evaluate on the turn with positional advantage.

I have middle pair (5s) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. Minni's button opening range is very wide, and her 200 bet into a 400 pot is a standard continuation bet. I have sufficient showdown value to call at least one street here to see how the turn develops. Raising would likely only get action from hands that beat me (like an Ace), so calling is the most efficient way to keep her bluffs in and minimize losses if I am behind.

I currently hold middle pair (fives) with a King kicker on an Ace-high board. The turn card is a Four, which introduces some straight possibilities (any Three now makes a straight), though it is unlikely to have improved Minni's range significantly unless she is playing 3-4 or 6-3. Given that I am out of position and my hand has moderate showdown value as a bluff-catcher, checking is the most prudent play. This allows me to see if Minni continues her aggression or checks back to realize her own equity, keeping the pot manageable for my marginal hand.

I was the preflop aggressor and c‑bet the dry A‑5‑2 flop; Flash check‑called, which heavily weights his range toward Ax (top pair), small made pairs (5s, 2s) or defensively calling with medium pairs/overcards. The turn 4♦ is a blank for my Q3 — it doesn’t improve me and it actually helps a lot of hands in his calling range (A‑x, 55/22, or even hands that can call flop and call turn). I only have ~6 outs (~12–13%) to pair up on the river, so I have very little real equity if he’s on an Ace or a pocket pair. Betting here to try to fold out Ax is unlikely to work often enough to be +EV, and a bet risks bloating the pot when I’m behind and could induce a raise. With position and a marginal hand, the correct line is pot control: check the turn, see how he behaves on the river, and only invest more if I actually pair or get a cheap river look. If he bets a small value on the river I can call; if he shows real aggression on the turn/river I’ll fold.

I have middle pair (fives) with a King kicker. On the river, the board is Ace-high and the Nine is a relatively safe card. Minni checked the turn, which often indicates she doesn't have an Ace or a straight (any Three). Since I have significant showdown value against her range of missed draws, small pocket pairs, or a Deuce, checking is the most logical action to realize my equity. Betting would likely only get called by hands that beat me (Aces or the newly rivered Nine), and checking allows me to win the pot if she checks behind or potentially catch a bluff if she decides to bet.

I check. I missed the entire board with Q3 and only have weak queen-high. After my flop c-bet he flat-called, which usually shows Ax, small pairs (5s/2s) or a defensive call/float. The turn 4♢ was a blank for me and I intentionally checked to control the pot; Flash checked back. The river 9♣ also doesn’t improve me and checking again preserves pot control — betting would be a thin bluff against a range that contains many hands that beat me and some that will simply call. With the pot at 800 and deep stacks, the +EV line is to check to showdown rather than bluff or inflate the pot when I have almost no equity vs his likely holdings.

With J3o in the Small Blind (Button) and only 10 hands remaining, I am looking for higher-equity opportunities to close the gap between my stack and Minni's. J3o is a very weak hand that does not play well post-flop, even with the benefit of position. I will fold and wait for a better spot.

Heads-up on the button (I’m SB) with J♦6♦: this hand has decent heads-up playability and I want to take the initiative rather than limp. The pot is 150 and calling only 50 is passive — I’ll open-raise to the minimum (to 200) to get fold equity, isolate the BB and play the postflop streets in position. If Flash 3-bets big I can fold a dominated J6s; if he calls I plan to c-bet many missed flops and continue aggressively with my equity (pairs, J, flush/straight draws). Raising keeps the pot small relative to stacks while maximizing pressure and EV here.

Holding Q-10o in the Big Blind during heads-up play is a strong position. I am getting 3:1 odds on a call against Minni's 2x open. Given my stack size and the strength of the hand, I will call the raise to see a flop.

On a paired board of J-J-2, the standard play is to check to the pre-flop aggressor. I have Queen-high with a backdoor straight draw and a backdoor spade draw. I want to see how Minni acts on this board before committing any more chips. If Minni checks back, I might have the best hand or can take it away later; if Minni bets, I can evaluate based on the bet size.

I’ve flopped trips (Jd with Jh Js on board) and I’m in position — this is almost the nuts on this flop (only a full house/quad beats me). Flash checked, so this is a straightforward value spot. I want a size that gets called by worse hands (A/K/Q, pocket pairs, some one-pair hands, maybe a lone J that ties) while not overcommitting the stack and leaving room to extract more on later streets. Betting ~3/4 pot balances extraction and fold equity. Plan: bet for value now (if called I’ll continue barreling blank turns; if raised I’ll strongly consider calling/shoving depending on size since trips is very strong), if folded take the pot.

On a paired board of Jack-Jack-Deuce, Minni has led with a 300-chip bet into a 400-chip pot. While I have Queen-high and a backdoor straight draw, a 75% pot-sized bet on such a dry board is usually quite strong or a very committed bluff. Given that I have no made hand and only marginal backdoor equity, calling is likely to lead to difficult decisions on the turn and river if I don't improve. Since I am looking to protect my stack as we approach the end of the tournament (hand 142 of 150), I will fold and wait for a better spot to commit chips.

I am on the button with 7c 3c. While suited, this is one of the weakest hands in a heads-up range. Minni has been playing aggressively and frequently raising from the big blind when I limp or call. With only 8 hands remaining in the tournament and a significant stack deficit, I need to be more selective with the hands I play to avoid bleeding chips. I will fold here and look for a more advantageous starting hand to challenge Minni's lead.

Heads-up on the button with T7o — a hand I’ll open-raise rather than limp. Calling (completing) is cheap but passive and lets the BB see flops cheaply; raising takes the initiative, gains fold equity, and builds a pot when I hit a Ten while keeping position postflop. With a deep 26.2k stack vs Flash’s 13.65k, the minimum heads-up open to 200 total is small relative to stacks and is the standard play. I’ll raise to 200 (total) to pressure the big blind and play the rest of the hand in position.

I have Jc 6c in the big blind. Minni has raised to 200 from the small blind/button, requiring me to call 100 more into a pot of 300. This is a very favorable price (3-to-1) to see a flop with a suited hand. Given my stack size and the stage of the game, calling is standard to see if I can connect with the flop.

I have top pair (Jacks) on a board of 3c-Js-9d. My kicker is relatively weak (6), but in a heads-up situation against a small blind open, top pair is a very strong hand. I will check to the aggressor to see how they play the flop. If Minni bets, I plan to continue with the hand.

I raised preflop and have initiative heads-up. With T♣7♥ on 3♣ J♠ 9♦ I have a gutshot to an 8 (4 clean outs) plus three tens to make a pair — roughly ~25–28% to improve by the river — and a backdoor club. Flash checked, so a standard continuation semi-bluff is correct: it applies pressure to unpaired hands, gets value from worse hands that call, and builds the pot when I hit. I’ll bet a half‑pot-sized c-bet (200 into 400) to balance fold equity and pot control; if he raises I’ll re-evaluate (fold or call depending on sizing and reads).

I have top pair with an additional backdoor club draw. In a heads-up situation, top pair is a premium holding. Minni's half-pot bet is standard. While my kicker is weak, I should not be folding top pair here. I will call the bet to keep her range wide and see how the turn develops, as a raise might only get called by better hands while forcing out her bluffs.

I have top pair with a weak kicker on a board of J-9-3-5. In a heads-up pot, top pair is a very strong hand, but it is not strong enough to start building a massive pot by leading into the pre-flop aggressor. Checking is the standard play here to see how Minni proceeds. If she bets, I will call to reach the river, and if she checks, I can consider a value bet or a check on the river depending on the card.

I continue my aggression. On the turn (3♣ J♠ 9♦ 5♠) Flash checked after calling my flop c-bet — that shows weakness or a marginal holding. I have T♣7♥ with 7 clean outs to improve (3 tens + 4 eights) ≈ 15% on the river, which isn’t enough to just call for equity. Betting leverages fold equity and extracts value from worse pairs (9x, 3x, some Jx floats) while charging any thin call-downs. I’ll bet a size large enough to force folds from overcards/marginal hands but still get called by worse — 500 into the 800 pot (~62.5%). If Flash check‑raises big I’ll strongly consider folding; if called I’ll re-evaluate on the river.

I have top pair with a weak kicker in a heads-up pot. Minni is betting 500 into 800 on the turn after a standard pre-flop and flop sequence. Folding top pair here would be too tight given the wide ranges in heads-up play and the favorable pot odds. While she could have a better Jack or a hand like J9, she could also be semi-bluffing with straight draws (like QT or T8) or betting a middle pair for protection. My hand is a clear 'bluff catcher' at worst and a value hand at best; however, it is not strong enough to raise, as a raise would likely only be called by hands that beat me. I will call to see the river and re-evaluate.

On the river, an Ace appears, which is an overcard to my pair of Jacks. This card is significantly better for the pre-flop aggressor's (Minni) range, as she likely raises many hands containing an Ace. My hand, Jack-Six, has now been demoted to second pair with a very weak kicker. I have been calling down to this point, and leading out now would likely only result in being called by better hands (like an Ace or a superior Jack) while folding out hands I currently beat. I will check to see if Minni checks behind for a cheap showdown or to decide whether to call a potential final bet based on the sizing.

I raised preflop and barreled flop (200 into 400) and turn (500 into ~800). Flash called both streets, which narrows his range to mostly made hands (Jx, 9x, small pairs like 3s/5s) and some spade draws. The river A♠ both (a) gives him additional made hands (Ax) and (b) completes a possible spade flush — it materially improves the hands that were already willing to call my flop/turn bets. Pot is 1,800 and Flash has ~12.85k behind, so a large bluff would need very high fold equity to be +EV; given his sticky calling line I don’t trust his fold frequency to be that high. Betting small will get called by many marginal hands; betting large risks a big call/raise from any made hand. Checking is the least-bad option: I lose at showdown only when he has a pair/flush (likely), but I avoid turning a marginal bluff into a big negative EV confrontation. If he shows aggression I can fold and preserve the bulk of my stack.
I am on the button with King-Six offsuit. This is a standard opening hand in heads-up play, especially with 146 big blinds. I will raise to 300 to take control of the pot and apply pressure on Minni.

Heads-up vs the button who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with 7♠9♥ — a marginal but defendable hand heads-up. Calling requires 200 more (bringing my total committed to 300). The current pot is 400, so calling 200 gives me a pot of ~600 and I need roughly 33% equity to break even. Against a wide button opening range in heads-up I have reasonable multi-street equity and can realize that equity on many flops. I don’t want to 3-bet a marginal hand out of position (min-raise to 500 would commit too much with poor equity and open me to postflop pressure), nor do I want to fold cheaply when pot odds and opponent tendencies justify a defend. Plan: call to see the flop, continue when I have pair/draws or a favorable texture, and fold to heavy turn/river pressure when I miss.

I have two pair (9s and 7s) on 3c-9s-7c — a very strong hand heads-up against a button opener’s wide range. The board has two clubs and some backdoor/runner straight possibilities, so I want to charge draws and extract value from single-pair hands (9x, 7x, overcards that will call) while denying free cards. Pot is 600; a ~2/3 pot value bet (~400) should keep worse hands and draws paying to see the turn without overbetting and inviting huge raises. Plan: bet 400 now; if Villain raises big I’ll re-evaluate (sets or strong draws likely), if called I’ll continue extracting or protect on the turn.

I raised pre-flop with King-Six offsuit, a standard opening. Minni called from the big blind. The flop comes 3-9-7 with two clubs. Minni leads out with a 'donk bet' of 400 into a 600 pot. My hand has completely missed this board, and I have no draws or significant backdoor potential. While I have a King as an overcard, the board is quite coordinated (straight and flush draws possible), making a float or a raise here risky. Given the tournament stage and my stack size relative to Minni's, there is no need to fight for this pot with air. I will fold and wait for a better opportunity in the remaining 5 hands.

Heads-up on the button with 8s6d is marginal, but in heads-up play I should be opening a wide range to take the initiative and leverage position postflop. The pot is 150 and it only costs me 50 to complete, with the minimum legal raise being to 200. I prefer a standard 3xBB-ish open here to gain more fold equity and define the hand — raise to 300 total. If Flash three-bets I can fold or proceed depending on sizing and reads; if he calls I have position to play postflop and can realize equity or take small pots away. Calling 50 is too passive and misses the chance to apply pressure to the shorter opponent.

With K3s in the Big Blind against a button raise from Minni, I have a clear call. My hand has decent high card value and flush potential, and I am getting 3:1 odds (calling 200 to win a total pot of 600). Minni has been quite active, so her opening range is wide. I will defend and see a flop.

I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw on a relatively dry T-8-2 rainbow board. As the Big Blind, I should check to the pre-flop aggressor to see how she reacts. Leading out here doesn't accomplish much against her wide button opening range. I will check and evaluate based on her bet sizing.

Heads-up in position I have middle pair (8s) on a fairly dry T♦ 2♠ 8♣ board. Flash checked, so I can use my initiative — I raised preflop and my range is wide, and this hand still has real value against a lot of the big blind’s calling range (worse pairs like 2x, small pocket pairs, some draws/overcards that will call). There are no immediate flush draws and only loose straight possibilities, so a half-pot c-bet both extracts value and denies equity to weaker hands. I’ll size to ~50% pot to get called by worse and keep pot control; if I face a big raise I’ll give up (likely behind to tens/overpairs/sets). Plan: bet 300 into 600, evaluate on the turn if called.

On a flop of Td 2s 8c, I have King-high with a backdoor flush draw. Minni has been consistently aggressive with her continuation bets. While I am getting 4:1 odds to call (300 into 900), my hand is very weak with almost no equity against her range. I have no straight draws and only a flimsy backdoor club draw. Calling here likely leads to me folding on the turn to any further aggression unless I hit a King, and even then, my kicker is extremely weak. Given we are near the end of the tournament (Hand 146 of 150), I should preserve my stack for a better spot to make a move rather than floating with a marginal King-high.

T2o is one of the weakest possible starting hands in Texas Hold'em. With only four hands remaining in the tournament and a substantial stack of 140 big blinds, there is no reason to commit chips with such a poor hand, even from the button. I will fold and look for a better opportunity to close the gap in the final three hands.

Heads-up on the button (small blind) with Q♦5♥ — a marginal but acceptable hand to open in heads-up. Against a single big blind I want to take the initiative, gain fold equity, and play postflop in position rather than calling and being OOP. Flash has shown an active/aggressive profile, so opening wide is profitable; I’ll min-raise to a standard 2x BB (200). Plan: fold to a big, non-committal 3-bet, but if called use positional advantage to C-bet favorable flops and apply pressure on many runouts.

With 10-8 offsuit in the Big Blind, I am facing a min-raise from Minni on the button. I am getting 3:1 pot odds (100 to call into a 300 pot), which makes this a mandatory defend in heads-up play. T8o has decent playability and realization of equity against a wide button opening range. Given the tournament is ending in three hands and I am trailing in chips, I need to look for spots to accumulate, but calling is the most standard and risk-averse way to proceed to a flop.

On a paired board of 4h-Kd-Kc, the range advantage lies heavily with the pre-flop raiser, Minni, who is more likely to hold a King or high pocket pairs. With 10-high and no real draw, I have very little equity here. My strategy should be to check and evaluate Minni's action. If Minni checks back, I can see a free turn; if Minni bets, I will likely have to fold unless the sizing is extremely small, as I cannot afford to lose chips on a board that doesn't hit my range with only three hands remaining in the tournament.

Heads-up on the button with Q♦5♥ on a K♦ K♣ 4♥ flop. I have no pair and very weak showdown value, but I have position and opened preflop wide — so my raising range contains a lot of missed hands. Flash checked, so a small continuation bet can often take this pot down and exploits his tendency to defend wide from the big blind. I want to keep the pot small with this marginal holding while retaining fold equity, so I’ll fire a small c-bet (~1/3 pot). If he raises me aggressively I’ll give up — fold to a large check-raise — but if he calls I can continue cautiously and re-evaluate on the turn (check back most medium turns, bluff when favorable).

On a board of 4h-Kd-Kc, Minni has a significant range advantage as the pre-flop raiser. My 10-high has very little equity and no straight or flush draws. While the bet size of 150 is small relative to the pot, calling with 10-high here usually leads to more difficult decisions on the turn and river. With only three hands left in the tournament and being significantly behind in chips, I need to preserve my stack for a more opportunistic moment rather than calling with air on a board that favors the opponent's range.

In a heads-up situation on the Button (Small Blind), a hand like Ten-Seven offsuit is well within the standard opening range. With only two hands remaining in the tournament and a significant stack deficit compared to Minni, I need to maintain aggression and look for opportunities to pick up blinds or win pots post-flop. A standard 3x raise to 300 is appropriate here to put pressure on the Big Blind and define the hand range.

Heads-up vs the button (Flash) who opened to 300. I’m in the big blind with Jc6h — a marginal but playable hand heads-up. The pot is 400 and it’s 200 to me to call; calling 200 to win ~600 gives ~25% pot odds. Versus a wide button open, J6o has roughly that equity (or better), so a call is +EV. I’m also the chip leader (26,100 vs his 13,500) — no need to gamble or overcommit with a marginal holding. A small 3‑bet to 500 would be weak and a larger 3‑bet/shove is too risky here. Plan: call to see a flop; if I hit (pair, good draw) I can apply pressure, otherwise fold to heavy action. Therefore I will call (total commitment 300).

Pot is 600, I have Jc6h on 8c 5s 5h — no pair and no real draw (only a backdoor club). Flash was the preflop raiser so his range contains many hands that already beat me (5x, 8x, any pocket pair, many overcards). I’m the chip leader and have no reason to bloat the pot with a marginal hand. I’ll check to control the pot, see a free turn, and fold to meaningful aggression on this street; only consider calling a very small probe-bet if the odds justify it or if I pick up actionable reads.

On a paired board of 8-5-5 after a pre-flop raise, a continuation bet is a standard and effective play. My 10-high currently has limited showdown value, but the board texture is unlikely to have connected strongly with Minni's Big Blind defending range. By betting 300 into the 600 pot (50% pot), I can take down the pot immediately if she has missed, which occurs frequently on this dry texture. With only two hands remaining in the tournament and a significant chip deficit, I must continue to play aggressively to accumulate chips.

Flash was the preflop raiser and is c-betting a small 300 into a 900 pot on a dry, paired board (8c 5s 5h). That sizing is consistent with a fairly wide continuation range that contains a lot of missed overcards in addition to some value pairs. Calling 300 requires ~25% equity (300/(900+300)) — Jc6h has roughly that equity vs a wide c-bet range plus a backdoor club and turn/river pair outs. I’m the chip leader and out of position, so I don’t want to bloat the pot or bluff-raise with a marginal hand that has poor blockers. Plan: call the 300 to see a turn, fold to significant turn aggression unless I improve (pair a J/6 or pick up a real club/further equity) or receive a clear read to continue.

Check. I have Jc6h on 8c-5s-5h-9c — I’ve missed (no pair, no meaningful draw). Flash is the preflop raiser and his flop c-bet called line contains a lot of value (5x, 8x, pairs) and some bluffs. I’m out of position and the pot is already 1,200; with the chip lead there’s no reason to bloat the pot with a marginal holding. Checking controls the pot, allows me to fold to a clear river shove or large bet, and gives the best chance to realize equity cheaply if I pair or see a cheap river. If Flash fires a small river probe I can call; I’ll fold to substantial aggression unless I improve or pick up a strong read.

On the turn, the nine of clubs adds a straight draw to my ten-seven (needing a six for a straight). After Minni checked for a second time, a second barrel is a strong play to put pressure on her flop-calling range, which likely consists of ace-high, small pocket pairs, or weak eights. By betting 800 into a 1200 pot, I am representing a strong hand like a trip five, a nine, or a high overpair. With only two hands left in the tournament and a substantial stack deficit, I must remain aggressive to force folds or build a pot I can potentially win if I improve on the river.

Pot is 2000 and Flash bets 800 on the turn (I must call 800). Calling requires ~800/(2000+800) ≈ 28.6% equity to break even. I hold Jc6h on 8c-5s-5h-9c: I have no pair and essentially no real draw (a river pair of J or 6 are my only realistic outs: 3 jacks + 3 sixes = 6 outs → ~6/46 ≈ 13% to hit the river). That’s well below the required equity. Flash opened preflop and has c-bet and barreled the turn with a large sizing — a lot of his continuing range is value (5x, 8x, pocket pairs, some 9x) and will call or raise my bluffs. A raise as a bluff here is thin and unnecessarily risky when I’m the chip leader (25600 vs 12400) with one hand left; I should preserve my stack and avoid bloat-pot lines with a marginal holding. Fold and wait for a better spot on the final hand.

Heads-up on the button I should be opening a very wide range. K♣4♦ is marginal but playable in position — raising is preferred to just calling the BB and giving up initiative. I’ll raise to the minimum standard open (200 total) to steal the blind and keep the pot small against a deep-stacked opponent. Postflop plan: continuation-bet on most flops that miss them, control the pot if I face resistance, and fold to a large 3-bet (K4o is often dominated). Total committed = 200 (I’ve already posted 50 SB, so I’ll add 150).

On the final hand of the tournament, I am in the Big Blind with 7s 8h. Minni has min-raised to 200 from the Button/Small Blind. My goal is to maximize my stack. 7-8 offsuit is a standard defending hand against a min-raise in heads-up play, providing decent equity and post-flop playability. Given the pot odds (3:1), calling is the most mathematically sound decision to see a flop and attempt to increase my stack one last time without overcommitting pre-flop.

I am in the Big Blind for the final hand of the tournament. The flop comes Ace-Ace-Five, which is a very dry board that favors the pre-flop raiser's range. Holding 7-high with no pair or real draw, I have no reason to lead out. I will check to see how Minni acts. My goal is to maximize my final stack, but bluffing into a paired board against the chip leader on the final hand is unnecessarily risky without any equity.

Heads‑up on the button I opened wide preflop with K♣4♦, so I keep applying pressure with a high frequency continuation bet. The flop As Ac 5h is dangerous (paired aces) and will hit a lot of calling hands, but Flash checked — that check is consistent with both non‑A holdings and some trap Aces. With no real showdown equity (only a weak backdoor possibility) I want to use position and initiative to fold out missed hands and keep the pot manageable when I’m behind. I’ll make a small c‑bet ~1/3 pot (150 into 400) to get value from Kx, broadways and small pairs, while avoiding bloating the pot vs Ax or sets. Plan: if he folds I take the pot; if he calls I’ll slow-play/control on the turn and fold to a large raise since I rarely have an Ace here.

Holding 8-high on an Ace-Ace-Five board provides no equity or real bluffing opportunities against a small continuation bet. Since this is the final hand of the tournament, the priority is preserving the remaining stack rather than chasing a marginal or losing pot. Folding is the most prudent way to finalize my stack size at 14,000.
